topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT758 - POLYMERS IN CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sandwich Panel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🏗️ MODULE 1 — SANDWICH PANEL: CORE DEFINITION & INTUITION

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sandwich Panel (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A **Sandwich Panel** is a *structural composite building element* manufactured by adhesively bonding two thin, high-strength external facings (skins) to a thick, low-density **polymer-based or mineral core**. The composite behaves as a single monolithic unit exhibiting a very high **flexural rigidity-to-weight ratio**, where the facings resist in-plane and bending stresses (tension/compression) while the core resists transverse shear forces and stabilizes the facings against buckling.

In KTU terminology, a sandwich panel is classified as a **load-bearing or non-load-bearing prefabricated polymer composite assembly**, widely recognized in modern construction for thermal insulation, rapid erection, and lightweight cladding applications.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "I-Beam Cake"

Imagine a chocolate layer cake:

| Cake Element | Sandwich Panel Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Top icing layer | **Upper Facing** (Steel / GFRP / FRP) | Resists compression |
| Cream filling | **Polymer Core** (PU / PIR / EPS / XPS) | Holds layers apart, resists shear |
| Bottom icing layer | **Lower Facing** (Steel / GFRP / FRP) | Resists tension |
| Sugar glue | **Adhesive bond** (Epoxy / Polyurethane) | Transfers shear between layers |

Just as the cream in the middle of a cake makes it harder to bend than a solid chocolate slab of the same weight, the lightweight core **separates** the two facings, dramatically increasing the **second moment of area (I)**. This is the *lever-arm principle* — moment of inertia scales with the **square of the distance** between the facings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Doubling the core thickness (while keeping facings thin) increases the flexural rigidity by roughly **4×**, with negligible weight gain. This is why a 100 mm thick sandwich panel can outperform a 200 mm solid wall in bending stiffness.

---

## 1.3 Constituent Components of a Sandwich Panel

A sandwich panel has **three primary components** plus a **bonding system**:

1. **Facings (Skins):** Thin, high-modulus sheets that bear tensile and compressive bending stresses.
2. **Core:** Thick, low-density material that resists shear and maintains the spacing between facings.
3. **Adhesive:** A polymer bonding layer (typically two-part polyurethane or epoxy) that transfers in-plane shear from the facings to the core.
4. **Edge Profile & Joint System:** Tongue-and-groove or cam-lock mechanical profiles for panel-to-panel sealing.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "list the components," students often forget the **adhesive layer** and the **joint profile**. Marks are reserved for completeness — always mention all four.

---

## 1.4 Common Facing & Core Materials Used in Construction

| Component | Common Materials | Typical Thickness |
|---|---|---|
| **Metal Facings** | Galvanized steel, Aluminum, Stainless steel | **0.4 mm – 0.7 mm** |
| **Polymer Facings** | GFRP (Glass Fibre Reinforced Plastic), FRP, ACP | **1.5 mm – 3.0 mm** |
| **Polymer Cores** | Polyurethane (PU), Polyisocyanurate (PIR), EPS, XPS | **40 mm – 200 mm** |
| **Mineral Cores** | Rockwool, Mineral wool, Glass wool | **50 mm – 150 mm** |
| **Phenolic Cores** | Phenolic foam (PF) | **40 mm – 100 mm** |

---

## 1.5 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU 2024 Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical KTU-Recognized Performance Ranges:**
> - **Thermal Conductivity (U-value):** 0.10 – 0.40 W/m²K (depending on core)
> - **Density:** 10 – 40 kg/m² (entire panel)
> - **Fire Rating:** Class B-s2, d0 to A2-s1, d0 (PIR > PU > EPS)
> - **Service Life:** **30 – 50 years** under standard weather exposure
> - **Span Capability:** 3 m – 12 m unsupported roof spans

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 MODULE 1 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Load-Transfer Mechanism — The "Why" Behind Sandwich Action

A sandwich panel works on a **composite action principle**. To understand *why* it is so efficient, we must analyse the flow of forces:

1. **Vertical load (q)** is applied on the upper facing.
2. The upper facing goes into **compression** and the lower facing goes into **tension** (Euler-Bernoulli bending analogy).
3. The **core material transfers shear** between the two facings, ensuring they bend together as one unit.
4. The **adhesive bond** must remain elastic — any debonding (delamination) causes catastrophic loss of strength.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a low-density core?** A core made of polymer foam is light but rigid *in shear*. The facings do the heavy lifting in tension/compression; the core just holds them apart. The further apart, the stiffer the panel.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Sandwich Panels (KTU 2024 Scheme)

Sandwich panels are classified along **three independent axes**:

### A. By Core Material

| Type | Core Material | Key Feature | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PU Panel** | Polyurethane foam | Best insulation (k ≈ 0.022 W/mK) | Cold storage, clean rooms |
| **PIR Panel** | Polyisocyanurate foam | Improved fire resistance | Industrial roofing |
| **EPS Panel** | Expanded Polystyrene | Lowest cost, recyclable | Residential walls |
| **XPS Panel** | Extruded Polystyrene | Moisture resistant | Basement, floor insulation |
| **Mineral Wool Panel** | Rockwool / Glasswool | A1 fire rating, acoustic | Fire-rated walls |
| **Phenolic Panel** | Phenolic foam | Very low smoke emission | High-rise façades |

### B. By Facing Material

- **Type S (Steel-faced):** Most common; used for industrial buildings.
- **Type A (Aluminum-faced):** Corrosion-resistant; coastal & architectural use.
- **Type F (FRP/GFRP-faced):** Polymer facing; non-conductive, chemical-resistant.
- **Type C (Cement-board-faced):** Heavy-duty; load-bearing walls.
- **Type W (Wood-faced):** Aesthetic; interior partitions.

### C. By Application / Function

- **Wall Panels** (vertical cladding)
- **Roof Panels** (horizontal / sloped)
- **Cold Room Panels** (insulated)
- **Clean Room Panels** (pharma, semiconductor)
- **Acoustic Panels** (sound-dampening cores)

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **This is the master reference table for all numerical problems in KTU ESE on Sandwich Panels.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Variables | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Panel Thickness | $d = 2t_f + t_c$ | $t_f$ = facing thickness, $t_c$ = core thickness | m |
| 2 | Flexural Rigidity (per unit width) | $D = \dfrac{E_f \, t_f \, d^2}{2} + \dfrac{E_f \, t_f^3}{6} + \dfrac{E_c \, t_c^3}{12}$ | $E_f$ = facing Young's modulus, $E_c$ = core Young's modulus | N·m²/m |
| 3 | Approx. Flexural Rigidity (when $t_f \ll t_c$) | $D \approx \dfrac{E_f \, t_f \, d^2}{2}$ | — | N·m²/m |
| 4 | Bending Stress in Facing | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{M \cdot (d/2)}{D}$ | $M$ = applied moment per unit width | N/m² |
| 5 | Shear Stress in Core | $\tau_c = \dfrac{V}{d}$ | $V$ = shear force per unit width | N/m² |
| 6 | Critical Buckling Load (Facing Wrinkling) | $\sigma_{cr} = 0.5 \cdot \sqrt[3]{E_f \, E_c \, G_c}$ | $G_c$ = core shear modulus | N/m² |
| 7 | Thermal Resistance (R-value) | $R = \dfrac{t}{k}$ | $t$ = thickness, $k$ = thermal conductivity | m²K/W |
| 8 | Total Thermal Resistance (Composite) | $R_{tot} = R_{si} + R_f + R_c + R_f + R_{so}$ | $R_{si}, R_{so}$ = surface resistances | m²K/W |
| 9 | Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient | $U = \dfrac{1}{R_{tot}}$ | — | W/m²K |
| 10 | Deflection (Simply Supported, UDL) | $\delta = \dfrac{5 w L^4}{384 D} + \dfrac{w L^2}{8 \, d \, G_c}$ | $w$ = load per unit area, $L$ = span | m |
| 11 | Self-Weight per Unit Area | $w_{sw} = 2 \rho_f t_f + \rho_c t_c$ | $\rho_f, \rho_c$ = densities | kg/m² |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When calculating flexural rigidity, students often forget the third term $\left(\dfrac{E_c \, t_c^3}{12}\right)$ — the core's *own* contribution to bending stiffness. While small (because $E_c$ is low), it is non-zero and may earn the remaining mark in long-answer questions.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Sandwich panels dominate **fast-track construction** in:
- **Pre-Engineered Buildings (PEB):** Warehouses, factories, logistics parks.
- **Modular Construction:** Portable cabins, site offices, COVID hospitals.
- **Cold Chain Infrastructure:** Cold rooms for vaccines, dairy, seafood (uses PU/PIR core for $-40°\text{C}$ operation).
- **Green Buildings:** GRIHA & IGBC-rated structures using EPS/recyclable cores.
- **Aerospace & Railways (advanced application):** Aerospace-grade honeycomb + GFRP facings.

# 📐 MODULE 1 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Flexural Rigidity of a Symmetric Sandwich Panel

### Given Setup
Consider a sandwich panel of unit width $b = 1$ m with:
- Two facings, each of thickness $t_f$, separated by a core of thickness $t_c$
- Facing material: Young's modulus $E_f$
- Core material: Young's modulus $E_c$
- Total depth: $d = 2t_f + t_c$

### Step 1 — Apply Parallel Axis Theorem

For a thin rectangular facing (width $b$, thickness $t_f$), located at distance $\dfrac{d}{2} - \dfrac{t_f}{2}$ from the neutral axis. For *thin facings* ($t_f \ll d$), this distance is approximately $\dfrac{d}{2}$.

The moment of inertia of one facing about the centroidal axis of the panel is:

$$I_f = I_{\text{self}} + A_f \cdot \left(\dfrac{d}{2}\right)^2$$

Where:
- $I_{\text{self}} = \dfrac{b \cdot t_f^3}{12}$ (own moment of inertia of the facing)
- $A_f = b \cdot t_f$ (area of the facing)

For **unit width** ($b = 1$):

$$I_f = \dfrac{t_f^3}{12} + t_f \cdot \left(\dfrac{d}{2}\right)^2$$

### Step 2 — Total Moment of Inertia for Both Facings

$$I_{\text{facings}} = 2 \cdot \left[\dfrac{t_f^3}{12} + t_f \cdot \left(\dfrac{d}{2}\right)^2\right]$$

$$\boxed{I_{\text{facings}} = \dfrac{t_f^3}{6} + \dfrac{t_f \cdot d^2}{2}}$$

### Step 3 — Moment of Inertia of the Core

The core is a rectangle of width $b = 1$ and height $t_c$, located symmetrically about the neutral axis:

$$I_{\text{core}} = \dfrac{b \cdot t_c^3}{12} = \dfrac{t_c^3}{12}$$

### Step 4 — Combined Moment of Inertia

$$I_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{t_f^3}{6} + \dfrac{t_f \cdot d^2}{2} + \dfrac{t_c^3}{12}$$

### Step 5 — Multiply by Respective Moduli to get Flexural Rigidity

The flexural rigidity $D$ is not simply $E \cdot I$ for a composite — each component contributes its own $E \cdot I$:

$$D = E_f \cdot I_{\text{facings}} + E_c \cdot I_{\text{core}}$$

$$\boxed{D = \dfrac{E_f \, t_f \, d^2}{2} + \dfrac{E_f \, t_f^3}{6} + \dfrac{E_c \, t_c^3}{12}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **governing equation** for sandwich panel bending analysis and is frequently asked in KTU Part B questions.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Numerical Computation of D

### Problem Statement
A sandwich panel has facings of **0.5 mm galvanized steel** ($E_f = 210$ GPa) and a core of **50 mm PU foam** ($E_c = 5$ MPa, $\rho_c = 35$ kg/m³). Calculate the flexural rigidity per unit width.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Convert units to SI (m):**
- $t_f = 0.5 \text{ mm} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $t_c = 50 \text{ mm} = 50 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $E_f = 210 \times 10^{9}$ Pa
- $E_c = 5 \times 10^{6}$ Pa

**Step 2 — Compute total depth:**
$$d = 2t_f + t_c = 2(0.5 \times 10^{-3}) + 50 \times 10^{-3} = 51 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 0.051 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Term 1: Facing contribution (dominant)**

$$\dfrac{E_f \, t_f \, d^2}{2} = \dfrac{(210 \times 10^9)(0.5 \times 10^{-3})(0.051)^2}{2}$$

$$= \dfrac{(210 \times 10^9)(0.5 \times 10^{-3})(2.601 \times 10^{-3})}{2}$$

$$= \dfrac{(210 \times 10^9)(1.3005 \times 10^{-6})}{2} = \dfrac{2.731 \times 10^{5}}{2}$$

$$= 1.366 \times 10^{5} \text{ N·m}^2/\text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Term 2: Facing self-bending (negligible)**

$$\dfrac{E_f \, t_f^3}{6} = \dfrac{(210 \times 10^9)(0.5 \times 10^{-3})^3}{6} = \dfrac{(210 \times 10^9)(1.25 \times 10^{-10})}{6}$$

$$= \dfrac{0.02625}{6} = 4.375 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N·m}^2/\text{m}$$

This is **negligible** (~0.003% of Term 1).

**Step 5 — Term 3: Core contribution**

$$\dfrac{E_c \, t_c^3}{12} = \dfrac{(5 \times 10^6)(50 \times 10^{-3})^3}{12} = \dfrac{(5 \times 10^6)(1.25 \times 10^{-4})}{12}$$

$$= \dfrac{625}{12} = 52.08 \text{ N·m}^2/\text{m}$$

**Step 6 — Sum all three terms**

$$D = 1.366 \times 10^5 + 4.375 \times 10^{-3} + 52.08$$

$$\boxed{D \approx 1.367 \times 10^{5} \text{ N·m}^2/\text{m} = 136.7 \text{ kN·m}^2/\text{m}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Observation:** Term 1 contributes **99.96%** of total rigidity. This validates the *approximation* $D \approx \dfrac{E_f \, t_f \, d^2}{2}$, which is acceptable for KTU calculations when $t_f \ll t_c$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Thermal Transmittance (U-value)

### Step 1 — Resistances in Series
Heat flows through five layers in series:

1. Inner surface film: $R_{si} = 0.13$ m²K/W (standard, still air)
2. Upper facing: $R_{f1} = t_f / k_f$
3. Core: $R_c = t_c / k_c$
4. Lower facing: $R_{f2} = t_f / k_f$
5. Outer surface film: $R_{so} = 0.04$ m²K/W (standard, exposed to wind)

$$R_{tot} = R_{si} + \dfrac{t_f}{k_f} + \dfrac{t_c}{k_c} + \dfrac{t_f}{k_f} + R_{so}$$

### Step 2 — Simplify (symmetric facings)

$$R_{tot} = 0.17 + \dfrac{2t_f}{k_f} + \dfrac{t_c}{k_c}$$

### Step 3 — Compute U-value

$$\boxed{U = \dfrac{1}{R_{tot}} = \dfrac{1}{0.17 + \dfrac{2t_f}{k_f} + \dfrac{t_c}{k_c}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Metal facings have $k_f \approx 50$ W/mK, so $\dfrac{2t_f}{k_f}$ is negligibly small ($\approx 2 \times 10^{-5}$ m²K/W). The core dominates thermal resistance — choose a low-$k$ core (PU: 0.022 W/mK) for high insulation.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: U-value of PU Core Panel

**Given:** $t_f = 0.5$ mm steel, $t_c = 50$ mm PU foam, $k_f = 50$ W/mK, $k_c = 0.022$ W/mK

**Step 1 — Compute each resistance:**

$$R_{f} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{50} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

$$R_c = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{0.022} = 2.273 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

**Step 2 — Total resistance:**

$$R_{tot} = 0.17 + 2 \times 10^{-5} + 2.273 \approx 2.443 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

**Step 3 — U-value:**

$$U = \dfrac{1}{2.443} \approx 0.409 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This satisfies **ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code) of India** residential wall requirement of $U \leq 0.4$ W/m²K (composite climate).

# 📝 MODULE 1 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a sandwich panel. List any four core materials used in construction sandwich panels.**

**Model Answer:**

A sandwich panel is a **prefabricated composite building element** consisting of two high-strength facings (skins) bonded to a thick, lightweight core. The facings carry bending stresses (tension and compression), while the core resists transverse shear and provides thermal/acoustic insulation.

**Four core materials:**
1. Polyurethane (PU) foam
2. Polyisocyanurate (PIR) foam
3. Expanded Polystyrene (EPS)
4. Mineral Wool (Rockwool / Glasswool)

*(Acceptable alternatives: Extruded Polystyrene — XPS, Phenolic foam.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State any three advantages of using sandwich panels in modern construction.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **High strength-to-weight ratio:** Sandwich panels offer excellent flexural rigidity at low self-weight, reducing dead load on the structural frame.
2. **Superior thermal insulation:** With U-values as low as 0.10 W/m²K, they reduce energy consumption and meet ECBC standards.
3. **Rapid installation:** Prefabricated panels enable fast-track construction, reducing site labour and project timelines by up to **50%**.
4. *(Bonus point — ½ mark if added)* Aesthetic versatility and acoustic performance.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **structural action** of a sandwich panel under transverse loading. Identify the role of facings, core, and adhesive. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sandwich panel has facings of thickness **0.6 mm** with $E_f = 200$ GPa and a core of thickness **60 mm** PU foam with $E_c = 4$ MPa. Calculate the **flexural rigidity per unit width**. Take panel width = 1 m. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Structural Action Explanation:**

Under a transverse load (e.g., wind on a wall, snow on a roof):

- **Upper facing** experiences **compressive bending stress** ($\sigma_c$).
- **Lower facing** experiences **tensile bending stress** ($\sigma_t$).
- The **core** transfers **transverse shear stress** ($\tau$) between the two facings, maintaining composite action.
- The **adhesive** (a thin polymer layer, typically PU or epoxy) ensures strain compatibility at the facing-core interface.

```
   COMPRESSIVE STRESS (σc) ↓
   ┌──────────────────────────┐  ← Upper Facing
   ├──────────────────────────┤  ← Adhesive
   │▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓│  ← Core (shear τ)
   ├──────────────────────────┤  ← Adhesive
   └──────────────────────────┘  ← Lower Facing
   TENSILE STRESS (σt) ↑
```

If the adhesive fails, facings act independently and rigidity drops by **> 90%** — this is called **delamination failure**.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $t_f = 0.6$ mm $= 0.6 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $t_c = 60$ mm $= 60 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $E_f = 200 \times 10^{9}$ Pa
- $E_c = 4 \times 10^{6}$ Pa
- $b = 1$ m (unit width)

**Step 1 — Total depth:**
$$d = 2t_f + t_c = 2(0.6 \times 10^{-3}) + 60 \times 10^{-3} = 61.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Term 1 (Dominant):**
$$T_1 = \dfrac{E_f \, t_f \, d^2}{2} = \dfrac{(200 \times 10^9)(0.6 \times 10^{-3})(61.2 \times 10^{-3})^2}{2}$$

$$= \dfrac{(200 \times 10^9)(0.6 \times 10^{-3})(3.7454 \times 10^{-3})}{2}$$

$$= \dfrac{(200 \times 10^9)(2.247 \times 10^{-6})}{2} = \dfrac{4.495 \times 10^{5}}{2} = 2.247 \times 10^{5} \text{ N·m}^2/\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Term 2 (Negligible):**
$$T_2 = \dfrac{E_f \, t_f^3}{6} = \dfrac{(200 \times 10^9)(0.6 \times 10^{-3})^3}{6} = \dfrac{(200 \times 10^9)(2.16 \times 10^{-10})}{6}$$

$$= \dfrac{0.0432}{6} = 7.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N·m}^2/\text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Term 3 (Core):**
$$T_3 = \dfrac{E_c \, t_c^3}{12} = \dfrac{(4 \times 10^6)(60 \times 10^{-3})^3}{12} = \dfrac{(4 \times 10^6)(2.16 \times 10^{-4})}{12}$$

$$= \dfrac{864}{12} = 72 \text{ N·m}^2/\text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Final Flexural Rigidity:**
$$D = T_1 + T_2 + T_3 = 2.247 \times 10^5 + 7.2 \times 10^{-3} + 72$$

$$\boxed{D \approx 2.247 \times 10^5 + 72 \approx 2.248 \times 10^5 \text{ N·m}^2/\text{m} = 224.8 \text{ kN·m}^2/\text{m}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Classify sandwich panels based on **core material** and **facing material**. Give at least two examples in each category. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 75 mm thick sandwich panel with 0.5 mm steel facings has a PU foam core of thermal conductivity $k_c = 0.024$ W/mK. Steel $k_f = 45$ W/mK. Calculate the **U-value** of the panel and comment on its suitability for an energy-efficient building envelope. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Classification:**

**By Core Material:**
| Type | Core | Example Use |
|---|---|---|
| **PU Foam Panel** | Polyurethane | Cold storage |
| **PIR Foam Panel** | Polyisocyanurate | Industrial roofing |
| **EPS Panel** | Expanded Polystyrene | Residential walls |
| **Mineral Wool Panel** | Rockwool | Fire-rated walls |

**By Facing Material:**
| Type | Facing | Example Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Steel-faced (S)** | Galvanized steel | Warehouses |
| **Aluminum-faced (A)** | Aluminum sheet | Coastal structures |
| **FRP/GFRP-faced** | Fibre-reinforced polymer | Chemical plants |
| **Cement-board-faced** | Fibre cement board | Load-bearing walls |

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $t_f = 0.5$ mm $= 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $t_c = 75$ mm $= 75 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $k_f = 45$ W/mK, $k_c = 0.024$ W/mK
- $R_{si} = 0.13$ m²K/W, $R_{so} = 0.04$ m²K/W

**Step 1 — Facing resistance:**
$$R_f = \dfrac{2t_f}{k_f} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{45} = 2.22 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

**Step 2 — Core resistance:**
$$R_c = \dfrac{t_c}{k_c} = \dfrac{75 \times 10^{-3}}{0.024} = 3.125 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

**Step 3 — Total resistance:**
$$R_{tot} = 0.13 + 2.22 \times 10^{-5} + 3.125 + 0.04 = 3.295 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

**Step 4 — U-value:**
$$\boxed{U = \dfrac{1}{R_{tot}} = \dfrac{1}{3.295} \approx 0.303 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}}$$

**Step 5 — Comment:**

A U-value of **0.303 W/m²K** is **well within the ECBC limit of 0.4 W/m²K** for composite climate residential walls. This panel is **highly suitable** for energy-efficient building envelopes and can contribute to **GRIHA/IGBC green rating points**. However, for extreme cold climates (Himalayan region), a thicker core (≥ 100 mm) or PIR foam ($k_c = 0.020$ W/mK) is recommended.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Always convert mm → m before plugging into formulas. Marks lost: 1–2 per question.
> 2. **Ignoring Term 3** ($E_c t_c^3 / 12$): Examiners *expect* you to write all three terms even if Term 2 is negligible.
> 3. **Confusing "flexural rigidity" with "bending stiffness":** $D$ is per unit width (N·m²/m). Students sometimes write $EI$ without dividing by width.
> 4. **Wrong R-value for surface films:** Standard Indian codes use $R_{si} = 0.13$ and $R_{so} = 0.04$. Do not interchange.
> 5. **Forgetting to draw the diagram** in 7-mark sub-parts — minimum 1 mark is reserved for the sketch.
> 6. **Mixing up PU and PIR:** PU has poor fire performance; PIR is the fire-rated variant. Examiners test this distinction.

---

## 5.3 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and revise 5 minutes before the exam.**

✅ **Definition:** Sandwich panel = 2 facings + 1 core + adhesive, behaving as one composite.

✅ **Three components:** Facings (load-bearing), Core (shear + insulation), Adhesive (bonding).

✅ **Core materials ranked by insulation value (best → worst):** Phenolic (0.018) > PIR (0.020) > PU (0.022) > XPS (0.029) > EPS (0.035) > Mineral Wool (0.040) W/mK.

✅ **Core materials ranked by fire performance (best → worst):** Mineral Wool (A1) > PIR (B-s1) > Phenolic (B-s1) > PU (C-s3) > EPS (E).

✅ **Master Formula (Flexural Rigidity):**
$$D = \dfrac{E_f \, t_f \, d^2}{2} + \dfrac{E_f \, t_f^3}{6} + \dfrac{E_c \, t_c^3}{12}$$

✅ **U-value Formula:**
$$U = \dfrac{1}{0.17 + \dfrac{2t_f}{k_f} + \dfrac{t_c}{k_c}}$$

✅ **Key failure modes (5):** Facing yielding, Core shear, Wrinkling, Debonding, Indentation.

✅ **Applications to remember:** PEB sheds, cold storage, modular housing, clean rooms, high-rise façades.

✅ **Standard surface resistances:** $R_{si} = 0.13$, $R_{so} = 0.04$ m²K/W (always state these).

✅ **ECBC limit (walls):** $U \leq 0.4$ W/m²K (composite climate).

✅ **Quick trick for "approximate D":** When $t_f \ll t_c$, use $D \approx \dfrac{E_f t_f d^2}{2}$ — error < 0.1%.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Piping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT758 - POLYMERS IN CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Piping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Polymer Piping** refers to the integrated system of tubes, pipes, fittings, joints, valves, and supporting hardware manufactured predominantly from **thermoplastic** and **thermosetting polymeric materials**, engineered for the conveyance of fluids (water, sewage, gases, chemicals) and used extensively in modern construction infrastructure. In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus for *Polymers in Construction*, polymer piping is formally defined as the **engineered application of high-molecular-weight organic polymers** (with molecular weights typically ranging from **$10^4$ to $10^7$ g/mol**) as the primary structural material for fluid distribution networks in residential, commercial, industrial, and civil engineering projects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The piping module emphasizes *material-grade identification*, *pressure ratings*, *thermal behaviour*, *jointing techniques*, and *standards conformity* (IS, ASTM, ISO, DIN) — all of which are high-weightage areas in KTU End Semester Examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the **human circulatory system**. The heart (pump) pushes blood (fluid) through arteries (large polymer pipes) which branch into capillaries (small diameter service pipes) delivering oxygen (water/gas) to every organ (fixtures/appliances). Polymer pipes are the **"synthetic arteries"** of a building — they are lightweight, flexible, corrosion-resistant, and can be routed through tight spaces just as veins navigate through the body. Unlike **GI (Galvanized Iron)** or **CI (Cast Iron)** pipes which are rigid and heavy, polymer pipes behave more like **flexible rubber hoses** that can bend around corners, snap together, and resist chemical attack — making them the modern-day preferred choice over conventional metallic piping in most non-high-temperature, non-high-pressure construction applications.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standardized metrics govern polymer pipe design across all major codes:

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Design Service Life | **50 years** | years |
| Operating Temperature (PVC-U) | **0 to 45** | °C |
| Operating Temperature (CPVC) | **0 to 93** | °C |
| Operating Temperature (HDPE) | **-40 to 60** | °C |
| Standard Density of uPVC | **1.40 – 1.46** | g/cm³ |
| Standard Density of HDPE | **0.941 – 0.965** | g/cm³ |
| Standard Dimension Ratio (SDR) | **11, 13.5, 17, 21, 26, 33, 41** | dimensionless |
| Hydrostatic Design Stress (HDS) for PVC | **14 MPa (2000 psi)** | MPa |

> [!NOTE]
> The **50-year design life** is a universal benchmark set by the *PPI (Plastics Pipe Institute)* and adopted by **IS 4985**, **ASTM D1785**, and **ISO 4422**. All pressure derating factors in polymer pipe design are calibrated to this service life.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Polymer Pipes Used in Construction

Polymer pipes used in construction are broadly classified into the following categories based on polymer chemistry, structure, and application:

### 2.1.1 Thermoplastic Pipes (Reversible Softening on Heating)

**a) Unplasticized Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC-U / uPVC)**
- Rigid, unplasticized PVC pipes
- Used for cold water distribution, irrigation, drainage, sewerage
- Conforms to **IS 4985**, **ASTM D1785**, **ISO 4422**
- Working pressure up to **$\sim$1.0 MPa**

**b) Chlorinated Polyvinyl Chloride (CPVC)**
- PVC chlorinated to ~63-69% Cl content
- Superior heat resistance (up to 93°C)
- Used for hot and cold water plumbing
- Conforms to **IS 15778**, **ASTM F441**

**c) High-Density Polyethylene (HDPE)**
- High strength-to-density ratio
- Used for water mains, gas distribution, marine outfalls
- Conforms to **IS 4984**, **ISO 4427**, **ASTM D3035**
- Excellent flexibility and chemical resistance

**d) Polypropylene (PP-R / PPR)**
- Random copolymer polypropylene
- Used for hot/cold water plumbing, radiator connections
- Conforms to **DIN 8077/8078**, **ISO 15874**

**e) Cross-linked Polyethylene (PEX)**
- Chemically or physically cross-linked PE
- Used for underfloor heating, radiant cooling, hot water lines
- Conforms to **ASTM F876**, **ASTM F877**

**f) Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene (ABS)**
- Used for drain-waste-vent (DWV) systems
- Conforms to **ASTM D2661**, **IS 15767**

**g) Glass Reinforced Plastic (GRP / FRP)**
- Fiberglass-reinforced thermoset polyester/epoxy
- Used for large diameter trunk sewers, industrial effluent lines
- Conforms to **IS 14457**, **AWWA C950**

### 2.1.2 Thermosetting Pipes (Irreversibly Cured)

**a) Glass Reinforced Plastic (GRP) Pipes**
**b) Reinforced Plastic Mortar (RPM) Pipes**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why thermoplastics dominate construction piping:** Their weldability, recyclability, and ability to be remelted and reprocessed make them economically and environmentally superior to thermosets in most non-specialized applications.

## 2.2 Operational Logic Steps in Pipe Selection

The engineering selection of a polymer pipe for a specific construction application follows a structured decision matrix:

1. **Identify Fluid Conveyed** → Potable water / sewage / gas / chemical / fire-fighting
2. **Determine Operating Pressure** → Working pressure + surge allowance
3. **Determine Operating Temperature** → Continuous + occasional peak
4. **Establish Service Life** → Typically 50 years (default) or project-specific
5. **Compute Required Pressure Rating (PR)** using derating factors
6. **Select Standard Dimension Ratio (SDR)** from manufacturer catalogue
7. **Compute Wall Thickness** from SDR
8. **Verify against Standards** (IS/ASTM/ISO)
9. **Select Jointing Method** based on material and application
10. **Specify Fittings, Valves, and Supports**

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMULAS** — These are board-exam favourites and appear almost every KTU cycle in some form (numerical or derivation).

### 2.3.1 Standard Dimension Ratio (SDR)

$$\text{SDR} = \frac{D_o}{t}$$

Where:
- $D_o$ = outside diameter of pipe (mm)
- $t$ = minimum wall thickness (mm)

### 2.3.2 Dimension Ratio (DR) and Pipe Stiffness Relationship

$$\text{DR} = \frac{D_o}{t}$$

$$\text{Pipe Stiffness (PS)} = \frac{E}{12 \cdot (\text{DR} - 1)^3}$$

Where $E$ = Modulus of Elasticity of the pipe material (MPa)

### 2.3.3 Hoop (Circumferential) Stress — Thin Wall Theory

$$\sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot D_m}{2 \cdot t}$$

Where:
- $\sigma_h$ = hoop stress (MPa)
- $P$ = internal pressure (MPa)
- $D_m$ = mean diameter = $D_o - t$ (mm)
- $t$ = wall thickness (mm)

### 2.3.4 Pressure Rating Calculation

$$PR = \frac{2 \cdot \sigma \cdot t}{D_o - t}$$

Where $\sigma$ = allowable design stress (MPa)

### 2.3.5 Pressure Class (PC) with Derating Factors

$$PC = \frac{2 \cdot HDS \cdot f_T \cdot f_A}{SDR - 1}$$

Where:
- $HDS$ = Hydrostatic Design Stress (MPa)
- $f_T$ = Temperature derating factor (dimensionless, $\leq 1$)
- $f_A$ = Application / Chemical compatibility factor (dimensionless, $\leq 1$)

### 2.3.6 Hydraulic Design — Hazen-Williams Head Loss

$$h_f = \frac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot D^{4.87}}$$

Where:
- $h_f$ = head loss due to friction (m)
- $L$ = pipe length (m)
- $Q$ = flow rate (m³/s)
- $C$ = Hazen-Williams roughness coefficient
  - PVC: $C = 140$–$150$
  - HDPE: $C = 140$–$150$
  - GI: $C = 120$
  - CI: $C = 100$
- $D$ = internal diameter (m)

### 2.3.7 Thermal Expansion / Contraction

$$\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$$

Where:
- $\Delta L$ = change in length (mm)
- $\alpha$ = coefficient of linear thermal expansion (mm/mm/°C)
- $L$ = original length (mm)
- $\Delta T$ = temperature change (°C)

Typical $\alpha$ values:
- PVC: $\alpha = 5.4 \times 10^{-5}$ /°C
- HDPE: $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-5}$ /°C
- CPVC: $\alpha = 6.2 \times 10^{-5}$ /°C
- PP: $\alpha = 11 \times 10^{-5}$ /°C

### 2.3.8 Required Expansion Loop / Offset Length

$$L_{loop} = C \cdot \sqrt{D_o \cdot \Delta L}$$

Where $C$ is a constant depending on loop geometry (typical value $C \approx 30$ for L-type offset).

### 2.3.9 Surge Pressure (Water Hammer) Allowance

$$\Delta P = \frac{a \cdot \Delta V}{g}$$

Where:
- $\Delta P$ = surge pressure (m of water)
- $a$ = pressure wave celerity (m/s), typically 300–450 m/s for polymer pipes
- $\Delta V$ = velocity change (m/s)
- $g$ = 9.81 m/s²

### 2.3.10 Long-Term Hydrostatic Strength (LTHS)

$$\log \sigma = A + B \cdot \log t$$

This is the linear regression model used to extrapolate 50-year strength from short-term tests, where $\sigma$ is hoop stress and $t$ is time to failure.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| Residential Plumbing | Hot/cold water distribution (CPVC, PPR, PEX) |
| Municipal Water Supply | HDPE trunk mains, MDPE service connections |
| Sewerage & Drainage | PVC-U, HDPE structured-wall pipes |
| Gas Distribution | MDPE/HDPE yellow pipes (PNG networks) |
| Industrial Process Lines | PP, PVDF, ECTFE for aggressive chemicals |
| Fire-Fighting | CPVC sprinkler systems (light hazard) |
| Agricultural Irrigation | HDPE laterals, PVC-O rising mains |
| Marine Outfalls | HDPE ocean discharge pipelines |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hoop Stress in a Thin-Walled Cylindrical Pipe

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider a thin-walled cylindrical pipe of internal radius $r_i$, external radius $r_e$, length $L$, subjected to an internal gauge pressure $P$. The pipe wall thickness is $t = r_e - r_i$, with the thin-wall assumption $t \ll r_i$.

### 3.1.2 Force Equilibrium on a Half-Cylinder Element

Cut the cylinder longitudinally into two halves. The internal pressure acts on the projected area $A_p = D_i \cdot L$ (where $D_i = 2r_i$). This pressure is resisted by the hoop stress $\sigma_h$ acting over the wall cross-sectional area on each side, which is $A_s = 2 \cdot t \cdot L$ (two walls).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pressure Force on Half-Cylinder} &= P \cdot D_i \cdot L \\
\text{Resisting Force from Wall} &= 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t \cdot L
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Setting Forces Equal

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t \cdot L &= P \cdot D_i \cdot L
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividing both sides by $2 \cdot t \cdot L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_h &= \frac{P \cdot D_i}{2 \cdot t}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.4 Using Mean Diameter

For thin-wall assumption, $D_i \approx D_o - t \approx D_m$, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_h &= \frac{P \cdot D_m}{2 \cdot t} = \frac{P \cdot (D_o - t)}{2 \cdot t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** This is the famous Barlow's Equation, foundational to all pressure pipe design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Intuition:** The hoop stress is essentially the "pull" trying to split the pipe open longitudinally. Doubling the pressure doubles the stress; doubling the thickness halves the stress — exactly as your mechanical intuition would predict.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Pressure Rating from Hydrostatic Design Stress

### 3.2.1 Starting Equation

From the hoop stress equation, we solve for pressure $P$ (renamed here as the pressure rating $PR$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_h &= \frac{PR \cdot (D_o - t)}{2 \cdot t}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.2 Rearrangement

$$
\begin{aligned}
PR &= \frac{2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t}{D_o - t}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Substitution of $D_o / t = \text{SDR}$

Since $\text{SDR} = D_o / t$, we have $D_o = t \cdot \text{SDR}$, hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_o - t &= t \cdot \text{SDR} - t = t \cdot (\text{SDR} - 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.4 Substituting Back

$$
\begin{aligned}
PR &= \frac{2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t}{t \cdot (\text{SDR} - 1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The $t$ terms cancel:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PR &= \frac{2 \cdot \sigma_h}{\text{SDR} - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.5 Incorporating Hydrostatic Design Stress (HDS) and Derating Factors

Replacing $\sigma_h$ with $HDS \cdot f_T \cdot f_A$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PR &= \frac{2 \cdot HDS \cdot f_T \cdot f_A}{\text{SDR} - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **master equation** used to select the SDR for a given pressure class in polymer pipe design.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Wall Thickness from Pressure Rating

Starting from:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PR &= \frac{2 \cdot HDS}{SDR - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

We solve for SDR:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PR \cdot (SDR - 1) &= 2 \cdot HDS \\
SDR - 1 &= \frac{2 \cdot HDS}{PR} \\
SDR &= 1 + \frac{2 \cdot HDS}{PR}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\text{SDR} = D_o / t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{D_o}{\text{SDR}} = \frac{D_o}{1 + \dfrac{2 \cdot HDS}{PR}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3.1 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A uPVC pipe of outside diameter $D_o = 110$ mm is required to carry water at a working pressure of $0.6$ MPa at 30°C. Given $HDS = 14$ MPa, $f_T = 1.0$, $f_A = 0.8$. Calculate the wall thickness.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PR_{\text{design}} &= \frac{2 \cdot HDS \cdot f_T \cdot f_A}{SDR - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

For maximum efficiency, choose SDR such that $PR_{\text{design}} \geq P_{\text{working}} = 0.6$ MPa. Try SDR = 13.5:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PR_{\text{design}} &= \frac{2 \cdot 14 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 0.8}{13.5 - 1} = \frac{22.4}{12.5} = 1.792 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $1.792 > 0.6$, SDR = 13.5 is acceptable. Now compute wall thickness:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{D_o}{\text{SDR}} = \frac{110}{13.5} = 8.15 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Adopt next standard thickness: **$t = 8.2$ mm** (or as per IS 4985 table).

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Thermal Expansion Loop Length

The expansion loop is sized so that the pipe can flex sideways to absorb thermal expansion. Consider an L-shaped expansion loop with legs of length $L$. The pipe ends are anchored; the midpoint deflects by $\Delta L / 2$ sideways. Using cantilever beam bending theory:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta L &= \frac{2 \cdot F \cdot L^3}{3 \cdot E \cdot I} \cdot \text{(bending flexibility)}
\end{aligned}
$$

After algebraic manipulation using the standard flexibility factor for an L-loop:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{loop}} &= C \cdot \sqrt{D_o \cdot \Delta L}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $C \approx 30$ for an L-type offset per *PPI Handbook of Polyethylene Pipe*.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Pressure Wave Celerity in Polymer Pipes

The surge pressure wave speed $a$ in an elastic pipe conveying a fluid is given by the Korteweg-Jaeger equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= \frac{\sqrt{\dfrac{K_f / \rho_f}{1 + \dfrac{K_f}{E} \cdot \dfrac{D_i}{t} \cdot c_1}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For water with bulk modulus $K_f = 2.2$ GPa, $\rho_f = 1000$ kg/m³, and typical polymer $E$ values:

- For uPVC: $E \approx 3.0$ GPa → $a \approx 350$ m/s
- For HDPE: $E \approx 0.8$ GPa → $a \approx 220$ m/s

> [!NOTE]
> Compare this to steel pipes where $a \approx 1200$ m/s. Polymer pipes therefore exhibit **lower surge pressures**, making them inherently safer in transient flow conditions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.3 Comparative Block — Polymer vs Traditional Metallic Pipes

```

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**Q:** Define the term **Standard Dimension Ratio (SDR)** as applied to polymer pipes. Mention its significance in pressure pipe design.

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Standard Dimension Ratio (SDR) is the dimensionless ratio of the outside diameter $D_o$ of a pipe to its minimum wall thickness $t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SDR} &= \frac{D_o}{t}
\end{aligned}
$$

It is significant because:
1. It directly determines the **pressure rating** of a pipe through the relation $PR = 2 \cdot \sigma / (\text{SDR} - 1)$
2. It provides a **single parameter** that governs pressure capacity irrespective of pipe diameter
3. Higher SDR → thinner wall → lower pressure rating
4. It enables standardization across manufacturers and diameters

### Question 2
**Q:** List any **four advantages** of polymer pipes over conventional GI pipes in building services.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Corrosion resistance:** Polymer pipes are inherently immune to rust and galvanic corrosion, unlike GI which corrodes in aggressive water/soil.
2. **Light weight:** Polymer pipes weigh 1/5 to 1/8 of equivalent GI pipes, reducing handling and structural load.
3. **Lower thermal conductivity:** $\sim 0.2$ W/m·K for polymer versus $\sim 50$ W/m·K for steel → less heat loss in hot water lines.
4. **Smooth bore:** Hazen-Williams $C \approx 140$–150 yields lower friction losses than $C \approx 120$ for GI.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1

**Q:** *(a)* Explain in detail the various **types of polymer pipes** used in construction, with their material composition, governing Indian Standard, and typical applications. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A uPVC pipe of outside diameter 160 mm is to be used for water supply at a working pressure of 0.8 MPa and 30°C. Given $HDS = 14$ MPa, temperature derating factor $f_T = 1.0$, application factor $f_A = 0.8$, calculate the required SDR and wall thickness. *(7 marks)*

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Types of Polymer Pipes (7 marks)

| S.No | Pipe Type | Material Composition | Indian Standard | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | uPVC / PVC-U | Unplasticized polyvinyl chloride, K-value ≥ 67 | IS 4985, IS 15328 | Cold water, irrigation, sewerage |
| 2 | CPVC | Chlorinated PVC (~63-69% Cl) | IS 15778 | Hot & cold potable water |
| 3 | HDPE | High-density polyethylene, density ≥ 0.941 g/cm³ | IS 4984, IS 14333 | Water mains, gas, marine |
| 4 | MDPE | Medium-density PE | IS 14885 | Gas distribution (PNG) |
| 5 | PPR | Polypropylene random copolymer | IS 15801, DIN 8077 | Hot/cold plumbing |
| 6 | PEX | Cross-linked polyethylene | IS 16086, ASTM F876 | Underfloor heating |
| 7 | GRP | Glass reinforced polyester/epoxy | IS 14457, AWWA C950 | Trunk sewers, jacking pipes |

Each pipe is engineered for specific temperature, pressure, and chemical exposure ranges and should be selected only after checking the relevant IS/ASTM conformance marking on every pipe.

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** $D_o = 160$ mm, $P = 0.8$ MPa, $HDS = 14$ MPa, $f_T = 1.0$, $f_A = 0.8$.

**Step 1 — Design Pressure Class**

For safe design, the actual pressure rating should provide a safety margin. Per IS 4985, a **surge allowance of 50%** is added to working pressure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{design}} &= 1.5 \times 0.8 = 1.2 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for SDR**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{design}} &= \frac{2 \cdot HDS \cdot f_T \cdot f_A}{\text{SDR} - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
1.2 &= \frac{2 \times 14 \times 1.0 \times 0.8}{\text{SDR} - 1} = \frac{22.4}{\text{SDR} - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SDR} - 1 &= \frac{22.4}{1.2} = 18.67
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SDR} &= 19.67 \approx 21
\end{aligned}
$$

Adopt next standard SDR higher than 19.67: **SDR = 21**.

**Step 3 — Compute Wall Thickness**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{D_o}{\text{SDR}} = \frac{160}{21} = 7.62 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Adopt next standard thickness from IS 4985: **$t = 7.7$ mm**.

**Step 4 — Verification**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PR_{\text{actual}} &= \frac{2 \times 14 \times 1.0 \times 0.8}{21 - 1} = \frac{22.4}{20} = 1.12 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $1.12 < 1.2$, marginally below. Retry with SDR = 17:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PR &= \frac{22.4}{16} = 1.40 \text{ MPa} > 1.2 \text{ MPa} \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Therefore adopt: SDR = 17, $t = 160/17 = 9.41$ mm → use $t = 10.0$ mm (IS 4985).**

---

### Question B — Option 2

**Q:** *(a)* Discuss the **jointing methods** for polymer pipes with their suitability for different pipe materials. Explain the heat fusion technique in detail for HDPE pipes. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A 200 m long HDPE pipe carries $0.025$ m³/s of water at 25°C. The pipe internal diameter is 100 mm. Compute the head loss using the Hazen-Williams equation. Take $C = 140$. *(7 marks)*

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Jointing Methods (7 marks)

The principal jointing methods for polymer pipes are:

| Jointing Method | Pipe Material | Mechanism | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Solvent Cement | PVC-U, CPVC | Chemical welding by solvent | Quick, low skill, used in plumbing |
| Socket Fusion | PPR, HDPE (small dia) | Heated socket + pipe, then joined | Used for plumbing transitions |
| Butt Fusion | HDPE (≥ 63 mm) | Heated plate, pressed together | Strongest joint, equal to pipe |
| Electrofusion | HDPE, MDPE | Embedded heating coil melts interface | Used for gas, repairs, fittings |
| Push-Fit / Compression | PEX, PB, PE | Mechanical grip + O-ring | Quick, demountable |
| Flanged | HDPE, GRP (large dia) | Bolted flanges with gasket | Used for valve connections |

**Heat Fusion (Butt Fusion) for HDPE Pipes — Detailed Process:**

1. The pipe ends are clamped in the fusion machine and faced (trimmed) to obtain perfectly parallel surfaces.
2. A **heated plate** (set at ~200°C, controlled by thermocouple per ASTM F2620) is inserted between the pipe ends.
3. The pipe ends are pressed against the plate under a defined **interface pressure** (~0.15 MPa) until a uniform **bead** of molten polymer forms around the circumference.
4. The **dwell time** on the heater is precisely maintained (typically 8-12 s/mm wall thickness).
5. The heater plate is quickly withdrawn, and the molten pipe ends are immediately brought together under **fusion pressure** (~0.15 MPa).
6. The joint is held under pressure and allowed to cool for a calculated **cool time** per material.
7. The resulting joint, on testing per ASTM F2620, must have **tensile strength ≥ pipe parent material** and the bead profile must be uniform on both sides.

This produces a **homogeneous, leak-proof** joint with no internal bead (or a small removable bead) that is hydraulically equivalent to the parent pipe.

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** $L = 200$ m, $Q = 0.025$ m³/s, $D = 100$ mm = $0.1$ m, $C = 140$.

Hazen-Williams formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_f &= \frac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot D^{4.87}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute $Q^{1.852}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^{1.852} &= (0.025)^{1.852}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using logarithms: $\log(0.025) = -1.602$, so $\log(0.025^{1.852}) = -1.602 \times 1.852 = -2.967$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^{1.852} &= 10^{-2.967} = 0.001081 \text{ m}^{5.556}\text{/s}^{1.852}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $C^{1.852}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C^{1.852} &= 140^{1.852}
\end{aligned}
$$

$\log(140) = 2.146$, so $\log(140^{1.852}) = 2.146 \times 1.852 = 3.974$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C^{1.852} &= 10^{3.974} = 9420
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $D^{4.87}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^{4.87} &= (0.1)^{4.87}
\end{aligned}
$$

$\log(0.1) = -1$, so $\log(0.1^{4.87}) = -4.87$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^{4.87} &= 10^{-4.87} = 1.349 \times 10^{-5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $h_f$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_f &= \frac{10.67 \times 200 \times 0.001081}{9420 \times 1.349 \times 10^{-5}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_f &= \frac{2.3067}{0.1271} = 18.15 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result: Head loss $h_f \approx 18.15$ m.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mistakes where students lose marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Converting $D$ to mm instead of m in Hazen-Williams (the constant 10.67 requires $D$ in m and $Q$ in m³/s). This is the #1 reason students lose 3-4 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting Surge Allowance:** In Part B numericals, examiners expect students to add **50% surge allowance** to working pressure per IS 4985. Omission costs full 7 marks.
> 3. **Confusing SDR with DR:** SDR uses *minimum* wall thickness; DR uses *average*. For thermoplastic pipes, they are numerically equal in standard practice, but stating the difference shows depth.
> 4. **No Derating Factors:** Quoting $HDS = 14$ MPa directly without $f_T$ and $f_A$ loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Head Loss Units:** Reporting head loss in "MPa" instead of "metres of water" — examiners are strict on unit presentation.
> 6. **Skip Steps:** Examiners award marks for each logical step. Skipping from given data to final answer forfeits 50% of the marks allotted.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Piping (Module 1)**

- **Polymer pipes** are made from thermoplastics (PVC, CPVC, HDPE, PPR, PEX, ABS) and thermosets (GRP, RPM).
- **uPVC (IS 4985)** is the workhorse of cold water and sewerage applications in India.
- **CPVC (IS 15778)** extends polymer piping to hot water service up to 93°C.
- **HDPE (IS 4984)** dominates in water mains, gas distribution, and trenchless applications due to flexibility and fusion welding.
- **SDR** is the single most important pipe parameter governing pressure rating.
- **Master equation:** $PR = 2 \cdot HDS \cdot f_T \cdot f_A / (\text{SDR} - 1)$
- **Barlow's hoop stress:** $\sigma_h = P \cdot (D_o - t) / (2t)$
- **Hazen-Williams C** for polymer pipes is **140–150**, much higher than metallic pipes (100–120), giving lower friction losses.
- **Surge allowance** of **+50%** must be added to working pressure per IS 4985 for PVC and IS 4984 for HDPE.
- **Thermal expansion** of HDPE is **~10× higher** than metallic pipes → expansion loops or offsets are mandatory in long above-ground runs.
- **Butt fusion (ASTM F2620)** is the gold standard jointing method for HDPE, producing a joint as strong as the parent pipe.
- **Solvent cement** joints are restricted to PVC-U and CPVC only — never use on PE or PP.
- **Celerity (wave speed)** in polymer pipes is **220–400 m/s**, much lower than steel (~1200 m/s), reducing water hammer severity.
- **Service life benchmark** = **50 years**, on which all HDS values are statistically extrapolated.
- **Standards to memorize:** IS 4985 (uPVC), IS 4984 (HDPE), IS 15778 (CPVC), IS 15801 (PPR), IS 14457 (GRP).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT758 - POLYMERS IN CONSTRUCTION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Cables # Cables in Construction: A Polymer Engineering Perspective ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of KTU's **PEPOT758 – Polymers in Construction**, a **cable** refers to a tension-carrying structural member composed of metallic wires/strands, polymeric fibres, or a hybrid combination of both, used to transmit tensile loads in civil engineering structures. The **polymer component** in cables manifests in three principal forms: 1. **Polymer-sheathed steel cables** (used in prestressing tendons and stay cables of cable-stayed bridges). 2. **Fibre-Reinforced Polymer (FRP) cables** (carbon, aramid, or glass fibres embedded in epoxy/polyester matrices). 3. **Synthetic polymer ropes** (high-modulus polyethylene, polypropylene, polyester — used for temporary bracing, lifting, and marine applications). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition:** A cable in construction is a flexible, high-tensile strength member made from one or more metallic or non-metallic (polymeric) elements bound together to act as a single unit in resisting tensile forces. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **human muscle**: a muscle is not one solid strand of fibre but thousands of tiny fibres bundled together. This is exactly how a **cable** is engineered — many thin wires or filaments bundled and twisted to create a single, strong, flexible tension member. The **polymer sheathing** (typically **High-Density Polyethylene (HDPE)**) acts like the **skin around the muscle**, providing: - Corrosion protection (waterproof barrier) - UV resistance (sun protection) - Mechanical cushioning against abrasion A **wire rope** used in a cable-stayed bridge is essentially a "muscle with skin", where the steel wires are the muscle fibres and HDPE sheath is the protective skin. ### 1.3 Classification Snapshot | Cable Category | Primary Material | Typical Polymer Role | Construction Use | |---|---|---|---| | **Steel Wire Strand** | High-tensile steel wires | HDPE sheathing | Prestressed concrete, stay cables | | **FRP Cable** | Carbon/Aramid/Glass fibres | Epoxy/polyester matrix | Corrosion-free reinforcement | | **Synthetic Rope** | HMPE/PP/PET | The rope itself | Temporary lifting, marine ties | | **Hybrid Cable** | Steel + FRP | Polymer matrix bonding | Long-span bridges, seismic retrofit | > [!NOTE] > **Key Terminology:** The term **"strand"** refers to an assembly of wires helically laid together, while a **"rope"** consists of multiple strands laid helically around a central core. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Structural Composition of a Polymer-Protected Cable A modern polymer-sheathed stay cable (e.g., as used in the **Bandra-Worli Sea-Link, Mumbai** or **Signature Bridge, Delhi**) consists of the following layered architecture: - **Core:** Bundle of high-tensile steel wires (typically 7-wire strands of diameter **15.7 mm**). - **Wedge-anchor system:** Mechanical gripping at both ends to transfer load to the structure. - **Corrosion protection:** Each individual wire is galvanized; the bundle is then encased in a wax-filled HDPE sheath. - **Outer HDPE sheath:** UV-stabilized, ribbed or smooth, providing the final environmental barrier. - **Damper systems:** Often fitted with **viscoelastic polymer dampers** at the lower end to suppress rain-wind induced vibrations. ### 2.2 Design Philosophy — Why Polymers? The introduction of polymers into cable construction addresses **three fundamental engineering challenges**: 1. **Corrosion mitigation** — Steel corrodes in marine/coastal atmospheres; HDPE/FPR offers inherent corrosion resistance. 2. **Fatigue performance** — Polymer matrices dampen micro-vibrations and reduce fatigue crack propagation in metallic components. 3. **Weight reduction** — FRP cables have a density of approximately **1.5–2.1 g/cm³** versus **7.85 g/cm³** for steel, drastically reducing dead load. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Property / Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Cross-sectional area of strand | $A_s$ | $A_s = n \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$ | mm² | | Tensile capacity | $P_u$ | $P_u = f_{pu} \cdot A_s$ | kN | | Young's Modulus of steel | $E_s$ | $E_s = 2.0 \times 10^5$ | N/mm² | | Young's Modulus of CFRP | $E_{cfrp}$ | $E_{cfrp} = (1.4 \text{ to } 2.0) \times 10^5$ | N/mm² | | Young's Modulus of GFRP | $E_{gfrp}$ | $E_{gfrp} = (3.5 \text{ to } 5.1) \times 10^4$ | N/mm² | | Density of HDPE sheath | $\rho_{HDPE}$ | $\rho_{HDPE} = 0.94 \text{ to } 0.97$ | g/cm³ | | Elongation at break (HDPE) | $\varepsilon$ | $\varepsilon = 600 \text{ to } 1200$ | % | | Thermal expansion (steel) | $\alpha_s$ | $\alpha_s = 11.7 \times 10^{-6}$ | /°C | | Thermal expansion (HDPE) | $\alpha_p$ | $\alpha_p = (100 \text{ to } 200) \times 10^{-6}$ | /°C | | Breaking strength efficiency | $\eta$ | $\eta = \frac{\text{Ultimate load}}{\sum \text{individual wire strength}}$ | % | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Engineering Constant:** For HDPE used as cable sheathing, the **Melt Flow Index (MFI)** must be maintained between **0.2–0.5 g/10 min** (at 190°C / 2.16 kg load) to ensure adequate environmental stress cracking resistance. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Polymer-incorporated cables are extensively used in: - **Cable-stayed and suspension bridges** for main stay cables and hangers. - **Prestressed concrete** structures — tendons housed in HDPE ducts. - **Post-tensioning systems** in high-rise building slabs and transfer girders. - **External prestressing** for retrofitting aged reinforced concrete structures. - **Tensile membrane structures** where HMPE or aramid cables form the primary load-carrying skeleton. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Tensile Load Capacity of a Standard 7-Wire Strand A **standard 7-wire strand** (commonly designated as **15.7 mm nominal diameter**) comprises **1 central wire** and **6 helical outer wires**. Let us derive its ultimate tensile capacity. ### Step 1: Identify the Geometric Inputs Let the diameter of each individual wire be **$d$** mm. For a 7-wire strand of nominal diameter **$D$**: $$D = 3d$$ This is because the outer 6 wires wrap around the central wire, with each outer wire having diameter $d$, so the total strand diameter equals $3d$ (one central wire diameter plus two outer wire diameters). ### Step 2: Calculate the Cross-Sectional Area of One Wire The area of one circular wire is: $$A_{wire} = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$ ### Step 3: Calculate the Total Metallic Cross-Sectional Area For 7 wires: $$A_s = 7 \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$ Substituting $d = D/3$: $$A_s = 7 \cdot \frac{\pi (D/3)^2}{4}$$ $$\begin{aligned} A_s &= \frac{7 \pi D^2}{4 \times 9} \\ A_s &= \frac{7 \pi D^2}{36} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 4: Compute the Ultimate Tensile Load Let the ultimate tensile strength of individual wire be **$f_{pu}$** (typically **1860 N/mm²** for prestressing strands). Then: $$P_u = f_{pu} \cdot A_s$$ $$\begin{aligned} P_u &= f_{pu} \cdot \frac{7 \pi D^2}{36} \\ P_u &= \frac{7 \pi f_{pu} D^2}{36} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 5: Numerical Example for Standard 15.7 mm Strand Given: $D = 15.7$ mm, $f_{pu} = 1860$ N/mm². $$A_s = \frac{7 \times \pi \times (15.7)^2}{36}$$ $$\begin{aligned} A_s &= \frac{7 \times \pi \times 246.49}{36} \\ A_s &= \frac{5418.7}{36} \\ A_s &= 150.52 \text{ mm}^2 \end{aligned}$$ The ultimate tensile capacity: $$\begin{aligned} P_u &= 1860 \times 150.52 \\ P_u &= 279,967 \text{ N} \\ P_u &\approx 280 \text{ kN} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 6: Account for Helical Lay Loss (Efficiency Factor) When wires are helically twisted, they carry load at an angle, reducing effective capacity. The **breaking strength efficiency** $\eta$ is typically **0.92–0.95** for standard strands. Therefore: $$P_{u,\text{actual}} = \eta \cdot P_u$$ For $\eta = 0.95$: $$P_{u,\text{actual}} = 0.95 \times 280 = 266 \text{ kN}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** In KTU examination problems, always check whether the helical lay efficiency factor $\eta$ is to be applied. Many textbook problems ignore this for simplicity, but board-level numerical problems often include it. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Performance of Cable Materials | Property | Steel Strand | CFRP Cable | GFRP Cable | Aramid (AFRP) Cable | |---|---|---|---|---| | Density (g/cm³) | **7.85** | 1.5–2.1 | 1.5–2.0 | 1.25–1.45 | | Tensile Strength (MPa) | 1860 | **2400–3700** | 1500–2400 | 2400–3600 | | Young's Modulus (GPa) | 195–210 | **140–200** | 35–51 | 70–130 | | Strain at failure (%) | 3.5–4.5 | 1.2–1.8 | 2.0–4.5 | 2.0–4.0 | | Corrosion Resistance | Poor (needs HDPE) | **Excellent** | **Excellent** | **Excellent** | | Fatigue Resistance | Moderate | **Superior** | Moderate | Good | | Cost (Relative) | 1× | **8–12×** | 3–5× | 5–8× | | Magnetic Neutrality | No | Yes (CFRP) | Yes | Yes | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Cross-Sectional Architecture of a Polymer-Sheathed Stay Cable ```mermaid flowchart TB A["CENTRAL WIRE
Diameter d"] --> B["HELICAL OUTER WIRES
6 wires around core"] B --> C["WAX / GREASE FILL
Corrosion inhibitor"] C --> D["INNER HDPE SHEATH
Wall thickness 2-4 mm"] D --> E["OUTER HDPE SHEATH
UV-stabilized ribbed"] E --> F["HELICAL WINDING
Polyester tape / wire"] F --> G["FINAL CABLE ASSEMBLY
Typical 15-200 strands"] H["ANCHORAGE AT TOP
Wedge grips / epoxy"] -.-> G I["DAMPER AT BOTTOM
Viscoelastic polymer"] -.-> G style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#C0C0C0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style F fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#2F4F4F,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ## 4.2 Functional Flow — Cable Manufacturing & Installation Lifecycle ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["STEP 1: WIRE DRAWING
Cold-work high-carbon steel
to final diameter d"] --> P2["STEP 2: WIRE STRANDING
7 wires twisted helically
around central core"] P2 --> P3["STEP 3: GALVANIZING
Hot-dip zinc coating
25-50 micrometre"] P3 --> P4["STEP 4: HDPE EXTRUSION
Polymer sheath extruded
at 200-230 degrees C"] P4 --> P5["STEP 5: CABLE ASSEMBLY
Multiple strands bundled
and tensioned"] P5 --> P6["STEP 6: SITE INSTALLATION
Anchored at both ends
stressed to design load"] P6 --> P7["STEP 7: MONITORING
Load cells, accelerometers
periodic inspection"] style P1 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style P2 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style P3 fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style P4 fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style P5 fill:#F0FFF0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style P6 fill:#F0FFF0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style P7 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — Selection Logic for Cable Material ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["SELECTION OF CABLE MATERIAL
FOR STRUCTURAL APPLICATION"] --> Q1{"Corrosive environment?
Marine or chemical zone"} Q1 -- "YES" --> Q2{"High stiffness required?
Long-span bridge / tower"} Q1 -- "NO" --> S1["USE STEEL STRAND
with HDPE protection"] Q2 -- "YES" --> S2["USE CFRP CABLE
Carbon Fibre Reinforced Polymer"] Q2 -- "NO" --> Q3{"Cost sensitive project?
Budget constraint"} Q3 -- "YES" --> S3["USE GFRP CABLE
Glass Fibre Reinforced Polymer"] Q3 -- "NO" --> S4["USE ARAMID AFRP
Kevlar based cable"] style Start fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style S1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style S2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style S3 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style S4 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define a cable in the context of polymer-incorporated structural cables. List any two advantages of using HDPE sheathing over traditional steel conduit protection.** **Model Answer:** A cable is a flexible tension member composed of metallic wires, polymeric fibres, or hybrid combinations, designed to transmit tensile loads in civil engineering structures. In polymer-incorporated cables, polymers are used either as the primary load-carrying component (FRP cables) or as protective sheathing (HDPE sheath). **Two advantages of HDPE sheathing:** 1. **Superior corrosion protection:** HDPE provides a continuous, seamless waterproof barrier that prevents chloride and moisture ingress into the steel core, eliminating the need for grouted ducts. 2. **UV and weather resistance:** UV-stabilized HDPE maintains integrity under prolonged sunlight exposure, with a service life exceeding **50 years** in coastal environments. --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between a 7-wire strand and a 19-wire strand. State one application of each.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | 7-Wire Strand | 19-Wire Strand | |---|---|---| | Configuration | 1 central + 6 helical | 1 central + 6 inner + 12 outer | | Diameter range | 9.5 – 17.8 mm | 17.8 – 28.6 mm | | Cross-section area | Smaller | Larger | | Tensile capacity | ~260 kN (15.7 mm) | ~500–900 kN | | Application | Slab post-tensioning | Bridge prestressing tendons, stay cables | --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]` **(a)** Describe with neat sketches the layered cross-sectional composition of a **polymer-sheathed stay cable** used in modern cable-stayed bridges. Explain the function of each layer in detail. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A standard **15.7 mm diameter, 7-wire prestressing strand** has a guaranteed ultimate tensile strength of **1860 MPa**. Determine: **(7 Marks)** (i) The cross-sectional area of the strand. (ii) The ultimate tensile load capacity. (iii) The effective design load using **0.95** helical lay efficiency factor. **Model Solution (a):** A modern polymer-sheathed stay cable (e.g., used in **Signature Bridge, Delhi**) consists of the following layers from inside to outside: 1. **Central Steel Wire:** The straight core wire of diameter $d$, which provides the structural backbone. Made from high-tensile cold-drawn steel (UTS = 1860 MPa). 2. **Helical Outer Wires (6 Nos):** Six wires of diameter $d$ helically twisted around the central wire. They share the tensile load and provide flexibility. 3. **Galvanizing Layer:** A zinc coating of 25–50 µm applied via hot-dip galvanizing, offering primary corrosion resistance to individual wires. 4. **Wax / Grease Fill:** The voids between wires are filled with microcrystalline wax or grease, displacing moisture and providing secondary corrosion protection and lubricity. 5. **Inner HDPE Sheath:** Extruded HDPE tube of 2–4 mm wall thickness, providing a continuous moisture barrier. The HDPE must have **MFI ≤ 0.5 g/10 min** for stress-cracking resistance. 6. **Outer HDPE Sheath:** UV-stabilized, ribbed or spiral-binder reinforced HDPE jacket, protecting against sunlight, abrasion, and weathering. 7. **Spiral Winding / Outer Binding:** Polymer or steel helix wound over the sheath to provide additional mechanical restraint and a reference surface for damper installation. 8. **Damper System (External):** Viscoelastic polymer dampers are often clamped near the lower anchorage to suppress rain-wind induced vibrations (a critical phenomenon for cables above 80 m length). *(Schematic sketch as shown in SECTION 4.1 above — 7 marks awarded for diagram, layer identification, and functional explanation of each layer.)* **Model Solution (b):** **Given:** - Strand nominal diameter $D = 15.7$ mm - Ultimate tensile strength $f_{pu} = 1860$ N/mm² - Helical lay efficiency $\eta = 0.95$ **(i) Cross-sectional area:** For a 7-wire strand, individual wire diameter $d = D/3 = 15.7/3 = 5.233$ mm. $$\begin{aligned} A_s &= 7 \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \\ A_s &= 7 \cdot \frac{\pi \times (5.233)^2}{4} \\ A_s &= 7 \cdot \frac{\pi \times 27.385}{4} \\ A_s &= 7 \cdot 21.504 \\ A_s &= 150.53 \text{ mm}^2 \end{aligned}$$ **(ii) Ultimate tensile load:** $$\begin{aligned} P_u &= f_{pu} \cdot A_s \\ P_u &= 1860 \times 150.53 \\ P_u &= 279,985 \text{ N} \\ P_u &\approx 280 \text{ kN} \end{aligned}$$ **(iii) Effective design load:** $$\begin{aligned} P_{design} &= \eta \cdot P_u \\ P_{design} &= 0.95 \times 280 \\ P_{design} &= 266 \text{ kN} \end{aligned}$$ **Final Answer:** $A_s = 150.53$ mm²; $P_u = 280$ kN; $P_{design} = 266$ kN. --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]` **(a)** Compare the properties and applications of **CFRP, GFRP, and AFRP cables** with conventional steel strands used in construction. Tabulate the comparison covering density, tensile strength, modulus of elasticity, corrosion resistance, and cost. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss in detail the **advantages and limitations** of using **polymer-based cables** in prestressed concrete structures. Mention at least **three specific examples** of structures where FRP cables have been used globally. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution (a):** | Property | Steel Strand | CFRP Cable | GFRP Cable | AFRP (Aramid) Cable | |---|---|---|---|---| | Density (g/cm³) | 7.85 | 1.5–2.1 | 1.5–2.0 | 1.25–1.45 | | Tensile Strength (MPa) | 1860 | 2400–3700 | 1500–2400 | 2400–3600 | | Elastic Modulus (GPa) | 195–210 | 140–200 | 35–51 | 70–130 | | Corrosion Resistance | Poor | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | | Relative Cost | 1× | 8–12× | 3–5× | 5–8× | **Applications:** - **Steel strand:** Prestressed concrete beams, slab post-tensioning, conventional stay cables. - **CFRP:** Long-span bridges (Stork Bridge, Switzerland), structures requiring high stiffness without magnetic interference. - **GFRP:** Reinforcing bars in concrete, pedestrian bridges, chemical plant structures. - **AFRP (Aramid):** Lightweight suspension elements, retrofitting of historical structures where low weight is critical. *(7 marks for tabular comparison and application clarity)* **Model Solution (b):** **Advantages of polymer-based cables:** 1. **Corrosion immunity** — Eliminates chloride-induced deterioration, extending service life in marine environments. 2. **High strength-to-weight ratio** — CFRP is **5× lighter** and **2× stronger** than steel. 3. **Electromagnetic neutrality** — CFRP and GFRP are non-magnetic, ideal for MRI rooms and railway signalling zones. 4. **Lower thermal conductivity** — Reduces thermal bridging in cold-storage structures. 5. **Tailorable modulus** — Modulus can be engineered by fibre type and volume fraction. **Limitations:** 1. **High initial cost** — 3–12× more expensive than steel. 2. **Brittle failure mode** — No plastic yielding before rupture; sudden collapse risk. 3. **Low shear strength** — Particularly in transverse direction. 4. **Anchorage difficulty** — Conventional wedge grips are unsuitable; need specialized potted/resin anchors. 5. **Limited long-term data** — Less than 50 years of field performance history versus 100+ years for steel. **Global Examples:** 1. **Stork Bridge, Winterthur, Switzerland** — First cable-stayed bridge using CFRP stay cables (1996). 2. **Ludwigshafen Bridge, Germany** — Prestressed concrete bridge using CFRP tendons. 3. **Hisho Bridge, Japan** — CFRP reinforced pedestrian bridge. 4. **Tower Block, Tokyo, Japan** — Used GFRP tendons in floor slabs. 5. **Schiessbergstraße Bridge, Germany** — External AFRP prestressing for retrofit. *(7 marks for comprehensive discussion with at least 3 examples)* --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting the helical efficiency factor ($\eta$):** Many students compute $P_u = f_{pu} \times A_s$ directly and stop. Always check whether the question specifies a lay efficiency or breaks the load, and apply $\eta$ if required. > 2. **Confusing wire diameter vs strand diameter:** For a 7-wire strand, wire diameter $d = D/3$, not $D/7$. This is the **single most common error** in KTU valuation. > 3. **Ignoring unit conversions:** Express final answers in **kN or N**, not raw N×mm². Always cross-check unit consistency. > 4. **Writing $D = 2d$ for the strand diameter:** This is WRONG for a 7-wire strand. Correct geometric relation is $D = 3d$. > 5. **Omitting diagrams:** In 14-mark questions, **diagrams carry 2–3 marks minimum**. A neat labelled cross-section is mandatory. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ A **cable** in construction is a tension member made of metallic wires, polymer fibres, or hybrid systems. - ✅ **HDPE sheathing** is the most common polymer application for protecting steel strands (MFI 0.2–0.5 g/10 min). - ✅ **FRP cables** (CFRP, GFRP, AFRP) offer corrosion immunity, high strength-to-weight ratio, and electromagnetic neutrality. - ✅ For a **7-wire strand**: wire diameter $d = D/3$, total wires = 7, area $A_s = 7 \cdot \pi d^2 / 4$. - ✅ Ultimate load $P_u = f_{pu} \cdot A_s$; apply helical lay efficiency $\eta = 0.92$–$0.95$ for design. - ✅ **CFRP** has the highest modulus among FRPs (140–200 GPa), closest to steel. - ✅ **GFRP** is the most economical FRP option but has lower stiffness. - ✅ Polymer cables require **specialized anchorage systems** (resin-potted, spike, or bonded anchors), NOT conventional wedge grips. - ✅ **Viscoelastic polymer dampers** are essential accessories on long stay cables to suppress rain-wind vibrations. - ✅ Always express load in **kN** and stress in **MPa (N/mm²)** for KTU numerical answers. - ✅ In KTU 14-mark answers, **neat labelled diagrams** are non-negotiable — they fetch 2–3 marks independently of the written explanation.

topics.title: Cladding and Profiles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT758 - POLYMERS IN CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cladding and Profiles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Cladding** in polymer construction refers to a non-load-bearing, weather-resistant external or internal skin layer applied to a building's structural frame, manufactured predominantly from polymeric materials such as **uPVC (unplasticized Polyvinyl Chloride)**, **GRP (Glass Reinforced Plastic / FRP)**, **Polycarbonate (PC)**, **Acrylic (PMMA)**, **HDPE**, and composite polymer blends. Cladding functions as the protective "envelope" of a structure against **moisture ingress, UV radiation, thermal fluctuations, and chemical attack**, while also providing aesthetic finish.

**Profiles** are standardized, geometrically-defined cross-sectional shapes of polymeric materials produced by continuous manufacturing processes (extrusion, pultrusion, or co-extrusion). In construction, profiles include **window frames, door frames, door panels, partition sections, structural channels, ceiling grids, skirting, architraves, and curtain-wall mullions**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** Cladding = protective surface envelope; Profiles = load-bearing or framing cross-sectional shapes. Both are predominantly manufactured from polymers using **extrusion** technology.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a human being in winter:
- **Skeleton (Structure)** → The structural RCC/steel frame of the building
- **Skin (Cladding)** → The polymer outer layer that protects against rain, wind, cold, and sun
- **Skeleton joints (Profiles)** → The polymer sections (window/door frames) that hold the skin together and provide shape, much like cartilage that connects skin to bones

Just as the skin must be **flexible, waterproof, UV-resistant**, and the joints must be **rigid, dimensionally stable, and strong**, polymer cladding and profiles are engineered to serve these exact complementary functions in a building.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following metrics are universally adopted in KTU Board Examinations for polymer cladding and profile evaluation:

| Metric | Standard Value / Range | Reference Context |
|---|---|---|
| Density of uPVC | **1.40 – 1.45 g/cm³** | BS EN 13245 |
| Density of GRP/FRP | **1.50 – 1.90 g/cm³** | ASTM D792 |
| Density of HDPE | **0.94 – 0.97 g/cm³** | ISO 1183 |
| Linear Thermal Expansion (uPVC) | **50 – 80 × 10⁻⁶ /K** | ISO 11359 |
| Service Temperature Range (uPVC) | **−15 °C to +60 °C** | EN 12608 |
| Fire Rating (Typical uPVC) | **Class B-s3, d0** (Euroclass) | EN 13501-1 |
| Impact Strength (uPVC profile) | **≥ 10 kJ/m²** | EN 477 |

> [!NOTE]
> Always cite the standard code (e.g., **EN 12608** for uPVC window profiles) when answering KTU questions — this fetches additional valuation marks from examiners.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Polymer Cladding Systems

Polymer claddings are classified based on **panel geometry, fixing mechanism, and polymer matrix**:

- **A. Horizontal/Vertical Lap Cladding (Weatherboard Style):** uPVC shiplap, cellular PVC planks, fibre-cement composites with polymer coating.
- **B. Panel Cladding (Rainscreen Systems):** Flat composite panels (ACP – Aluminium Composite Panels with PE/FR core), HPL (High-Pressure Laminate), solid phenolic panels, GRP sheets.
- **C. Sheet/Tile Cladding:** Corrugated polymer roofing sheets (FRP, PC corrugated), PVC ceiling tiles, acrylic wall panels.
- **D. Sandwich Panel Cladding:** Two polymer skins bonded to a core (PIR/PUR/PIR foam) — typical for cold storage and industrial buildings.

### 2.2 Classification of Construction Profiles

- **Hollow Profiles:** Multi-chamber uPVC window/door profiles (3-chamber, 5-chamber, 6-chamber systems).
- **Solid Profiles:** Compact skirting, architrave, handrail sections.
- **Open Profiles:** U-channels, C-channels, J-channels used in false ceilings and partition frameworks.
- **Co-extruded Profiles:** Dual-hardness profiles (rigid core + flexible gasket coextruded).
- **Reinforced Profiles:** Steel-reinforced uPVC profiles for large spans.
- **Pultruded Profiles:** Glass-fibre reinforced polymer (GFRP) sections, I-beams, channels, angles.

### 2.3 Manufacturing Process Logic

#### 2.3.1 Extrusion of uPVC Profiles (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Raw Material Preparation:** uPVC resin + stabilizers (Ca-Zn or Pb-based) + impact modifiers + TiO₂ + lubricants + fillers.
2. **Dry Blending:** High-speed mixer at **1200 rpm** for 10–15 min to homogenize additives.
3. **Compounding:** Twin-screw extruder melts and disperses additives.
4. **Die Forming:** Conical die shapes the molten polymer into the desired cross-section.
5. **Calibration & Cooling:** Vacuum calibration tank with water spray (1–5 °C).
6. **Haul-off & Cutting:** Caterpillar puller advances the profile; saw cuts to **6 m standard length**.
7. **Corner Cleaning & Welding:** For frames, mitre-cut and fusion-welded at **260–280 °C** (hot-plate welding).

#### 2.3.2 Pultrusion of GFRP Profiles (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Fibre Roving Creels:** Glass fibres fed from multiple spools.
2. **Resin Bath Impregnation:** Fibres dipped in polyester/vinylester/ epoxy resin.
3. **Pre-former:** Shapes fibres into near-final geometry.
4. **Heated Die:** Cures resin at **120–180 °C** as fibres are pulled through.
5. **Pulling Mechanism:** Caterpillar grippers.
6. **Cut-off Saw:** Length cut to specification (typically **6 m or 12 m**).

### 2.4 Key 'Why & How' Rationale

- **Why multi-chamber profiles?** Air trapped in hollow chambers acts as a **thermal insulator**, reducing heat transfer (low U-value ~1.2–1.8 W/m²K).
- **Why TiO₂ additive in uPVC?** TiO₂ acts as a **UV screen**, protecting the polymer matrix from photo-degradation (yellowing & embrittlement).
- **Why co-extrude flexible gaskets?** To achieve **monolithic weather-seal** between the rigid profile and glass/glazing bead.
- **Why pultrusion for FRP profiles?** Continuous fibre alignment gives **high specific strength** (strength-to-weight ratio ~5× that of structural steel).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Tensile stress on profile cross-section | N/mm² (MPa) |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | Strain in profile under load | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ | Young's Modulus (Modulus of Elasticity) | MPa or GPa |
| 4 | $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L_0 \cdot \Delta T$ | Linear thermal expansion of polymer profile | m, mm |
| 5 | $U = \dfrac{1}{R_{total}} = \dfrac{1}{R_{si} + \sum \frac{d_i}{\lambda_i} + R_{se}}$ | Thermal Transmittance (U-value) of cladding | W/(m²·K) |
| 6 | $\lambda = \dfrac{Q \cdot d}{A \cdot \Delta T \cdot t}$ | Thermal Conductivity (Fourier's Law) | W/(m·K) |
| 7 | $R = \dfrac{\Delta T}{\dot{Q}}$ | Thermal Resistance | m²·K/W |
| 8 | $\rho_b = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | Bulk density of polymer profile | kg/m³ |
| 9 | $I_z = \sum \dfrac{b h^3}{12}$ | Moment of Inertia for composite profile (rectangular parts) | mm⁴ |
| 10 | $S = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I}$ | Bending stress in profile under bending moment | N/mm² |
| 11 | $\delta_{max} = \dfrac{5 w L^4}{384 E I}$ | Maximum deflection of simply-supported profile (UDL) | mm |
| 12 | $f = \dfrac{\sigma_u}{SF}$ | Allowable design stress using Factor of Safety | MPa |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU derivations, the **thermal expansion (Eq. 4)** and **U-value (Eq. 5)** are most frequently asked. Always remember to convert **°C to K** for ΔT (1 K = 1 °C in temperature difference).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Polymer cladding and profiles are critical in:
