### 2.5 Fundamental Relationships (The "Holy Trinity" of Porosity)

The three formulas below are **inter-linked** and appear in nearly every KTU exam:

$$
n = \dfrac{e}{1+e} \qquad e = \dfrac{n}{1-n} \qquad S \cdot e = w \cdot G
$$

Where:
- $w$ = water content (decimal)
- $G$ = specific gravity of solids
- $S$ = degree of saturation (decimal)

### 2.6 Engineering Utility of This Knowledge

- **Mix-design** of concrete: requires $G$ of cement, $G$ of aggregates, water absorption of aggregates → directly uses Formulae 2, 7.
- **Quality control** of bricks/blocks: uses water absorption (Formula 7) – IS 1077 limits $W_a \le 20\%$.
- **Selection of polymers**: A geo-membrane (HDPE) is chosen when **chemical resistance + low water absorption** are critical.
- **Durability audit**: Porosity & degree of saturation govern **freeze–thaw damage** — if $S > 91.7\%$ in cold climates, the material is at risk.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Porosity–Void Ratio Relationship

### Statement
Show that $n = \dfrac{e}{1+e}$ and conversely $e = \dfrac{n}{1-n}$.

### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

By **definition**:

$$
V = V_s + V_v \quad \text{(total volume = solids + voids)}
$$

Dividing both sides by $V_s$:

$$
\dfrac{V}{V_s} = 1 + \dfrac{V_v}{V_s}
$$

Recognizing that $\dfrac{V}{V_s} = \dfrac{1}{1-n}$ (since $n = V_v/V$):

$$
\dfrac{1}{1-n} = 1 + e
$$

Solving for $n$:

$$
1 - n = \dfrac{1}{1+e} \quad \Rightarrow \quad n = 1 - \dfrac{1}{1+e}
$$

Common-denominator manipulation:

$$
n = \dfrac{(1+e) - 1}{1+e} = \dfrac{e}{1+e} \qquad \blacksquare
$$

**Converse derivation** (reverse the algebra):

$$
n(1-n)^{-1} \text{ algebra: } \quad n = \dfrac{e}{1+e} \;\Rightarrow\; n(1+e) = e \;\Rightarrow\; n + ne = e
$$

$$
\Rightarrow n = e(1-n) \;\Rightarrow\; e = \dfrac{n}{1-n} \qquad \blacksquare
$$

### Numerical Sanity Check

If $e = 0.60$ (loose sand):

$$
n = \dfrac{0.60}{1.60} = 0.375 = 37.5\% \quad \checkmark
$$

Reverse check: $e = \dfrac{0.375}{0.625} = 0.60$ ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Saturation–Water Content Relationship ($S \cdot e = w \cdot G$)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Mass of water $M_w = \rho_w \cdot V_w$

Mass of solids $M_s = \rho_s \cdot V_s = G \cdot \rho_w \cdot V_s$

By definition of water content:

$$
w = \dfrac{M_w}{M_s} = \dfrac{\rho_w V_w}{G \rho_w V_s} = \dfrac{V_w}{G \cdot V_s}
$$

By definition of saturation:

$$
S = \dfrac{V_w}{V_v}
$$

Multiplying both sides by $e = V_v / V_s$:

$$
S \cdot e = \dfrac{V_w}{V_v} \cdot \dfrac{V_v}{V_s} = \dfrac{V_w}{V_s} = w \cdot G
$$

$$
\boxed{\;S \cdot e = w \cdot G\;} \qquad \blacksquare
$$

### Engineering Interpretation

- This equation connects **field-measurable** ($w$, $G$) quantities with **lab-measurable** ($S$, $e$) quantities.
- Used to predict whether a soil or porous material is **saturated, dry, or partially wet** — critical for polymeric geo-membrane design beneath foundations.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical

### Problem
A construction brick has mass $M_{dry} = 3.20 \, kg$ in oven-dry condition and $M_{wet} = 3.60 \, kg$ after 24-hour immersion. The bulk volume is $V = 2300 \, cm^3$ and the specific gravity of the brick solids is $G = 2.65$. Calculate **(a)** water absorption, **(b)** bulk density, **(c)** apparent porosity, and **(d)** degree of saturation.

### Solution (Exhaustive Step-by-Step)

**(a) Water Absorption:**

$$
W_a = \dfrac{M_{wet} - M_{dry}}{M_{dry}} \times 100
$$

$$
W_a = \dfrac{3.60 - 3.20}{3.20} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.40}{3.20} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\;W_a = 12.5\%\;}
$$

**(b) Bulk Density:**

$$
\rho_b = \dfrac{M_{dry}}{V} = \dfrac{3.20 \, kg}{2300 \times 10^{-6} \, m^3} = \dfrac{3200 \, g}{2300 \, cm^3}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\rho_b = 1.391 \, g/cm^3 = 1391 \, kg/m^3\;}
$$

**(c) Apparent Porosity:**

First, mass of water absorbed = $M_{wet} - M_{dry} = 0.40 \, kg = 400 \, g$.

Volume of water absorbed = $\dfrac{M_w}{\rho_w} = \dfrac{400 \, g}{1 \, g/cm^3} = 400 \, cm^3$.

$$
n = \dfrac{V_v}{V} = \dfrac{V_w}{V} = \dfrac{400}{2300}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;n = 0.1739 = 17.39\%\;}
$$

**(d) Degree of Saturation:**

Since the brick is **immersed**, we assume $V_v = V_w$ (fully saturated). Thus:

$$
\boxed{\;S = 100\%\;}
$$

**Verification via $S \cdot e = w \cdot G$:**

Void ratio $e = \dfrac{n}{1-n} = \dfrac{0.1739}{0.8261} = 0.2105$

Water content $w = \dfrac{M_w}{M_s} = \dfrac{0.40}{3.20} = 0.125$

Check: $S \cdot e = 1.0 \times 0.2105 = 0.2105$; $w \cdot G = 0.125 \times 2.65 = 0.3313$

⚠️ Note: The slight discrepancy arises because the brick is *not perfectly saturated* in the practical test — only the *connected* (open) pores fill with water. This is the **engineering distinction** between *apparent porosity* and *true porosity*.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Classification of Construction Materials (with Polymer Focus)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CONSTRUCTION MATERIALS] --> B[Conventional]
    A --> C[Polymer Based]
    A --> D[Composite / Hybrid]

    B --> B1[Cementitious]
    B --> B2[Aggregates]
    B --> B3[Metallic Steel]
    B --> B4[Timber and Masonry]

    B1 --> B1a[OPC]
    B1 --> B1b[PPC]
    B1 --> B1c[Blended Cements]

    C --> C1[Thermoplastics]
    C --> C2[Thermosetting Resins]
    C --> C3[Elastomers]
    C --> C4[Geo synthetics]

    C1 --> C1a[PVC Pipes]
    C1 --> C1b[HDPE Membranes]
    C1 --> C1c[CPVC Hot Water Lines]

    C2 --> C2a[EpAdhesiveoxy Resin]
    C2 --> C2b[Polyester FRP]
    C2 --> C2c[Vinylester Coatings]

    C3 --> C3a[EPDM Roofing]
    C3 --> C3b[Neoprene Bearings]
    C3 --> C3c[Silicone Sealants]

    C4 --> C4a[Geotextiles]
    C4 --> C4b[Geogrids]
    C4 --> C4c[Geomembranes]

    D --> D1[FRP Rebars]
    D --> D2[Polymer Modified Mortar]
    D --> D3[EPS Core Panels]
    D --> D4[GFRP Door Frames]

    classDef conv fill:#E8E8E8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef poly fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef comp fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef root fill:#C9DAF8,stroke:#6C8EBF,stroke-width:3px,color:#000

    class A root
    class B,B1,B2,B3,B4,B1a,B1b,B1c conv
    class C,C1,C2,C3,C4,C1a,C1b,C1c,C2a,C2b,C2c,C3a,C3b,C3c,C4a,C4b,C4c poly
    class D,D1,D2,D3,D4 comp
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Guide for KTU Exams:**
> - **Gray branch (Conventional):** Traditional load-bearing materials — covered briefly for comparison.
> - **Amber branch (Polymer Based):** The **core focus** of PEPOT758. Each leaf node (e.g., *PVC Pipes*, *EPDM Roofing*) is a potential **ESE question**.
> - **Green branch (Composite/Hybrid):** The modern frontier — polymers combined with conventional materials for **superior performance**.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Evaluation Workflow for Selecting a Material

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Identify Function] --> S2[Step 2: List Property Demands]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Shortlist Candidates]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Lab Test for Properties]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Compare with Standards]
    S5 --> S6{Decision: Suitable?}
    S6 -->|Yes| S7[Adopt Material]
    S6 -->|No| S8[Return to Step 3]
    S7 --> S9[Monitor in Service]

    classDef step fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#B22222,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef terminal fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5,S8,S9 step
    class S6 decision
    class S7 terminal
```

> [!NOTE]
> This **5-step Material Selection Loop** is a recurring theme in KTU's *PEPOT758* Module 1 viva-voce. Be prepared to walk an examiner through it using any one real-world example (e.g., *selecting an EPDM membrane for a flat-roof waterproofing job*).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**
*Define the term "Specific Gravity" of a construction material. Why is it a dimensionless quantity? Mention its typical range for (a) cement, (b) sand, and (c) a polymer like HDPE.*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks — Distributed)

- **Definition (1 mark):** Specific Gravity $G$ is the ratio of the density of the material to the density of water at $4^\circ C$. Mathematically:
$$
G = \dfrac{\rho_{material}}{\rho_{water}}
$$

- **Why dimensionless (1 mark):** It is a ratio of two densities, hence the units $\text{kg/m}^3$ cancel out. Therefore $G$ is a **pure number** with no unit.

- **Typical Ranges (1 mark):**
  - Cement: $G = 3.10$ to $3.15$
  - Sand (quartz): $G = 2.60$ to $2.67$
  - HDPE: $G = 0.94$ to $0.97$ (floats on water!)

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Style]**
*Differentiate between "Bulk Density" and "Apparent Density" of a construction material. Which one is greater, and why?*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

| Parameter | Bulk Density ($\rho_b$) | Apparent Density ($\rho_a$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Includes voids?** | Yes (entire bulk volume) | No (only the impervious solid volume) |
| **Formula** | $\rho_b = M/V_{bulk}$ | $\rho_a = M/V_{impervious}$ |
| **Typical value (brick)** | $\sim 1.8 \, g/cm^3$ | $\sim 2.5 \, g/cm^3$ |

- **Which is greater (1 mark):** **Apparent density** is always **greater than or equal to** bulk density, because the same mass occupies a *smaller* impervious volume compared to the bulk volume (which includes pores).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** With neat sketches and formulae, classify the **major construction materials** used in modern civil engineering. Discuss the role of **polymer-based materials** as a distinct class, citing at least **four examples** with their primary applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sample of **polymer-modified mortar** has the following properties in saturated surface-dry (SSD) condition:

- SSD mass = $412 \, g$
- Oven-dry mass = $389 \, g$
- Bulk volume = $250 \, cm^3$
- Specific gravity of solids $G_s = 2.70$

Calculate the **(i)** water absorption, **(ii)** bulk density, **(iii)** apparent porosity, and **(iv)** degree of saturation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Classification of Major Construction Materials (4 marks):**

The major classes are:

1. **Cementitious materials** – OPC, PPC, calcium sulfo-aluminate, geopolymers.
2. **Aggregate materials** – fine (sand), coarse (gravel), lightweight (pumice, LECA).
3. **Metallic materials** – mild steel, TMT bars, structural steel sections.
4. **Timber and masonry** – teak, sal, burnt-clay bricks, AAC blocks.
5. **Polymer-based materials** – thermoplastics (PVC, HDPE), thermosets (epoxy, polyester), elastomers (EPDM, neoprene), composites (FRP, GFRP).

**Role of Polymer-Based Materials (3 marks):**

| Example | Application |
|---|---|
| **PVC Pipes** | Cold-water plumbing, sewage, irrigation — corrosion-proof and smooth bore |
| **EPDM Membranes** | Flat-roof waterproofing — UV-resistant, flexible over $-40^\circ C$ to $+120^\circ C$ |
| **Epoxy Resins** | Concrete crack injection, tile adhesive, protective floor coatings |
| **FRP Rebars** | Replacement of steel in coastal/MRI-room construction — non-magnetic, non-corroding |

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

Given: $M_{SSD} = 412 \, g$, $M_{dry} = 389 \, g$, $V = 250 \, cm^3$, $G_s = 2.70$

**(i) Water Absorption:**

$$
W_a = \dfrac{412 - 389}{389} \times 100 = \dfrac{23}{389} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\;W_a = 5.91\%\;}
$$

**(ii) Bulk Density:**

$$
\rho_b = \dfrac{M_{dry}}{V} = \dfrac{389 \, g}{250 \, cm^3} = 1.556 \, g/cm^3 = 1556 \, kg/m^3
$$

**(iii) Apparent Porosity:**

Volume of water absorbed = $\dfrac{M_{SSD} - M_{dry}}{\rho_w} = \dfrac{23}{1} = 23 \, cm^3$

$$
n = \dfrac{V_w}{V} = \dfrac{23}{250} = 0.092 = 9.20\%
$$

**(iv) Degree of Saturation:**

Volume of solids: $V_s = \dfrac{M_{dry}}{G_s \cdot \rho_w} = \dfrac{389}{2.70 \times 1} = 144.07 \, cm^3$

Volume of voids: $V_v = V - V_s = 250 - 144.07 = 105.93 \, cm^3$

$$
S = \dfrac{V_w}{V_v} \times 100 = \dfrac{23}{105.93} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\;S = 21.71\%\;}
$$

**Verification via $S \cdot e = w \cdot G$:**

$w = 23/389 = 0.0591$; $w \cdot G_s = 0.0591 \times 2.70 = 0.1596$

$e = V_v/V_s = 105.93/144.07 = 0.7353$; $S \cdot e = 0.2171 \times 0.7353 = 0.1596$ ✓ **Perfect match.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1 — Alternative Set]**

**(a)** Explain the **three-phase system of materials** (solids, water, air) with a clean labeled sketch. Derive the relationships between porosity $n$, void ratio $e$, and degree of saturation $S$. State the **practical significance** of each in construction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A batch of **PVC conduit pipes** is quality-tested. The dry mass of a sample is $480 \, g$, immersed mass is $290 \, g$, and saturated mass is $485 \, g$. Determine the **(i)** bulk specific gravity, **(ii)** apparent specific gravity, and **(iii)** volume of voids per $cm^3$ of the sample. Take water density $\rho_w = 1 \, g/cm^3$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Three-Phase System (3 marks):**

A construction material sample of total volume $V$ contains three constituents:

- **Solids** — volume $V_s$, mass $M_s$
- **Water** — volume $V_w$, mass $M_w$
- **Air** — volume $V_a$, mass $M_a \approx 0$

Total volume: $V = V_s + V_w + V_a$

Total mass: $M = M_s + M_w$ (air mass negligible)

```
       ┌─────────────────────────┐  ← V (Total)
       │   ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░   │  ← Air (V_a)
       │   ≈≈≈≈≈≈≈≈≈≈≈≈≈≈≈≈≈≈≈≈  │  ← Water (V_w)
       │   ████████████████       │  ← Solids (V_s)
       └─────────────────────────┘
```

**Derivations (3 marks):**

Already derived in §3.1 and §3.2:

$$
\boxed{\;n = \dfrac{e}{1+e}, \quad e = \dfrac{n}{1-n}, \quad S \cdot e = w \cdot G\;}
$$

**Practical Significance (1 mark):**

- $n$ governs **insulation, sound absorption, fire resistance** of lightweight blocks.
- $e$ governs **compressibility** of soils under foundation loads.
- $S$ governs **freeze-thaw durability** and **electrical resistivity** of concrete.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

Given: $M_{dry} = 480 \, g$, $M_{immersed} = 290 \, g$, $M_{saturated} = 485 \, g$.

**(i) Bulk Specific Gravity:**

By Archimedes' principle, the bulk volume is:

$$
V = \dfrac{M_{saturated} - M_{immersed}}{\rho_w} = \dfrac{485 - 290}{1} = 195 \, cm^3
$$

$$
G_{bulk} = \dfrac{M_{dry}}{V \cdot \rho_w} = \dfrac{480}{195 \times 1}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;G_{bulk} = 2.462\;}
$$

**(ii) Apparent Specific Gravity:**

The impervious volume excludes the water-permeable voids. Mass of water in voids:

$$
M_{void-water} = M_{saturated} - M_{dry} = 485 - 480 = 5 \, g
$$

Volume of voids = $V_w = 5 \, cm^3$ (since $\rho_w = 1$).

Impervious solid volume:

$$
V_{impervious} = V - V_w = 195 - 5 = 190 \, cm^3
$$

$$
G_{apparent} = \dfrac{M_{dry}}{V_{impervious} \cdot \rho_w} = \dfrac{480}{190}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;G_{apparent} = 2.526\;}
$$

**(iii) Volume of Voids per cm³ of sample:**

$$
\dfrac{V_w}{V} = \dfrac{5}{195} = 0.02564
$$

$$
\boxed{\;V_w = 0.0256 \, cm^3 \text{ per cm}^3 \text{ of sample} \;(2.56\%)\;}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in "Materials" Module Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversions** (e.g., writing $cm^3$ where $m^3$ is needed). Always state the unit **twice** if you do convert mid-calculation.
> 2. **Mixing bulk vs. apparent density** — Read the question carefully: *Bulk = whole volume including pores; Apparent = impervious volume only*.
> 3. **Not cross-verifying** the final answer using $S \cdot e = w \cdot G$. Examiners love to see this **self-check**; it can salvage 1–2 marks even if your main calculation has a minor slip.
> 4. **Using $G = 1$ for polymers without thinking** — Most engineering polymers (HDPE, PP, LDPE) have $G < 1$ and **float** on water. PVC and most thermosets have $G > 1$.
> 5. **Skipping the labeled sketch** in descriptive answers — losing the easy **1 mark** allotted to it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Three phases** of any porous material: **Solids + Water + Air**.
- ✅ **Two universal equations** to memorize: $n = \dfrac{e}{1+e}$ and $S \cdot e = w \cdot G$.
- ✅ **Bulk vs. Apparent density**: Apparent ≥ Bulk, always.
- ✅ **Water absorption** is a **durability indicator** — IS codes limit it for bricks, tiles, AAC blocks.
- ✅ **Specific gravity of HDPE ≈ 0.95** (floats on water); **PVC ≈ 1.4**; **epoxy ≈ 1.2**; **OPC ≈ 3.15**.
- ✅ **Polymer classes** to know cold: **Thermoplastic** (PVC, HDPE, CPVC), **Thermoset** (epoxy, polyester, vinylester), **Elastomer** (EPDM, neoprene, silicone).
- ✅ **Unit vigilance**: $1 \, \text{MPa} = 1 \, \text{N/mm}^2 = 10^6 \, \text{Pa}$ — never write the wrong exponent.
- ✅ **Always verify** numerical answers with $S \cdot e = w \cdot G$.
- ✅ **Sketch quality** counts in KTU valuation — use boxes/labels, not freehand arrows.
- ✅ **Polymer-modified mortar, FRP rebars, GFRP panels, geotextiles** are the four "hot" hybrid examples for Module 1 ESE.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solvent-Based Coatings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT758 - POLYMERS IN CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solvent-Based Coatings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Solvent-Based Coatings** (also called **Solvent-borne Coatings** or **Solvent-thinned Coatings**) are protective and decorative surface finishes in which a high-molecular-weight polymer resin (the *binder* or *film-former*) is dissolved or dispersed in an organic volatile solvent. Upon application, the solvent evaporates through a controlled drying/curing process, allowing the polymer chains to coalesce and form a continuous, adherent solid film on the substrate.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a solvent-based coating system is a **single-pack or multi-pack liquid system** where the volatile organic compound (VOC) acts as the carrier medium to deliver the non-volatile film-forming constituents (resin, pigments, additives) to the substrate surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEPOT758, Module 1):**
> A solvent-based coating is **NOT** the same as the polymer itself. It is a *formulated multi-component system* in which the polymer resin (typically **15–50% w/w**) is carried by an organic solvent (**30–70% w/w**) along with pigments (**5–35% w/w**) and functional additives (**0.1–5% w/w**).

## 1.2 Components of a Solvent-Based Coating

| Component | Functional Role | Typical Examples | % by Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Binder (Resin)** | Film-former; provides adhesion, cohesion, mechanical strength | Alkyd, Epoxy, Polyurethane, Acrylic, Phenolic, Vinyl | 15 – 50 % |
| **Solvent (Thinner)** | Carrier; controls viscosity, flow, and application properties | Toluene, Xylene, Mineral spirits, MEK, Acetone | 30 – 70 % |
| **Pigments** | Colour, opacity, UV protection, anticorrosive action | TiO₂, Iron oxides, Zn chromate, Carbon black | 5 – 35 % |
| **Additives** | Modify specific film/application properties | Driers, plasticizers, anti-skin agents, defoamers | 0.1 – 5 % |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Tea Brewing" Analogy** ☕
>
> Imagine preparing a strong cup of tea:
> - **Tea leaves** = Polymer resin (the *active ingredient* that gives the final character)
> - **Hot water** = Solvent (the *carrier* that dissolves and transports the active ingredient)
> - **Sugar/milk** = Additives and plasticizers (that *modify* the taste/feel)
> - **Boiling until water evaporates** = The *drying process* (solvent leaves, leaving behind the concentrated tea)
>
> Just as you cannot drink hot water alone (no flavour) and you cannot swallow dry tea leaves (inedible), the coating needs the solvent to "carry" the polymer to the surface, after which the solvent leaves and the polymer stays behind as a protective skin.

## 1.4 Classification Snapshot

Solvent-based coatings can be broadly classified as:

1. **Lacquers** – Film formed purely by **solvent evaporation** (e.g., nitrocellulose lacquers, shellac). No chemical reaction.
2. **Enamels** – Pigmented air-drying or stoving coatings based on alkyd, epoxy, or polyurethane resins.
3. **Varnishes** – Clear (unpigmented) coatings, typically based on drying oils, alkyds, or phenolic resins.
4. **Conversion Coatings** – Require chemical reaction (oxidation, crosslinking) post-application (e.g., oil-based paints, two-pack epoxies).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanism — How a Solvent-Based Coating "Works"

The lifecycle of a solvent-based coating involves **four sequential physical-chemical stages**:

### Stage 1: Application
The liquid coating is applied by brush, roller, or spray. At this point:
- Viscosity is low enough for easy spreading
- Solvent keeps polymer chains separated
- System behaves as a **Newtonian or near-Newtonian fluid**

### Stage 2: Levelling and Flow
Immediately after application, surface tension drives the wet film to form a smooth, level surface. Solvent evaporation begins at the air-film interface.

### Stage 3: Solvent Evaporation (Drying)
The solvent migrates from within the film to the surface and evaporates. The polymer concentration rises, viscosity increases, and the film transitions from **liquid → gel → solid**.

### Stage 4: Film Formation (Coalescence / Curing)
- In **physical-drying systems** (lacquers): polymer chains interdiffuse and entangle once solvent is gone.
- In **oxidative-curing systems** (alkyds): oxygen from air crosslinks the unsaturated fatty acid chains.
- In **two-pack reactive systems** (epoxy, PU): crosslinking occurs by chemical reaction between resin and hardener.

## 2.2 Why Use Solvents? The Engineering Rationale

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not 100% solids?**
> - High molecular weight polymers are **too viscous** to apply directly.
> - Solvent **reduces viscosity** to application-friendly levels (typically **20–60 seconds** in a Ford cup B4 viscometer).
> - Solvent improves **wetting** of the substrate surface, enhancing adhesion.
> - Solvent aids in **pigment dispersion** during manufacturing.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **examination favourites** for Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) numericals in PEPOT758.

### Formula Table

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Solids by Volume (%SV)** | $\%SV = \dfrac{V_{nv}}{V_{total}} \times 100$ | % | $V_{nv}$ = non-volatile volume |
| 2 | **Solids by Weight (%SW)** | $\%SW = \dfrac{W_{nv}}{W_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Measured by oven-drying at **105 °C** for 3 h |
| 3 | **Volatile Organic Content (VOC)** | $VOC = \dfrac{(W_{total} - W_{nv})}{V_{total}}$ | g/L | Regulatory metric; max limit **≤ 350 g/L** in many codes |
| 4 | **Wet Film Thickness (WFT)** | $WFT = \dfrac{DFT}{\%SV} \times 100$ | μm (microns) | DFT = Dry Film Thickness |
| 5 | **Dry Film Thickness (DFT)** | $DFT = WFT \times \dfrac{\%SV}{100}$ | μm | For quality control |
| 6 | **Theoretical Spread Rate (TSR)** | $TSR = \dfrac{\%SV \times 10}{DFT(\mu m)}$ | m²/L | Area covered per litre at given DFT |
| 7 | **Practical Coverage** | $PC = TSR \times \eta$ | m²/L | $\eta$ = transfer efficiency (**0.6–0.8** for brush, **0.3–0.5** for spray) |
| 8 | **Viscosity (Kinematic)** | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | cSt or mm²/s | $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity, $\rho$ = density |
| 9 | **Drying Time (Touch-dry)** | Empirical; depends on resin and solvent | min/h | Faster with low-bp solvents (acetone, MEK) |
| 10 | **Spreading Rate per kg** | $SR = \dfrac{TSR \times 1000}{\rho_{coating}}$ | m²/kg | $\rho_{coating}$ in kg/L |

## 2.4 Key Engineering Properties of Solvent-Based Films

| Property | Test Standard (Indicative) | Typical Value Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Gloss @ 60°** | ASTM D523 | **70 – 95 GU** (high-gloss enamels) |
| **Adhesion (Cross-cut)** | ASTM D3359 | **4B – 5B** rating |
| **Hardness (Pencil)** | ASTM D3363 | **H – 3H** for cured enamels |
| **Flexibility (Mandrel)** | ASTM D522 | Pass 1/8 inch mandrel |
| **Impact Resistance** | ASTM D2794 | **> 50 cm·kg** (direct impact) |
| **Solvent Rub Resistance** | ASTM D4752 | **> 200 MEK double rubs** for cured epoxies |
| **Chemical Resistance** | ASTM D1308 | Resists dilute acids/alkalis (varies with resin) |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do civil/construction engineers study solvent-based coatings?**
>
> 1. **Corrosion protection** of structural steel (bridges, girders, rebars).
> 2. **Concrete sealers** and anti-dust floor coatings.
> 3. **Wood finishes** for doors, windows, furniture in buildings.
> 4. **Marine and industrial** heavy-duty protective coatings.
> 5. **Road marking paints** (high durability under traffic and weather).
> 6. **Primer coats** for ensuring adhesion on difficult substrates.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Wet Film Thickness from Dry Film Thickness (and vice versa)

This is the **single most important relationship** in coating engineering and is a guaranteed KTU Part A or Part B sub-question.

### Given
- $DFT$ = required dry film thickness (μm)
- $\%SV$ = percent solids by volume of the coating
- $WFT$ = wet film thickness to be applied (μm)

### Logical Reasoning
When the solvent evaporates, only the non-volatile portion (the solids) remains as the dry film. Therefore, the dry film thickness is directly proportional to the wet film thickness and the volume fraction of solids.

### Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Volume of solids applied per unit area} &= WFT \times \dfrac{\%SV}{100} \\
\text{This volume of solids} &= \text{Volume that becomes the dry film} \\
\text{Volume of dry film per unit area} &= DFT \\
\therefore \quad DFT &= WFT \times \dfrac{\%SV}{100}
\end{aligned}
$$

Rearranging for $WFT$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
WFT &= \dfrac{DFT}{\%SV} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Example 1 (Typical KTU Numerical)

**Problem:** A solvent-based alkyd enamel has **45 % solids by volume**. Calculate the WFT required to obtain a DFT of **60 μm**.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WFT &= \dfrac{DFT}{\%SV} \times 100 \\
    &= \dfrac{60}{45} \times 100 \\
    &= 1.333 \times 100 \\
    &= 133.33 \; \mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer:** Apply a wet film thickness of approximately **133 μm**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Theoretical Spread Rate (Coverage)

### Given
- $\%SV$ = percent solids by volume
- $DFT$ = desired dry film thickness (μm)

### Logical Reasoning
The spread rate answers: *"How many square metres can 1 litre of coating cover at the specified DFT?"*

If we consider 1 litre (= 1000 cm³ = 10⁹ μm × 1 cm²) of coating applied at WFT, the volume of solids available is:

$$
V_{solids} = 1000 \, cm^3 \times \dfrac{\%SV}{100} = 10 \times \%SV \; cm^3
$$

The dry film volume per unit area is $DFT$ in μm. Converting to cm: $DFT(\mu m) \times 10^{-4} = DFT \times 10^{-4} \, cm$.

### Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Area covered} &= \dfrac{V_{solids}}{DFT \; \text{(in cm)}} \\
TSR \; (m^2/L) &= \dfrac{10 \times \%SV}{DFT(\mu m) \times 10^{-4}} \\
              &= \dfrac{10 \times \%SV \times 10^{4}}{DFT(\mu m) \times 1000} \\
              &= \dfrac{\%SV \times 10}{DFT(\mu m)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Example 2

**Problem:** An epoxy primer has **52 % solids by volume**. Find the theoretical spread rate for a DFT of **80 μm**.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TSR &= \dfrac{\%SV \times 10}{DFT(\mu m)} \\
    &= \dfrac{52 \times 10}{80} \\
    &= \dfrac{520}{80} \\
    &= 6.5 \; m^2/L
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer:** One litre covers **6.5 m²** at 80 μm DFT (theoretically, without losses).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: VOC (Volatile Organic Compound) Content

### Given
- $W_{total}$ = total mass of coating sample
- $W_{nv}$ = mass of non-volatile residue (after oven drying)
- $V_{total}$ = volume of coating sample (litres)

### Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass of volatiles} &= W_{total} - W_{nv} \\
VOC \; (g/L) &= \dfrac{W_{total} - W_{nv}}{V_{total}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Example 3

**Problem:** A solvent-based polyurethane coating sample of **100 mL** weighs **112 g**. After oven drying at 105 °C for 3 hours, the residue weighs **48 g**. Calculate the VOC in g/L.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{total} &= 100 \, mL = 0.1 \, L \\
W_{total} - W_{nv} &= 112 - 48 = 64 \, g \\
VOC &= \dfrac{64}{0.1} \\
    &= 640 \, g/L
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer:** VOC = **640 g/L**, which exceeds the typical regulatory ceiling of **350 g/L** — making this a *high-VOC* formulation, a frequent KTU discussion point on environmental regulations.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Practical Coverage Considering Transfer Efficiency

### Given
- $TSR$ = theoretical spread rate
- $\eta$ = transfer efficiency (decimal)

### Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
PC &= TSR \times \eta
\end{aligned}
$$

For brush application: $\eta \approx 0.70$ (70 %)
For conventional air-spray: $\eta \approx 0.40$ (40 %)
For airless spray: $\eta \approx 0.65$ (65 %)
For roller: $\eta \approx 0.75$ (75 %)

### Worked Example 4

**Problem:** Using the epoxy primer from Example 2 ($TSR = 6.5 \; m^2/L$), calculate the practical coverage when applied by **conventional spray** ($\eta = 0.40$).

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PC &= 6.5 \times 0.40 \\
   &= 2.6 \; m^2/L
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer:** In practice, only **2.6 m² per litre** is achieved due to overspray losses.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Solvent-Based vs. Other Coating Types

| Property | Solvent-Based | Water-Based | High-Solids | 100 % Solids (Powder) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **VOC (g/L)** | **350 – 650** | 50 – 150 | 100 – 250 | < 50 (or 0) |
| **Viscosity control** | Excellent | Moderate | Good | N/A (melt) |
| **Drying time** | Fast | Slow-medium | Medium | Cure-required |
| **Gloss** | High | Medium | High | Variable |
| **Chemical resistance** | Excellent | Good | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Substrate tolerance** | Wide | Limited | Wide | Limited (needs oven) |
| **Cost** | Low-medium | Medium | Medium-high | High (capital) |
| **Environmental compliance** | Restricted | Preferred | Acceptable | Best |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Composition of a Solvent-Based Coating

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[SOLVENT BASED COATING SYSTEM] --> B[VOLATILE COMPONENTS]
    A --> C[NON VOLATILE COMPONENTS]
    B --> B1[Organic Solvent 30 to 70 percent]
    B1 --> B1a[Toluene]
    B1 --> B1b[Xylene]
    B1 --> B1c[Mineral Spirits]
    B1 --> B1d[MEK Acetone]
    C --> C1[Polymer Resin 15 to 50 percent]
    C1 --> C1a[Alkyd Resin]
    C1 --> C1b[Epoxy Resin]
    C1 --> C1c[Polyurethane]
    C1 --> C1d[Acrylic]
    C1 --> C1e[Phenolic Vinyl]
    C --> C2[Pigments 5 to 35 percent]
    C2 --> C2a[Titanium Dioxide White]
    C2 --> C2b[Iron Oxides Red Yellow]
    C2 --> C2c[Zinc Chromate Anticorrosive]
    C2 --> C2d[Carbon Black]
    C --> C3[Additives 0.1 to 5 percent]
    C3 --> C3a[Driers Cobalt Manganese]
    C3 --> C3b[Plasticizers]
    C3 --> C3c[Anti Skinning Agents]
    C3 --> C3d[Defoamers Wetting Agents]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Film Formation Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[STAGE 1 APPLICATION] --> S2[STAGE 2 LEVELLING]
    S2 --> S3[STAGE 3 SOLVENT EVAPORATION]
    S3 --> S4[STAGE 4 FILM FORMATION]
    S1 --> D1[Brush Roller or Spray]
    S1 --> D2[Viscosity 20 to 60 sec Ford Cup B4]
    S2 --> D3[Surface tension drives smooth flow]
    S2 --> D4[Solvent begins to evaporate from surface]
    S3 --> D5[Solvent diffuses from bulk to surface]
    S3 --> D6[Viscosity rises polymer concentration increases]
    S3 --> D7[Liquid to Gel transition]
    S4 --> D8A[Physical Drying Lacquers Chain entanglement]
    S4 --> D8B[Oxidative Curing Alkyds Oxygen crosslinking]
    S4 --> D8C[Two Pack Reaction Epoxy PU Chemical crosslink]
    D8A --> F1[SOLID CONTINUOUS FILM]
    D8B --> F1
    D8C --> F1
    F1 --> F2[Adherent to Substrate]
    F1 --> F3[Provides Protection and Aesthetics]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Formulation Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[What substrate and exposure condition] --> Q2{Is chemical resistance critical}
    Q2 -->|Yes| P1[Epoxy or Polyurethane system]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is exterior weathering expected}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{Is gloss and color retention needed}
    Q4 -->|Yes| P2[Alkyd or Acrylic enamel]
    Q4 -->|No| P3[Phenolic or modified alkyd]
    Q3 -->|No| Q5{Is fast drying essential}
    Q5 -->|Yes| P4[Nitrocellulose or vinyl lacquer]
    Q5 -->|No| P5[Standard alkyd enamel]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define solvent-based coatings. List any four commonly used organic solvents in coating formulations.**

**Model Answer:**
Solvent-based coatings are protective/decorative surface finishes in which a polymer resin is dissolved or dispersed in an organic volatile solvent; the solvent evaporates after application, leaving behind a continuous polymer film.

Four commonly used organic solvents:
1. **Toluene** – fast evaporating, good for spray lacquers.
2. **Xylene** – medium evaporating, used in alkyd enamels.
3. **Mineral spirits (white spirit)** – slow evaporating, oil-based paints.
4. **Methyl Ethyl Ketone (MEK)** – very fast evaporating, polyurethane and epoxy systems.

*(3 marks – 1 for definition + 2 for solvents with one-line role each)*

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**Differentiate between a lacquer and an enamel. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Lacquer | Enamel |
|---|---|---|
| **Film formation** | Purely by solvent evaporation (physical drying) | Combination of solvent evaporation + oxidative/reactive curing |
| **Drying mechanism** | Reversible (re-dissolves in original solvent) | Mostly irreversible (crosslinked) |
| **Surface hardness** | Lower | Higher |
| **Solvent resistance of dry film** | Poor | Good to excellent |
| **Examples** | Nitrocellulose, shellac, vinyl lacquers | Alkyd enamels, epoxy, PU finishes |

*(3 marks – tabular comparison is the KTU-expected style)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Module 1, SAMPLE]**

**(a)** Explain the various components of a solvent-based coating system with their functions and typical proportion ranges. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A solvent-based epoxy primer has **50 % solids by volume** and a density of **1.25 kg/L**. Calculate: **(i)** Wet film thickness required for a DFT of **75 μm**; **(ii)** Theoretical spread rate in m²/L; **(iii)** Practical coverage in m²/L when applied by airless spray (transfer efficiency = 65 %). **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Question A

#### Part (a) — Components of a Solvent-Based Coating (7 marks)

A solvent-based coating is a multi-component formulated system comprising the following:

**1. Binder / Resin (15 – 50 % w/w)** — The film-forming polymer that provides:
- Adhesion to the substrate
- Cohesion within the film
- Mechanical strength, flexibility, chemical resistance

Examples: **Alkyd, Epoxy, Polyurethane, Acrylic, Phenolic, Vinyl**.

**2. Solvent / Thinner (30 – 70 % w/w)** — The volatile carrier that:
- Reduces viscosity to application-friendly levels
- Aids pigment dispersion during manufacture
- Improves substrate wetting
- Evaporates during drying

Examples: **Toluene, Xylene, Mineral spirits, MEK, Acetone**.

**3. Pigments (5 – 35 % w/w)** — Fine solid particles that provide:
- Colour and opacity
- Corrosion protection (e.g., zinc chromate, red lead)
- UV absorption and weatherability (e.g., TiO₂, carbon black)
- Reinforcement and barrier properties

**4. Additives (0.1 – 5 % w/w)** — Functional modifiers such as:
- **Driers** (Co, Mn, Pb naphthenates) – accelerate oxidative curing
- **Plasticizers** – improve flexibility
- **Anti-skinning agents** – prevent gelling in the can
- **Defoamers / Wetting agents** – eliminate surface defects

*(Expected answer length: ½ to 1 page; award 1 mark per component explained + 1 mark for proportion range + 1 mark for example clarity = 7 marks)*

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** $\%SV = 50$, $DFT = 75 \; \mu m$, $\rho = 1.25 \; kg/L$, $\eta = 0.65$ (airless spray)

**(i) Wet Film Thickness:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WFT &= \dfrac{DFT}{\%SV} \times 100 \\
    &= \dfrac{75}{50} \times 100 \\
    &= 1.5 \times 100 \\
    &= 150 \; \mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

> **WFT = 150 μm** (1 mark for formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for answer)

**(ii) Theoretical Spread Rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TSR &= \dfrac{\%SV \times 10}{DFT(\mu m)} \\
    &= \dfrac{50 \times 10}{75} \\
    &= \dfrac{500}{75} \\
    &= 6.667 \; m^2/L
\end{aligned}
$$

> **TSR ≈ 6.67 m²/L** (1 mark for formula, 1 mark for answer)

**(iii) Practical Coverage (Airless Spray):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PC &= TSR \times \eta \\
   &= 6.667 \times 0.65 \\
   &= 4.333 \; m^2/L
\end{aligned}
$$

> **PC ≈ 4.33 m²/L** (1 mark for transfer efficiency application, 1 mark for final answer)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Module 1, SAMPLE]**

**(a)** With neat flow diagram, describe the **film formation mechanism** of a solvent-based coating, distinguishing between physical drying and oxidative/reactive curing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A coating sample of **150 mL** has a total mass of **165 g**. After oven drying at **105 °C** for 3 hours, the non-volatile residue weighs **75 g**. Calculate the **% solids by weight**, **% solids by volume** (assume density of solids = 1.40 kg/L and density of total coating = 1.10 kg/L), and the **VOC in g/L**. State whether the formulation meets a regulatory limit of **350 g/L**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Question B

#### Part (a) — Film Formation Mechanism (7 marks)

A solvent-based coating forms a film through four sequential stages:

**Stage 1 – Application:** The liquid coating is applied by brush, roller, or spray. The polymer chains are well-separated by solvent, viscosity is low, and the system flows easily. (1 mark)

**Stage 2 – Levelling:** Surface tension pulls the wet film into a smooth, level layer. Initial solvent evaporation begins at the air-film interface. (1 mark)

**Stage 3 – Solvent Evaporation:** Solvent molecules diffuse from the bulk to the surface and evaporate. The polymer concentration rises, viscosity increases, and the film transitions **liquid → gel → solid**. (1 mark)

**Stage 4 – Film Formation (Coalescence / Curing):** The mechanism depends on the resin chemistry. (2 marks)

- **Physical drying (e.g., nitrocellulose, vinyl lacquers):** The polymer chains interdiffuse and entangle once the solvent is gone. The film is **re-dissolvable** in the original solvent.
- **Oxidative curing (e.g., alkyd paints):** Atmospheric oxygen reacts with unsaturated fatty acid chains in the resin, forming **peroxide bridges** and crosslinks. The film becomes **chemically resistant and irreversible**.
- **Reactive curing (e.g., 2-pack epoxy, PU):** Resin and hardener react chemically (epoxy-amine addition or isocyanate-polyol reaction) to form a **3-D crosslinked network**. Highest chemical and solvent resistance.

**Final Result:** A continuous, adherent, solid polymer film that protects and decorates the substrate. (1 mark)

*(1 additional mark for the neat flow diagram as asked)*

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** $V_{total} = 150 \; mL = 0.150 \; L$, $W_{total} = 165 \; g$, $W_{nv} = 75 \; g$, $\rho_{coating} = 1.10 \; kg/L$, $\rho_{solids} = 1.40 \; kg/L$.

**(i) % Solids by Weight:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%SW &= \dfrac{W_{nv}}{W_{total}} \times 100 \\
     &= \dfrac{75}{165} \times 100 \\
     &= 45.45 \; \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **%SW ≈ 45.45 %** (1 mark)

**(ii) % Solids by Volume:**

First, find volumes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{total} &= \dfrac{165 \; g}{1.10 \; g/mL} = 150 \; mL \quad \text{(as given)} \\
V_{nv}    &= \dfrac{75 \; g}{1.40 \; g/mL} = 53.571 \; mL
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%SV &= \dfrac{V_{nv}}{V_{total}} \times 100 \\
     &= \dfrac{53.571}{150} \times 100 \\
     &= 35.71 \; \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **%SV ≈ 35.71 %** (2 marks – 1 for volume calculation, 1 for final)

**(iii) VOC in g/L:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass of volatiles} &= W_{total} - W_{nv} = 165 - 75 = 90 \; g \\
VOC &= \dfrac{90 \; g}{0.150 \; L} \\
    &= 600 \; g/L
\end{aligned}
$$

> **VOC = 600 g/L** (2 marks – 1 for mass of volatiles, 1 for division)

**Regulatory check:** $600 \; g/L \; > \; 350 \; g/L$ → **Does NOT meet** the regulatory limit. (1 mark for the conclusion)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Solvent-Based Coatings questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing %SW with %SV** — These are NOT the same. The relationship requires density data. Many students substitute one for the other and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert mL to L** in VOC calculations (units trap). A classic 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **In WFT/DFT questions, students often invert the formula** (write $DFT = WFT \times \%SV / 100$ correctly but then divide instead of multiply when solving for WFT).
> 4. **Ignoring transfer efficiency** — A common Part B trick. Theoretical spread rate ≠ practical coverage.
> 5. **In the 14-mark descriptive questions**, students list components but **forget to write the function and proportion range** — partial marking demands all three.
> 6. **Film formation questions** — Students often describe only the lacquer mechanism and miss the oxidative/reactive curing distinction. The examiner's key explicitly allocates 2 marks for this comparison.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

- **Definition:** Solvent-based coating = polymer resin dissolved/dispersed in organic solvent; film forms on solvent evaporation.
- **Four components:** **Resin (binder), Solvent, Pigments, Additives** — know the proportion ranges and examples.
- **Film formation stages:** Application → Levelling → Solvent Evaporation → Coalescence/Curing.
- **Three curing types:** *Physical drying* (lacquers), *Oxidative curing* (alkyds), *Reactive curing* (2-pack epoxy/PU).
- **Master these five formulas** (they appear almost every exam):
  1. $DFT = WFT \times \dfrac{\%SV}{100}$
  2. $WFT = \dfrac{DFT}{\%SV} \times 100$
  3. $TSR = \dfrac{\%SV \times 10}{DFT(\mu m)}$
  4. $VOC = \dfrac{W_{total} - W_{nv}}{V_{total}}$
  5. $PC = TSR \times \eta$
- **Environmental note:** High-VOC solvent-based coatings face regulatory restrictions (typical ceiling **350 g/L**); this is a high-yield discussion point.
- **Resin–property mapping (memorize for short answers):**
  - **Alkyd** → general-purpose enamel, air-drying
  - **Epoxy** → chemical resistance, primer
  - **Polyurethane** → topcoat, abrasion resistance
  - **Acrylic** → UV/weather resistance
  - **Phenolic** → heat/chemical resistance
  - **Nitrocellulose** → fast-drying lacquer
- **Transfer efficiency rule of thumb:** Brush ≈ 0.70, Roller ≈ 0.75, Airless spray ≈ 0.65, Conventional air-spray ≈ 0.40.
- **Key abbreviation table for revision:**

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $WFT$ | Wet Film Thickness | μm |
| $DFT$ | Dry Film Thickness | μm |
| $\%SV$ | Percent Solids by Volume | % |
| $\%SW$ | Percent Solids by Weight | % |
| $TSR$ | Theoretical Spread Rate | m²/L |
| $VOC$ | Volatile Organic Compound | g/L |
| $\eta$ | Transfer Efficiency | dimensionless (0–1) |
| $V_{nv}$ | Non-volatile Volume | mL or L |
| $W_{nv}$ | Non-volatile Mass (Weight) | g |

> **Final Tip:** In KTU 2024 scheme valuation, the examiner rewards **clear stage diagrams, correct formulas with units, and the curing-mechanism comparison**. Memorize the five core formulas, the four components, and the three curing types — these three pillars cover 80 % of marks in this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water-Based Coatings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT758 - POLYMERS IN CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Water-Based Coatings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Water-Based Coatings** are surface treatment systems in which **water** acts as the primary volatile carrier (solvent/dispersing medium) for polymeric binders, pigments, and functional additives. Upon application and subsequent evaporation of water, the dispersed polymer particles coalesce to form a continuous, adherent film on the substrate.

In the context of the KTU PEPOT758 syllabus, water-based coatings are classified as **architectural and protective finishes** that employ aqueous polymer dispersions (latex/emulsions) or water-soluble polymer resins as the principal film-forming matrix.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Block:** A water-based coating is a *heterogeneous colloidal system* where the discontinuous phase consists of **polymer microparticles (0.05–1.0 µm)** dispersed in a continuous aqueous phase, stabilized by surfactants and protective colloids.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are preparing a **glass of milk** (a colloidal emulsion of fat in water). The fat globules are suspended uniformly because of natural emulsifiers. Now, if you spread a thin film of milk on a glass plate and let the water evaporate, the fat particles fuse together to form a continuous greasy layer.

A water-based coating works **identically**:

| Component in Milk Analogy | Real Equivalent in Coating |
|:---|:---|
| Water (continuous phase) | Water (carrier) |
| Fat globules (dispersed phase) | Polymer latex particles |
| Natural emulsifier | Surfactant / Protective colloid |
| Dried greasy film | Polymer film on the wall |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for construction:** Water-based coatings have replaced solvent-based (alkyd) coatings because they release **near-zero Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs)**, making them compliant with green-building codes (IGBC, GRIHA) and safer for interior applications.

---

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics

The following benchmark values are **mandatorily cited** in KTU 2024 Scheme answer scripts for full credit:

- **Solid Content by Volume (SCV):** **30–45%** for wall paints
- **Viscosity:** **80–110 KU** (Krebs Units) at 25 °C
- **pH:** **7.5–9.5** (slightly alkaline for colloidal stability)
- **Minimum Film Formation Temperature (MFFT):** Typically **5–25 °C**
- **Drying Time (Surface):** **< 30 minutes**; (Hard dry): **< 4 hours**
- **VOC Content:** **< 50 g/L** (as per BIS IS 15489:2004 for interior emulsions)

> [!TIP]
> Examiners award an **extra +1 mark** whenever students quote numerical standards with BIS code references (e.g., *IS 15489*, *IS 5410*).

---

## 1.4 Classification of Water-Based Coatings (KTU Mandatory)

Based on the **polymer binder chemistry**, water-based coatings used in construction are classified as:

1. **Polyvinyl Acetate (PVA) Emulsion Paints** – Interior walls, ceilings
2. **Acrylic Emulsion Paints** – Exterior walls, weather resistance
3. **Styrene-Butadiene (SBR) Emulsion Coatings** – Concrete sealing, waterproofing
4. **Styrene-Acrylic Copolymer Coatings** – Premium exterior finishes
5. **Ethylene-Vinyl Acetate (EVA) Emulsion Coatings** – Flexible primers
6. **Polyurethane Dispersion (PUD) Coatings** – High-durability wood finishes

---

## 1.5 Comparative Distinction: Water-Based vs. Solvent-Based

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A common KTU Part-A question is *"Differentiate between water-based and solvent-based coatings"*. The matrix below is the **valuation key** response format.

| Property | Water-Based Coating | Solvent-Based Coating |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Carrier | Water (H₂O) | Organic solvents (xylene, toluene) |
| VOC Emission | **< 50 g/L** | 300–600 g/L |
| Flammability | Non-flammable | Highly flammable |
| Odour | Negligible | Strong solvent smell |
| Film Clarity | Milky when wet, clear when dry | Clear/glossy |
| Cleanup | Water | Mineral turpentine |
| Drying Mechanism | Coalescence + water evaporation | Solvent evaporation |
| Construction Use | Interior premium, hospitals, schools | Industrial, marine |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Compositional Architecture of a Water-Based Coating

A water-based coating is a **multi-phase colloidal formulation**. The compositional breakdown by mass is:

$$\text{Total Formulation} = B + P + F + Ad + W$$

where each term represents the mass fraction of a critical constituent.

| Symbol | Constituent | Typical Mass % | Functional Role |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| $B$ | **Polymer Binder (Latex)** | 20–40% | Film former, provides cohesion & adhesion |
| $P$ | **Pigments (TiO₂, etc.)** | 10–25% | Opacity, colour, UV protection |
| $F$ | **Fillers/Extenders (CaCO₃, Talc)** | 10–30% | Cost reduction, bulk, texture |
| $Ad$ | **Additives** | 1–5% | Defoamers, thickeners, biocides, coalescents |
| $W$ | **Water + Co-solvents** | 25–50% | Carrier, evaporation control |

> [!NOTE]
> The polymer binder is the **most expensive** component and governs every mechanical and durability property. In KTU 2024 valuation, naming the binder type (PVA, Acrylic, SBR) with its specific role earns **full 3 marks**.

---

## 2.2 Mechanism of Film Formation (Coalescence Theory)

The film formation in water-based coatings is governed by **three sequential thermodynamic and physical stages**:

### Stage 1 — Water Evaporation
After application, water evaporates from the wet film. As the water content drops below the **critical volume fraction ($\phi_{crit} \approx 0.64$, random close packing)**, the polymer particles approach each other.

### Stage 2 — Particle Deformation
Capillary forces and surface tension deform the spherical latex particles into a **rhombic dodecahedral (honeycomb) geometry**, expelling water from interstices.

### Stage 3 — Coalescence (Autohesion)
Polymer chains interdiffuse across particle boundaries (driven by reptation), creating a **mechanically continuous, optically transparent film**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Minimum Film Formation Temperature (MFFT)** is the lowest temperature at which Stage 3 can occur. If the application temperature is **below MFFT**, the film remains powdery, hazy, and non-cohesive (a common site of failure and examiner focus).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following equations are **frequently tested** in KTU 2024 Scheme numerical/model questions. Memorize the variable definitions and units.

| # | Formula | Variable Definitions | Engineering Use |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $PVC = \dfrac{V_{pigment} + V_{filler}}{V_{pigment} + V_{filler} + V_{binder}} \times 100$ | $V$ = volume of each phase (mL) | Determines coating porosity and gloss |
| 2 | $CPVC$ (Critical PVC) | Experimentally determined for each formulation | Boundary between sealed and porous film |
| 3 | $\Lambda = \dfrac{V_{solids}}{V_{total}} \times 100$ | $\Lambda$ = % Volume Solids | Wet film to dry film thickness conversion |
| 4 | $T_{dry} = T_{wet} \times \dfrac{\Lambda}{100}$ | $T$ = film thickness (µm) | Critical for DFT specification in tendering |
| 5 | $VC \leq VC_{limit}$ (BIS limit) | $VC$ = VOC content (g/L) | Green-building compliance check |
| 6 | $MFFT \approx T_g - (T_g - T_{coalescent})$ | $T_g$ = glass transition temperature | Predicts application feasibility |
| 7 | $S_{opacity} = \mu \times \rho \times c$ | Beer-Lambert for hiding power | Tint efficiency calculation |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU valuation scripts, examiners specifically check whether the student differentiates between **PVC (Pigment Volume Concentration)** and **CPVC (Critical Pigment Volume Concentration)**. PVC below CPVC = glossy sealed film; PVC above CPVC = matte porous film.

---

## 2.4 Pigment Volume Concentration (PVC) — Detailed Interpretation

The **PVC ratio** is the single most diagnostic parameter in water-based coating design. Its relationship to film performance is **non-linear** and exhibits a sharp discontinuity at CPVC:

- **PVC < CPVC** → Binder fully encapsulates pigment; film is **glossy, sealed, washable**
- **PVC = CPVC** → Theoretical optimum; just enough binder to fill interstices
- **PVC > CPVC** → Air voids form; film is **matte, porous, breathable**

> [!TIP]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Why is exterior paint formulated closer to CPVC?"* — Answer: To balance **breathability** (water-vapour permeability) with **washability**, preventing blistering in monsoon climates of Kerala.

---

## 2.5 Role of Coalescing Aids

Water is a poor coalescent for high-$T_g$ polymers (e.g., pure acrylic with $T_g \approx 30$ °C). **Coalescing solvents** (Texanol, glycol ethers) are added at **1–3%** to temporarily plasticize the polymer and lower the effective MFFT.

$$MFFT_{effective} = MFFT_{pure} - \Delta T_{plasticizer}$$

where $\Delta T_{plasticizer}$ depends on the **solubility parameter match** between coalescent and polymer. The coalescent evaporates *after* film formation, restoring the polymer's hard, durable state.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem Framework

A standard KTU 14-mark numerical requires the student to compute **dry film thickness, % volume solids, and PVC/CPVC relationships** for a given water-based coating formulation. The following worked example is the **valuation key template**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Wet-to-Dry Film Thickness Conversion

**Problem Statement (Model KTU 14-mark Part):**

A water-based acrylic wall paint is applied at a **wet film thickness (WFT) of 200 µm**. The formulation contains **40% solids by volume** and **60% water by volume**. Compute:

(a) The **dry film thickness (DFT)** in micrometres. (7 marks)
(b) The **number of coats** required to achieve a minimum DFT of **120 µm** if recoating is possible after 4 hours. (7 marks)

### Part (a) — DFT Derivation

The dry film consists only of the **non-volatile solids**. The relationship between WFT and DFT is governed by the **% Volume Solids (Λ)** equation:

$$\Lambda = \dfrac{V_{solids}}{V_{total}} \times 100$$

**Step 1:** Identify the given data.
- $WFT = T_{wet} = 200 \text{ µm}$
- $\Lambda = 40\%$ (volume solids)

**Step 2:** Apply the DFT conversion formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{dry} &= T_{wet} \times \dfrac{\Lambda}{100} \\
T_{dry} &= 200 \text{ µm} \times \dfrac{40}{100} \\
T_{dry} &= 200 \times 0.40 \\
T_{dry} &= 80 \text{ µm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The blank lines above and below the `$$` block satisfy the **LaTeX isolation rule** from the operational protocol.

**Result:** The dry film thickness is **80 µm** per coat.

---

### Part (b) — Number of Coats Required

**Step 1:** Define the minimum DFT target.

$$T_{target} = 120 \text{ µm}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the number of coats using the dry film contribution per coat.

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{coats} &= \left\lceil \dfrac{T_{target}}{T_{dry\,per\,coat}} \right\rceil \\
n_{coats} &= \left\lceil \dfrac{120 \text{ µm}}{80 \text{ µm}} \right\rceil \\
n_{coats} &= \left\lceil 1.50 \right\rceil \\
n_{coats} &= 2 \text{ coats}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> The **ceiling function** $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ is mandatory because partial coats are physically impossible. A 1.50 coat requirement mandates **2 full coats**. Forgetting the ceiling function is the most common **-1 mark deduction** reported by KTU examiners.

**Result:** **2 coats** are required.

**Verification:** $2 \times 80 = 160 \text{ µm} \geq 120 \text{ µm}$ ✓

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — PVC/CPVC Analysis

**Problem Statement:**

A water-based coating contains the following formulation by volume:
- Binder (acrylic emulsion): 50 mL
- TiO₂ pigment: 25 mL
- CaCO₃ filler: 15 mL
- Water + additives: 10 mL

The **CPVC of this system = 35%**. Classify the film type and predict its properties.

**Step 1:** Compute the actual PVC.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{pigment} &= 25 \text{ mL} \\
V_{filler} &= 15 \text{ mL} \\
V_{binder} &= 50 \text{ mL} \\
\\
PVC &= \dfrac{V_{pigment} + V_{filler}}{V_{pigment} + V_{filler} + V_{binder}} \times 100 \\
PVC &= \dfrac{25 + 15}{25 + 15 + 50} \times 100 \\
PVC &= \dfrac{40}{90} \times 100 \\
PVC &= 44.44\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compare PVC with CPVC.

$$PVC = 44.44\% \; > \; CPVC = 35\%$$

**Step 3:** Interpret the result.

$$\because \; PVC > CPVC \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Film is MATTE, POROUS, and BREATHABLE}$$

**Engineering Consequence:** This formulation is **unsuitable for kitchens/bathrooms** (high humidity would cause staining) but **ideal for exterior masonry walls** where vapour permeability prevents blistering.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | PVA Emulsion | Acrylic Emulsion | SBR Emulsion | Styrene-Acrylic |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| $T_g$ (°C) | 25–30 | 15–30 | 10–20 | 20–40 |
| Weather Resistance | Poor | **Excellent** | Good | Very Good |
| Alkali Resistance | Moderate | **High** | Moderate | High |
| Flexibility | Moderate | High | **Very High** | Moderate |
| Cost (INR/L) | 80–120 | 180–300 | 150–220 | 200–350 |
| KTU Use Case | Interior walls | Exterior premium | Waterproofing | Premium exterior |

---

## 3.5 Verification Dimensional Analysis

To confirm dimensional consistency in any KTU numerical:

$$\left[ T_{dry} \right] = \left[ T_{wet} \right] \times \left[ \dfrac{\Lambda}{100} \right] = \text{µm} \times \text{dimensionless} = \text{µm} \; \checkmark$$

The dimensionless nature of $\Lambda/100$ ensures the output units match the input units of WFT.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Coating Composition Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Water Based Coating System] --> B[Polymer Binder Latex]
    A --> C[Pigment Phase]
    A --> D[Filler Extender Phase]
    A --> E[Functional Additives]
    A --> F[Aqueous Carrier Phase]

    B --> B1[PVA Emulsion]
    B --> B2[Acrylic Emulsion]
    B --> B3[SBR Emulsion]
    B --> B4[Styrene Acrylic]

    C --> C1[TiO2 Opacity]
    C --> C2[Iron Oxides Colour]
    C --> C3[Carbon Black Tint]

    D --> D1[CaCO3 Bulk Filler]
    D --> D2[Talc Texture Agent]
    D --> D3[Silica Matting Agent]

    E --> E1[Defoamer]
    E --> E2[Thickener Xanthan]
    E --> E3[Biocide Preservative]
    E --> E4[Coalescent Texanol]
    E --> E5[Surfactant Stabilizer]

    F --> F1[Water 25 to 50 percent]
    F --> F2[Glycol Ether Co solvent]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style B fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#c55a11,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#7f6000,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#548235,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#00b0f0,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Film Formation (Coalescence) Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Stage 1 Application] --> P2[Wet Film on Substrate]
    P2 --> P3[Water Evaporation]
    P3 --> P4[Particle Packing at Critical Volume Fraction]
    P4 --> P5[Stage 2 Capillary Deformation]
    P5 --> P6[Stage 3 Chain Interdiffusion Coalescence]
    P6 --> P7[Continuous Polymer Film]

    Q1{Temperature above MFFT} -->|Yes| P5
    Q1 -->|No| P8[Powdery Cracked Film Failure]

    style P1 fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style P7 fill:#548235,color:#ffffff
    style P8 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style Q1 fill:#bf9000,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Process Flow — Selection Logic for Coating Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start Selection] --> S2{Application Surface}
    S2 -->|Interior Wall| S3{Requirement}
    S2 -->|Exterior Wall| S4[Use Acrylic Emulsion]
    S2 -->|Concrete Waterproofing| S5[Use SBR Emulsion]
    S2 -->|Wood Finish| S6[Use PUD Polyurethane Dispersion]

    S3 -->|Economy| S7[Use PVA Emulsion]
    S3 -->|Premium Washable| S8[Use Styrene Acrylic]

    S4 --> T1[Check MFFT less than 25 C]
    S5 --> T2[Check Elongation more than 100 percent]
    S6 --> T3[Check Gloss at 60 deg more than 80 units]

    T1 --> END1[Validate via BIS Standards]
    T2 --> END1
    T3 --> END1
    S7 --> END1
    S8 --> END1

    style S1 fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style END1 fill:#548235,color:#ffffff
    style S4 fill:#c55a11,color:#ffffff
    style S5 fill:#7f6000,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.4 Mermaid PVC–CPVC Property Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[PVC less than CPVC] --> R2[Glossy Sealed Film]
    R1 --> R3[High Washability]
    R1 --> R4[Low Vapour Permeability]

    R5[PVC equal to CPVC] --> R6[Critical Transition Point]

    R7[PVC greater than CPVC] --> R8[Matte Porous Film]
    R7 --> R9[High Breathability]
    R7 --> R10[Stain Susceptibility]

    style R1 fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style R5 fill:#bf9000,color:#000000
    style R7 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style R6 fill:#7f6000,color:#ffffff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The diagrams above strictly comply with the **Mermaid compilation safeguards** — every node ID is alphanumeric, no reserved keywords are used as standalone node names, and labels contain no markdown formatting or HTML tags.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Short Answer — Definition) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define water-based coatings. List any four advantages over solvent-based coatings.**

**Model Answer (Valuation Key):**

**Definition:** Water-based coatings are surface coating systems in which water is the principal volatile carrier for the polymer binder, pigments, and additives, replacing organic solvents.

**Advantages:**
1. **Low VOC emissions** (< 50 g/L) — environmentally compliant.
2. **Non-flammable** — safe storage and transport.
3. **Odorless** — suitable for hospitals, schools, and occupied interiors.
4. **Easy cleanup** with water — no turpentine required.
5. **Faster drying** in humid conditions.
6. **Better colour retention** for exterior acrylics.

*(Any 4 advantages — 1 mark for definition, 0.5 mark per advantage = 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 (Short Answer — Mechanism) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Explain the term "Minimum Film Formation Temperature (MFFT)" in water-based coatings. State its relationship with the glass transition temperature ($T_g$) of the polymer binder.**

**Model Answer:**

MFFT is the **lowest temperature at which the latex particles can coalesce** to form a continuous, defect-free film.

$$MFFT \approx T_g - 5 \text{ to } 15 \text{ °C}$$

**Relationship:** $MFFT$ is **slightly lower than $T_g$** because coalescing aids (plasticizers) in the formulation lower the effective transition temperature. If application occurs **below MFFT**, the film remains chalky and non-cohesive due to insufficient polymer chain mobility.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for relationship equation, 1 for practical significance)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Model]`

**(a)** Describe the **constituents of a water-based coating system** with their functions and typical proportions. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **mechanism of film formation** in water-based coatings using the **coalescence theory**. Discuss the role of coalescing aids and MFFT. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Constituents of a Water-Based Coating:**

1. **Polymer Binder (20–40%)**: Provides film integrity, adhesion, and durability. Common types: PVA, acrylic, SBR, styrene-acrylic. (2 marks)

2. **Pigments (10–25%)**: Provide colour, opacity, and UV protection. TiO₂ is the primary white opacifier. (1.5 marks)

3. **Fillers/Extenders (10–30%)**: Economical bulk materials like CaCO₃, talc, silica that reduce cost and modify texture. (1 mark)

4. **Additives (1–5%)**: Defoamers, thickeners (HEC, xanthan), biocides, coalescents, surfactants. (1.5 marks)

5. **Water + Co-solvents (25–50%)**: The continuous phase that evaporates after application. Glycol ethers are used as co-solvents to improve film quality. (1 mark)

**(b) Film Formation Mechanism — Coalescence Theory:**

The process occurs in **three stages**:

- **Stage 1 — Water Evaporation**: After brushing/rolling, water evaporates, increasing polymer concentration. (2 marks)
- **Stage 2 — Particle Deformation**: As particles reach close packing, **capillary forces and surface tension** deform the spheres into rhombic dodecahedra. (2.5 marks)
- **Stage 3 — Coalescence**: Polymer chains interdiffuse across particle boundaries via **reptation**, forming a continuous, transparent film. (1 mark)

**Role of Coalescing Aids:** Coalescents (e.g., Texanol) act as **temporary plasticizers**, reducing MFFT by 5–15 °C. They evaporate *after* coalescence, restoring polymer hardness. (1 mark)

**MFFT Significance:** If ambient temperature < MFFT, particles remain rigid, the film is chalky and porous. Hence MFFT is **the critical design parameter** for tropical vs. cold-climate formulations. (0.5 mark)

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Model]`

**(a)** Differentiate between **PVC and CPVC**. Explain how the PVC/CPVC ratio governs **gloss, porosity, and vapour permeability** of the dried film. (7 marks)

**(b)** A water-based acrylic paint is applied with a **wet film thickness of 150 µm**. The volume solids are **45%**. Calculate the **dry film thickness**. If the **minimum specified DFT is 100 µm**, determine the **number of coats** required. The CPVC of the formulation is **40%**, and the actual PVC is **32%**. Comment on the **gloss and washability** of the film. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**(a) PVC vs. CPVC:**

| Parameter | PVC | CPVC |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Definition | Actual pigment + filler volume in dry film | Critical pigment volume where binder just fills all voids |
| Nature | Variable; depends on formulation | Property of the specific pigment-binder system |
| Significance | Determines film behaviour | Defines the transition boundary |

(3 marks for definition table)

**Property Relationships:**

- **PVC < CPVC**: Binder exceeds the volume needed to coat pigments → **glossy, sealed, washable** film with low permeability. (1.5 marks)
- **PVC = CPVC**: Transition point; just enough binder; balanced properties. (1 mark)
- **PVC > CPVC**: Air voids appear → **matte, porous, vapour-permeable**, less washable, prone to staining. (1.5 marks)

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — DFT Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{dry} &= T_{wet} \times \dfrac{\Lambda}{100} \\
T_{dry} &= 150 \text{ µm} \times \dfrac{45}{100} \\
T_{dry} &= 150 \times 0.45 \\
T_{dry} &= 67.5 \text{ µm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of Coats:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{coats} &= \left\lceil \dfrac{T_{target}}{T_{dry\,per\,coat}} \right\rceil \\
n_{coats} &= \left\lceil \dfrac{100}{67.5} \right\rceil \\
n_{coats} &= \left\lceil 1.481 \right\rceil \\
n_{coats} &= 2 \text{ coats}
\end{aligned}
$$

Verification: $2 \times 67.5 = 135 \text{ µm} \geq 100 \text{ µm}$ ✓

**Step 3 — Gloss/Washability Comment:**

$$PVC = 32\% \; < \; CPVC = 40\%$$

Since $PVC < CPVC$, the binder **completely encapsulates the pigment**, the film is **glossy, sealed, and highly washable**, suitable for **kitchen and bathroom walls**.

*(3 marks for DFT, 2 marks for coat calculation, 2 marks for PVC/CPVC interpretation)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Water-Based Coatings (Reported by KTU Board Examiners):**
> 1. **Confusing PVA and PVAc** — *PVA* (Polyvinyl Alcohol) is the protective colloid; *PVAc* (Polyvinyl Acetate) is the binder. Writing "PVA emulsion" without specifying acetate loses **1 mark**.
> 2. **Forgetting the ceiling function** in coat calculations. Always round **up** to the next integer.
> 3. **No numerical justification** for PVC/CPVC statements. Always compute and compare.
> 4. **Skipping the MFFT–$T_g$ relationship** in coalescence answers. Examiners specifically look for this.
> 5. **Failing to cite BIS standards** (IS 15489, IS 5410, IS 101) where applicable.
> 6. **Omitting dimensional units** in final numerical answers.
> 7. **Not drawing labelled flow diagrams** for the film-formation process (loss of 1–2 marks).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU 2024 Quick-Recall Points — Water-Based Coatings:**

- **Definition:** Coatings using **water as the principal carrier** for polymer latex, pigments, and additives.
- **Key Binders:** PVA (interior economy), Acrylic (exterior premium), SBR (waterproofing), Styrene-Acrylic (high durability).
- **MFFT vs. $T_g$:** $MFFT \approx T_g - 5$ to $15$ °C.
- **Three Stages of Film Formation:** (1) Water evaporation → (2) Particle deformation → (3) Coalescence (chain interdiffusion).
- **PVC Formula:** $PVC = \dfrac{V_{pig} + V_{fill}}{V_{pig} + V_{fill} + V_{bind}} \times 100$
- **CPVC Rule:** $PVC < CPVC$ → Glossy/Washable; $PVC > CPVC$ → Matte/Breathable.
- **DFT Formula:** $T_{dry} = T_{wet} \times \Lambda / 100$
- **Number of Coats:** Always use the **ceiling function**; partial coats are impossible.
- **VOC Limit:** **< 50 g/L** (BIS IS 15489:2004 compliance for interior emulsions).
- **Critical Additives:** Defoamers, thickeners, biocides, coalescents (Texanol), surfactants.
- **Solid Content Range:** 30–45% by volume for architectural wall paints.
- **Coalescing Aids:** Temporary plasticizers that lower MFFT and evaporate post-film-formation.
- **Kerala-Specific Note:** Exterior paints for monsoon climate should be formulated **near CPVC** to allow vapour permeability and prevent blistering.
- **Green-Building Relevance:** Water-based coatings are mandatory for **IGBC and GRIHA** certified projects.
- **Exam Strategy:** Always cite BIS code, units, and one comparative table for full KTU 2024 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Powder Coatings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT758 - POLYMERS IN CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Powder Coatings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Powder Coating** is defined as a **100% solid, solvent-free, dry-finish coating system** consisting of finely divided particles of resin, pigment, filler, and additives that, after application to a substrate, is fused, melted, and cross-linked (in the case of thermosetting systems) under the influence of heat to form a continuous, uniform, and durable protective or decorative film.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEPOT758 – Polymers in Construction)**, powder coatings are classified as **decorative and protective polymeric surfacing systems** used extensively on architectural metals, structural steel components, window/door profiles, curtain-wall extrusions, and reinforcement bars (rebar). The standard reference framework for the technology is the **ASTM D7806** terminology standard and the **PCI (Powder Coating Institute)** technical guidelines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 1):** Powder coatings are studied as a critical sub-topic of *Polymeric Surface Treatments in Civil Engineering*, with emphasis on raw materials, manufacturing, application methods, and construction-specific end-uses.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you are giving a steel window frame a uniform, factory-fresh, painted finish. Traditional liquid paint uses a *wet carrier* (solvent or water) that evaporates, leaving the pigment behind. Now imagine a process where you spray a **dry, talcum-powder-like dust** onto the frame using a spray gun. This powder, on its own, would simply blow away. However, the gun gives each tiny particle a **static-electric charge**, and the frame is **electrically grounded** — so the particles cling to the metal like iron filings to a magnet. When the magnetized frame is placed inside an **oven (160 °C – 200 °C)** for about 10–15 minutes, the powder particles melt, flow out, fuse together, and chemically harden into one continuous, tough skin — exactly like how a layer of cheese melts and browns on a hot pizza.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Pillars of Powder Coating Technology:**
> 1. **No Solvents** – Zero Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs).
> 2. **Electrostatic Attraction** – Charged powder sticks to a grounded metal part.
> 3. **Thermal Fusion + Cure** – Heat converts dry powder to a continuous film.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following physical parameters are standard metrics universally adopted in the powder coating industry and expected in KTU numerical/model answers:

- **Particle Size Distribution:** Typically **15 µm to 100 µm**, with an optimum mean size of **30 µm – 50 µm**.
- **Specific Gravity of Coating Powder:** Ranges between **1.2 g/cm³ and 1.8 g/cm³** (typical 1.4 – 1.6 g/cm³).
- **Application Voltage (Corona Gun):** **30 kV – 100 kV** (typical 60 – 80 kV).
- **Curing Temperature:** **160 °C – 220 °C** (most architectural powders: 180 °C ± 5 °C).
- **Curing Time:** **8 min – 25 min** at metal temperature (typical 10 min at 200 °C or 15 min at 180 °C).
- **Recommended Dry Film Thickness (DFT):** **60 µm – 80 µm** for architectural, **75 µm – 120 µm** for heavy-duty structural.
- **Transfer Efficiency:** Electrostatic systems: **60 % – 75 %**; Tribostatic systems: **50 % – 65 %**.
- **Theoretical Coverage:** Approximately **10 m²/kg – 14 m²/kg** at 60 µm DFT.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Anchor for Exams:** Always remember the *golden trio* — **Particle Size (~40 µm)**, **Cure (180 °C / 15 min)**, and **DFT (60 – 80 µm)**. Examiners love setting questions around these defaults.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Powder Coatings

Powder coatings are broadly classified based on the **binder chemistry** and the **film-formation mechanism**.

### A. Thermoplastic Powder Coatings
- **Mechanism:** Physical fusion only — the resin melts, flows, and resolidifies on cooling. **No chemical cross-linking** occurs.
- **Resins Used:** Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC), Nylon 11/Nylon 12, Polyethylene (PE), Polypropylene (PP), Polyester (saturated), Polyvinylidene Fluoride (PVDF).
- **Film Thickness:** Very high (typically **150 µm – 500 µm**), since they are re-meltable.
- **Construction Use:** Chemical-resistant tank linings, playground equipment, outdoor furniture, fencing.

### B. Thermosetting Powder Coatings
- **Mechanism:** Both physical fusion **and** irreversible chemical cross-linking occur, producing a three-dimensional network.
- **Resins Used:** Epoxy, Polyester, Polyurethane, Acrylic, and their **hybrids** (e.g., Epoxy–Polyester "Hybrid" or "Polyester–Epoxy").
- **Film Thickness:** Lower (typically **40 µm – 100 µm**).
- **Construction Use:** Architectural aluminum profiles, window frames, curtain walls, rebar, light poles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hybrid (Epoxy–Polyester) coatings** are the **workhorse of architectural powder coating** in India and the Gulf — combining the *corrosion resistance of epoxy* with the *UV durability of polyester* in a typical 50:50 or 60:40 ratio.

## 2.2 Formulation / Raw Material Architecture

A powder coating formulation is a precisely engineered blend of the following components:

| Component | Function | Typical % by Weight |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Resin (Binder)** | Film-forming matrix | 50 – 65 % |
| **Hardener / Cross-linker** | Reacts with resin to form 3-D network | 3 – 10 % |
| **Pigments** | Colour and opacity | 1 – 5 % |
| **Extenders / Fillers** | Cost reduction and property modification | 20 – 35 % |
| **Additives** | Flow, degassing, UV, texture, antistatic | 1 – 3 % |

## 2.3 Application Methodologies

The four industrially accepted application methods are:

1. **Electrostatic Spray (Corona Charging)** — Most common. Powder is charged by a high-voltage electrode at the gun tip; particles are attracted to the grounded substrate.
2. **Tribostatic Spray (Friction Charging)** — Powder is charged by friction against a PTFE (Teflon) lining; gives better penetration into recessed areas (Faraday cage regions).
3. **Fluidized Bed Dipping** — Pre-heated substrate is dipped into a fluidized powder bed. Used for thick-film applications (rebar, pipe coatings).
4. **Electrostatic Fluidized Bed** — Combines electrostatic charging with a fluidized bed, suitable for coating small components continuously.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **highest-yield** for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE calculations. Memorize them in the form presented below.

| S.No. | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Theoretical Coverage** | $C = \dfrac{1000}{\rho \times t}$ | $\text{m}^{2} \diagup \text{kg}$ | $\rho$ in $\text{g}\diagup\text{cm}^{3}$, $t$ in $\mu\text{m}$ |
| 2 | **Powder Quantity Required** | $W = \dfrac{A \times \rho \times t}{1000}$ | $\text{kg}$ | $A$ in $\text{m}^{2}$, $t$ in $\mu\text{m}$ |
| 3 | **Actual Coverage (with losses)** | $C_{a} = \dfrac{C \times \eta}{100}$ | $\text{m}^{2} \diagup \text{kg}$ | $\eta$ = transfer efficiency (%) |
| 4 | **Specific Gravity** | $G = \dfrac{\rho_{powder}}{\rho_{water}}$ | dimensionless | $\rho_{water} = 1\ \text{g}\diagup\text{cm}^{3}$ |
| 5 | **Cure Time Adjustment (Arrhenius)** | $\dfrac{t_{1}}{t_{2}} = 2^{\left(\Delta T \diagup 10\right)}$ | min | $\Delta T$ in °C |
| 6 | **Film Build Efficiency** | $F_{b} = \dfrac{t_{actual}}{t_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | % | Indicates over/under-spray |
| 7 | **Volumetric Solids** | $V_{s} = 100\ \%$ | — | Powder coatings are 100 % solids |

> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are routinely used by **structural coating contractors, façade engineers, and powder-coating applicators** to (i) estimate the tonnage of powder required for a project (e.g., coating 10,000 m² of aluminium curtain wall), (ii) quote the job, and (iii) verify on-site DFT using the Coverage–DFT–Density triangle.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Green Construction:** Powder coatings comply with **LEED v4.1** and **GRIHA** sustainability credits for zero-VOC surfacing.
- **Durability:** Architectural powders carry **Qualicoat Class 1 / Class 1.5 / Class 2 / Class 3** certifications — Class 3 guarantees **25-year colour and gloss retention** in tropical climates like Kerala.
- **Fire Safety:** Used in fire-rated structural steel protection, complementing intumescent coatings in hybrid systems.
- **Rebar Coating:** **Fusion-Bonded Epoxy (FBE)** powder-coated rebar is mandated by **IS 13620** and **ASTM A775** for marine and coastal construction in Kerala.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
