# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> Differentiate between **Polymer-Impregnated Concrete (PIC)** and **Polymer-Cement Concrete (PCC)** with respect to binder composition and curing mechanism.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | PIC | PCC |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Binder | Cement-only at casting; polymer is added later as impregnant | Cement + polymer latex co-binders at mixing stage |
| Polymer Role | Fills pre-existing pores (post-treatment) | Co-exists with cement, forms interpenetrating network |
| Curing | Standard 28-day OPC cure → oven-dry → monomer soak → thermal/radiation polymerisation | Normal moist-curing of cement; latex film coalesces as water is drawn out |
| Polymer Content | 4 – 8 % of dry mass | 5 – 20 % latex solids on cement mass |
| Application | Heavy-duty precast, bridge decks, nuclear shielding overlays | Floor toppings, repair mortars, waterproof renders |

*(Award 1 mark for binder difference, 1 mark for mechanism difference, 1 mark for application/context difference.)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> List any **three resins** used as binders in Polymer Concrete and state **one characteristic application** for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Unsaturated Polyester Resin** — most economical; used in *pre-cast utility ducts, manhole covers, drainage channels* (1 mark).
2. **Epoxy Resin** — highest strength and adhesion; used in *structural repair patches, machine tool bases, bridge-deck overlays* (1 mark).
3. **Vinyl Ester Resin** — superior chemical and corrosion resistance; used in *chemical-plant floors, electrolytic cell bases, pickling-tank linings* (1 mark).

*(Alternative answers: Furan resin for acid resistance; MMA for ultra-rapid road repair.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper Adaptation]**
> **(a)** Explain with a neat flow diagram the **manufacturing process of Polymer-Impregnated Concrete (PIC)**. Discuss the role of each stage in determining the final strength of the composite. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Compare the **mechanical and durability properties** of PC, PIC, PCC and OPC concrete. In what situations would you prefer PCC over PC for a given project? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — PIC Manufacturing Process (7 marks)

The manufacture of PIC is a *multi-stage post-treatment* process applied to a fully cured OPC concrete body.

**Stage 1 — Casting & Curing of the Base OPC Concrete (1 mark)**
The OPC concrete is proportioned, mixed, cast and water-cured for 28 days to attain its design strength. The hardened body must be *dimensionally stable* before impregnation.

**Stage 2 — Drying (1 mark)**
The cured body is dried in an oven at **110 – 150 °C** for 24 – 48 hours to drive out all free moisture from the capillary pores. *Why?* Any residual water in the pores acts as a *chain-transfer inhibitor* during polymerisation, weakening the final polymer network.

**Stage 3 — Vacuum De-aeration (1 mark)**
The dried body is placed in a pressure vessel and subjected to a *vacuum of about 0.05 – 0.08 MPa* to evacuate trapped air from the pores. *Why?* Air pockets block the ingress of monomer; pre-evacuation increases the *depth and uniformity* of impregnation.

**Stage 4 — Monomer Impregnation (1.5 marks)**
A low-viscosity monomer (typically **methyl methacrylate — MMA**, viscosity ≈ 0.6 cP at 25 °C, viscosity of water is 1 cP) is admitted into the vessel, and the pressure is raised to **0.5 – 5 MPa gauge** for 1 – 4 hours. The applied pressure forces the monomer deep into the capillary pore network. A *coupling agent* (e.g., silane) is often pre-applied to the monomer to enhance polymer–aggregate bond.

**Stage 5 — Polymerisation (1.5 marks)**
The monomer-impregnated body is removed, surface-dried, and polymerised *in situ* by one of two methods:
- **Thermal initiation** — hot water bath at 70 – 90 °C, using a peroxide catalyst (e.g., benzoyl peroxide) pre-dissolved in the monomer.
- **Radiation initiation** — exposure to cobalt-60 gamma rays or an electron beam, which ionises the monomer and triggers free-radical polymerisation without thermal stress.

The result is a continuous, cross-linked polymer phase that *locks into* the cement matrix, sealing all accessible capillary pores.

**Stage 6 — Cooling and Conditioning (1 mark)**
The PIC body is cooled slowly to ambient temperature to avoid thermal-shock micro-cracking, then surface-cleaned and is ready for service.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Insight:** The final strength of PIC is governed by *three factors*: (i) **PLF** (polymer loading factor) — higher is stronger; (ii) **degree of polymerisation** — complete conversion of monomer is essential; (iii) **uniformity of impregnation** — controlled by viscosity, pressure and dwell-time.

#### Part (b) — Comparative Property Matrix (4 marks) and Selection Logic (3 marks)

| Property | OPC | PIC | PCC | PC |
|----------|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| Compressive Strength | 20 – 50 | 70 – 150 | 30 – 70 | 60 – 150 |
| Tensile Strength | 2 – 5 | 7 – 12 | 4 – 7 | 8 – 20 |
| Water Absorption (%) | 3 – 8 | 0.3 – 1.0 | 1.5 – 4.0 | < 0.5 |
| Acid Resistance | Poor | Moderate | Moderate | Excellent |
| Curing Time | 28 days | Hours (post-cure) | 7 – 28 days | 1 – 24 h |
| Cost (Index) | 1.0× | 5 – 10× | 1.5 – 2.5× | 3 – 6× |

**When PCC is preferred over PC (3 marks):**

1. **Cost-sensitive projects** — PCC is **1.5 – 2.5×** cost of OPC but only a fraction of PC's cost. For *floor toppings, renders, repair mortars* in civil buildings, PCC gives adequate performance at reasonable price.
2. **Vapour permeability required** — PC is *too impermeable* and may blister under hydrostatic pressure from the substrate side. PCC allows some *breathability* while still reducing permeability versus plain OPC.
3. **Compatibility with cementitious substrates** — PCC bonds well to existing OPC concrete without a coupling agent; PC requires a primer.
4. **Alkali/silica reaction (ASR) mitigation** — SBR and acrylic latices in PCC *sequester alkalis* and reduce ASR expansion in susceptible aggregates.
5. **Fire performance** — PCC has substantially better fire-rating than PC because the cement matrix remains stable at elevated temperatures.

**Hence PCC is preferred for:** *general building repairs, screeds, water-proof plasters, tile adhesives, and overlays where extreme chemical resistance is not the design driver.*

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]**
> **(a)** Describe the **constituents and mix-design philosophy** of Polymer Concrete (PC). Explain the role of (i) resin, (ii) hardener, (iii) aggregate grading, and (iv) coupling agent. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A Polymer Concrete specimen is to be designed for a **manhole-cover** application in a coastal chemical plant. Recommend a suitable resin system, justify your choice, and discuss the **curing and quality-control testing** protocol. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Constituents of Polymer Concrete (7 marks)

PC is fundamentally a *two-phase composite*: a **resin matrix** (continuous phase) binding a densely packed **aggregate skeleton** (dispersed phase). The mix-design philosophy targets **minimum resin content with maximum aggregate packing** to reduce cost and exotherm while maintaining workability and strength.

**(i) Resin (1.5 marks):**
The resin is the *only binder*. It must be a low-to-medium viscosity thermosetting liquid. Common choices:
- *Unsaturated polyester* (general purpose, economical)
- *Vinyl ester* (superior chemical resistance)
- *Epoxy* (highest mechanical strength and adhesion)
- *Furan* (acid-only environments)
- *MMA* (fastest cure)

The resin holds the aggregates together and transfers stress across the matrix.

**(ii) Hardener / Curing Agent (1.5 marks):**
Hardeners trigger the cross-linking (curing) reaction.
- *For polyester / vinyl ester:* **Methyl Ethyl Ketone Peroxide (MEKP)** initiator + **cobalt naphthenate** promoter at room temperature.
- *For epoxy:* **Polyamine** (TETA, DETA) or **polyamide** hardener.
- *For MMA:* **Benzoyl peroxide (BPO)** + tertiary amine accelerator.

The hardener-to-resin ratio is critical — under-dosing gives incomplete cure, over-dosing gives brittle matrix.

**(iii) Aggregate Grading (2 marks):**
Aggregates comprise **88 – 92 %** of the mass. A *well-graded* combination of:
- Coarse aggregate (10 – 20 mm crushed granite or quartzite)
- Fine aggregate (Zone II silica sand, < 4.75 mm)
- Filler (calcium carbonate, fly ash, silica flour < 75 µm)

is used to achieve the **maximum packing density** (lowest void content), which minimises the resin demand. *Why?* Resin is the most expensive component; lower resin content = lower cost and lower exothermic heat of cure (less thermal-cracking).

**(iv) Coupling Agent (2 marks):**
Coupling agents (typically **silane** compounds such as *3-methacryloxypropyl-trimethoxysilane*) are applied either as a *pre-treatment on the aggregate* or *admixed into the resin*. They form a *chemical bridge* between the **inorganic aggregate surface (Si–OH groups)** and the **organic polymer matrix (C=C, –OH groups)**, dramatically improving:
- Wet/dry bond strength
- Resistance to moisture-induced debonding
- Long-term durability under cyclic loading

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Point:** In KTU valuation, examiners often award 1 mark specifically for mentioning *silane coupling agent and its role*. Do not skip it.

#### Part (b) — Design Recommendation for Coastal Chemical-Plant Manhole Cover (7 marks)

**Recommended Resin System: Vinyl Ester Resin (1.5 marks)**

**Justification (3 marks):**

1. **Acid & Alkali Resistance** — Vinyl ester has the *ester linkage only at the chain ends* (unlike polyester which has it throughout), making it vastly superior in resisting hydrolysis by acids (HCl, H₂SO₄ vapours) and alkalis (NaOH leaks) typical of chemical plants.
2. **Salt-Water Durability** — The cross-linked vinyl ester matrix absorbs < 0.1 % water, preventing chloride-ion ingress that would corrode any embedded steel reinforcement in the surrounding environment.
3. **Mechanical Strength** — Compressive strength of vinyl-ester PC is 80 – 120 MPa, easily handling wheel loads (Class A – D manhole ratings per IS 12592 / EN 124).
4. **Adhesion to Concrete Ring** — Vinyl ester bonds tenaciously to the surrounding OPC chamber, minimising seat-rotation.

**Curing Protocol (1.5 marks):**

1. Mix vinyl ester + cobalt promoter → stir thoroughly.
2. Add MEKP initiator (1 – 2 % of resin mass) → mix for 1 – 2 minutes.
3. Pour into the mould around pre-placed aggregate skeleton (or pre-mix aggregate + resin and pour).
4. Allow **gel time ≈ 15 – 30 min** at 25 °C.
5. **De-mould after 4 – 6 hours** (initial cure).
6. **Full cure in 7 days** at ambient; *post-cure at 60 – 80 °C for 2 hours* if accelerated production is required to develop full Tg (glass transition temperature).

**Quality-Control Testing (1 mark):**

- **Compressive strength test** on 100 mm cubes (target ≥ 80 MPa).
- **Water absorption** (target < 0.5 % by mass, 24-hour immersion).
- **Acid resistance** — 7-day immersion in 5 % H₂SO₄; mass loss < 1 %.
- **Barcol hardness** to verify full cure (target ≥ 35).
- **Visual inspection** for voids, cracks, surface defects.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing PIC with PC** — many students use the terms interchangeably. *PIC* involves *pre-cast OPC* that is later impregnated; *PC* has *no cement* at all. Examiners deduct 2 – 3 marks for this.
> 2. **Forgetting the coupling agent** in PC mix design. Always mention silane treatment.
> 3. **Citing wrong polymer content** — PC = 8 – 12 % resin, PCC = 5 – 20 % latex solids, PIC = 4 – 8 % polymer loading.
> 4. **Ignoring fire/UV resistance** as a *disadvantage* of PC — examiners expect a balanced answer.
> 5. **Skipping the units** in numerical problems (MPa, kg/m³, %, °C) — instant 0.5 mark deduction per instance.
> 6. **Writing the resin name without mentioning the hardener** — for example, "use polyester" is incomplete; specify "polyester + MEKP + cobalt naphthenate."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Polymer Concrete = Aggregate + Polymer Resin Binder (no cement).**
- **Three families:** **PIC** (post-treatment), **PCC** (hybrid cement + latex), **PC** (pure resin concrete).
- **Polymer Impregnated Concrete (PIC)** is made by drying cured OPC → vacuum-pressure impregnation with **MMA/styrene** → thermal or radiation polymerisation.
- **Polymer Cement Concrete (PCC)** uses **SBR, PVA, acrylic or epoxy latex** as a *co-binder* with cement; forms an *interpenetrating* C–S–H + polymer network.
- **Polymer Concrete (PC)** uses **thermosetting resins** — polyester, vinyl ester, epoxy, furan, MMA — with hardeners; aggregates are 88 – 92 % of mass.
- **Compressive strength** of PC typically **60 – 150 MPa** (vs. 20 – 50 MPa for OPC).
- **Water absorption** of PC is **< 0.5 %** (vs. 3 – 8 % for OPC) — near-impermeable.
- **Polymer Loading Factor (PLF)** = (Mass of polymer / Mass of parent concrete) × 100; typically 4 – 8 %.
- **Coupling agents (silanes)** are essential to bond the inorganic aggregate to the organic resin.
- **Hardener systems:** MEKP + cobalt for polyester/vinyl ester; amines for epoxy; BPO for MMA.
- **Key advantages:** rapid strength, low permeability, high chemical resistance, excellent bond to substrate.
- **Key disadvantages:** high cost, reduced fire resistance, UV degradation, *no* vapour permeability (risk of blistering on damp substrates).
- **High-yield applications:** bridge deck overlays, manhole covers, chemical-plant floors, machine tool bases, marine structures, pre-cast façade panels.
- **Strength enhancement correlation for PIC:** $\sigma_{c,\,PIC} = \sigma_{c,\,OPC} \times (1 + 4.5 \cdot PLF)$.
- **Water absorption formula:** $W_a = (M_{sat} - M_{dry}) / M_{dry} \times 100$.
- **Apparent porosity formula:** $n = (M_{sat} - M_{dry}) / (\rho_w \cdot V) \times 100$.
- **Flexural strength formula:** $\sigma_f = 3PL / (2 b d^2)$.
- **Modulus of elasticity (empirical):** $E_c = 4700 \sqrt{\sigma_c}$.
- **Always state units** in numerical answers (MPa, kg/m³, %).
- **Distinguish PIC, PCC, PC clearly** — examiners explicitly test this distinction every year.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:

> - **Course:** PEPOT758 - POLYMERS IN CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fiber Reinforced Polymer (FRP) Composites** are engineered composite materials formed by combining high-strength **fibers** (the reinforcement phase) with a **polymer matrix** (the binder phase) to produce a synergistic material whose mechanical properties are superior to those of the individual constituents acting alone. In civil and structural engineering contexts, FRP composites are predominantly used as external strengthening systems, internal reinforcements, and structural profiles, owing to their exceptional **strength-to-weight ratio**, **corrosion resistance**, and tailorable directional properties.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 2):**
> FRP Composites are heterogeneous, anisotropic, two-phase materials in which a high-performance fibrous reinforcement is embedded within a polymeric resin matrix. The load is transferred from the matrix to the fibers through the **interfacial shear stress** developed at the fiber–matrix interface.

### 1.2 Constituents of FRP Composites

A typical FRP composite consists of the following phases:

1. **Fibers (Reinforcement Phase):** Carry the applied load and provide strength and stiffness.
2. **Matrix (Polymer Phase):** Binds the fibers, transfers load, protects fibers, and maintains fiber positioning.
3. **Interphase (Fiber–Matrix Interface):** The critical boundary region governing load transfer efficiency.
4. **Fillers/Additives (Optional):** Modify cost, processing, fire resistance, and UV stability.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🍌 Real-World Analogy: The "Steel-Reinforced Concrete" of Polymers**
> Think of FRP composites exactly the way you think of **Reinforced Cement Concrete (RCC)**:
> - The **fibers** act like the **steel rebar** — they carry the tensile load.
> - The **polymer matrix** acts like the **concrete** — it holds the rebar in place, transfers compression, and protects the bars from corrosion.
> - Just as RCC is far stronger than plain concrete, FRP is far stronger than the pure polymer resin.
>
> **Why two materials?** Because no single material can simultaneously provide high tensile strength, compressive load transfer, environmental protection, and processability. By combining them cleverly, we get a *super-material* that outperforms both.

### 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics for FRP

| Parameter | Typical Range (Civil FRP) | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Density** | **1200 – 2100 kg/m³** | ~1/4 to 1/5 of steel |
| **Tensile Strength** | **500 – 3700 MPa** | 2 – 10× mild steel |
| **Young's Modulus** | **20 – 200 GPa** | Highly variable by fiber type |
| **Strain at Failure** | **0.5 % – 4.0 %** | Brittle failure mode |
| **Poisson's Ratio** | **0.2 – 0.3** | Near-isotropic in plane |
| **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion** | **(-1 to +10) × 10⁻⁶ /°C** | Near-zero for CFRP |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** Unlike steel, FRP is a **linear-elastic** material up to failure (no yielding). This single fact governs nearly every design provision in IS 456, ACI 440, and fib Bulletin 40.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of FRP Behavior

### Pillar 1: The Rule of Mixtures (ROM)

The fundamental theoretical framework for predicting composite properties. For a unidirectional lamina loaded along the fiber direction:

**Longitudinal Modulus (E_L):**

$$E_{L} = E_{f} V_{f} + E_{m} V_{m}$$

**Longitudinal Tensile Strength (σ_L):**

$$\sigma_{L} = \sigma_{f} V_{f} + \sigma'_{m} V_{m}$$

**Transverse Modulus (E_T):**

$$\frac{1}{E_{T}} = \frac{V_{f}}{E_{f}} + \frac{V_{m}}{E_{m}}$$

where:
- $E_f$ = Fiber Young's modulus
- $E_m$ = Matrix Young's modulus
- $V_f$ = Fiber volume fraction
- $V_m$ = Matrix volume fraction ( = 1 − V_f )
- $\sigma'_m$ = Matrix stress at fiber failure strain

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the difference between longitudinal and transverse?**
> In the longitudinal direction, fibers and matrix experience the **same strain** (iso-strain, Voigt model). In the transverse direction, they experience the **same stress** (iso-stress, Reuss model). This is why FRP is **highly anisotropic** — strong along fibers, weak across them.

### Pillar 2: Critical Fiber Length & Load Transfer

For a fiber of diameter $d_f$ and length $L_f$ embedded in a matrix, the **critical fiber length** $L_c$ is the minimum length required to develop the fiber's ultimate tensile strength through interfacial shear:

$$L_{c} = \frac{\sigma_{fu} \, d_{f}}{2 \, \tau_{i}}$$

where:
- $\sigma_{fu}$ = Ultimate tensile strength of fiber
- $d_f$ = Fiber diameter
- $\tau_i$ = Interfacial (matrix) shear strength

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Rule of Thumb (KTU-favorite):** If $L_f / d_f < L_c/d_f$, the fiber will **pull out** before breaking. If $L_f / d_f > L_c/d_f$, the fiber will **fracture** — this is the desired failure mode for maximum composite strength.

### Pillar 3: Volume Fraction Limits

$$0 \leq V_{f} \leq 0.906 \quad \text{(hexagonal close packing of fibers)}$$

Practical limits: $V_f$ typically lies between **0.30 and 0.65** for structural FRP.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | Longitudinal modulus (iso-strain) | GPa |
| 2 | $1/E_T = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$ | Transverse modulus (iso-stress) | GPa |
| 3 | $\sigma_L = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma'_m V_m$ | Longitudinal strength | MPa |
| 4 | $L_c = \sigma_{fu} d_f / (2 \tau_i)$ | Critical fiber length | mm |
| 5 | $V_f + V_m = 1$ | Volume fraction constraint | — |
| 6 | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | Composite density | kg/m³ |
| 7 | $\varepsilon_{cu} = \sigma_{fu} / E_f$ | Composite ultimate strain | — |
| 8 | $n_f = E_f / E_m$ | Modulus ratio (governs stress transfer) | — |
| 9 | $\tau = G_m \gamma_{xy}$ | Interfacial shear stress (linear elastic) | MPa |
| 10 | $f_{FRP} = \eta \cdot f_{fu}$ | Design strength (η = environmental reduction factor) | MPa |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often apply the longitudinal ROM equation to predict the **transverse** strength. This is wrong — the transverse strength is dominated by the matrix and interface, and is typically only **15 – 35 %** of the longitudinal strength.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

FRP composites are used in:
- **External strengthening** of deficient RC beams/columns (EB-FRP technique)
- **Internal reinforcement** as rebar (GFRP/ basalt rebars) in corrosion-prone structures
- **Bridge decks** and pedestrian walkways
- **Seismic retrofitting** of masonry and concrete
- **Pipes, profiles, tanks, cooling towers** in chemical plants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Almost every Module 2 question in past papers (2019 – 2023) has asked either (a) explain the constituents of FRP, (b) derive the rule of mixtures, or (c) compare GFRP/CFRP/AFRP on the basis of modulus, cost, and application. Master these three sub-topics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation: Longitudinal Modulus (E_L) by Rule of Mixtures

### Step 1 — Set up the assumptions (Voigt / Iso-strain model)

Consider a unidirectional FRP lamina with:
- All fibers aligned parallel to the loading axis (1-direction).
- Perfect bonding at the fiber–matrix interface (no slip).
- Both phases experience the same strain: $\varepsilon_1$.

$$\varepsilon_{f} = \varepsilon_{m} = \varepsilon_{1}$$

### Step 2 — Express the applied load using force equilibrium

The total load on the composite cross-section equals the sum of loads carried by fiber and matrix:

$$P_{c} = P_{f} + P_{m}$$

Substituting $P = \sigma A$ and using total area $A_c = A_f + A_m$:

$$\sigma_{c} \, A_{c} = \sigma_{f} \, A_{f} + \sigma_{m} \, A_{m}$$

### Step 3 — Convert area ratios to volume fractions

Since fibers and matrix have the same length, area ratios equal volume fractions:

$$V_{f} = \frac{A_{f}}{A_{c}}, \qquad V_{m} = \frac{A_{m}}{A_{c}}, \qquad V_{f} + V_{m} = 1$$

Therefore:

$$\sigma_{c} = \sigma_{f} \, V_{f} + \sigma_{m} \, V_{m}$$

### Step 4 — Apply Hooke's Law in the iso-strain condition

Using $\sigma = E \, \varepsilon$ for each phase (and $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m$):

$$\sigma_{c} = E_{f} \varepsilon_{1} V_{f} + E_{m} \varepsilon_{1} V_{m} = \varepsilon_{1} (E_{f} V_{f} + E_{m} V_{m})$$

But by definition $\sigma_c = E_c \varepsilon_1$, so dividing both sides by $\varepsilon_1$:

$$\boxed{E_{L} = E_{c} = E_{f} V_{f} + E_{m} V_{m}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The composite modulus is a **weighted arithmetic mean** of the fiber and matrix moduli, weighted by their volume fractions. This is why the **stiffer fiber dominates** the response when $V_f$ is high.

## 3.2 Full Derivation: Transverse Modulus (E_T)

### Step 1 — Assume iso-stress (Reuss model)

When the load is applied perpendicular to the fibers, both phases experience the same stress:

$$\sigma_{f} = \sigma_{m} = \sigma_{2}$$

### Step 2 — Strain compatibility

The total elongation is the sum of fiber and matrix elongations (series arrangement in transverse direction):

$$\Delta_{c} = \Delta_{f} + \Delta_{m}$$

$$\varepsilon_{c} \, t_{c} = \varepsilon_{f} \, t_{f} + \varepsilon_{m} \, t_{m}$$

where $t$ is the dimension in the loading direction. Since $t_f / t_c = V_f$ and $t_m / t_c = V_m$:

$$\varepsilon_{c} = \varepsilon_{f} V_{f} + \varepsilon_{m} V_{m}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Hooke's Law

$$\frac{\sigma_{2}}{E_{T}} = \frac{\sigma_{2}}{E_{f}} V_{f} + \frac{\sigma_{2}}{E_{m}} V_{m}$$

Dividing both sides by $\sigma_2$:

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{E_{T}} = \frac{V_{f}}{E_{f}} + \frac{V_{m}}{E_{m}}}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A unidirectional GFRP lamina has the following properties:
- $E_f$ (E-glass fiber) = 72 GPa
- $E_m$ (epoxy matrix) = 3.5 GPa
- $V_f$ = 0.45

**Find:** (a) $E_L$ (b) $E_T$ (c) Comment on anisotropy.

### Solution

**(a) Longitudinal Modulus (E_L):**

$$E_{L} = E_{f} V_{f} + E_{m} V_{m}$$

$$E_{L} = (72 \times 0.45) + (3.5 \times 0.55)$$

$$E_{L} = 32.40 + 1.925 = 34.325 \text{ GPa}$$

**(b) Transverse Modulus (E_T):**

$$\frac{1}{E_{T}} = \frac{V_{f}}{E_{f}} + \frac{V_{m}}{E_{m}} = \frac{0.45}{72} + \frac{0.55}{3.5}$$

$$\frac{1}{E_{T}} = 0.00625 + 0.15714 = 0.16339 \text{ (GPa)}^{-1}$$

$$E_{T} = \frac{1}{0.16339} = 6.12 \text{ GPa}$$

**(c) Anisotropy Ratio:**

$$\frac{E_{L}}{E_{T}} = \frac{34.325}{6.12} = 5.61$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The composite is **5.6× stiffer along the fiber direction** than transverse to it. This strong anisotropy is a hallmark of unidirectional FRP and is the reason fabric/cloth reinforcements (woven roving) are used when bidirectional properties are required.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Common Fibers for Civil FRP

| Property | E-Glass | S-Glass | Carbon (HS) | Carbon (HM) | Aramid (Kevlar 49) | Basalt |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Density (g/cm³)** | 2.55 | 2.49 | 1.78 | 1.85 | 1.44 | 2.65 |
| **Tensile Strength (MPa)** | 2400 | 3450 | 3530 | 2400 | 3620 | 2800 |
| **Tensile Modulus (GPa)** | 72 | 87 | 230 | 390 | 124 | 89 |
| **Strain to Failure (%)** | 4.0 | 4.5 | 1.5 | 0.6 | 2.5 | 3.1 |
| **Cost (Relative)** | Low | Medium | High | Very High | High | Low–Med |
| **Alkali Resistance** | Poor | Poor | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Excellent |
| **Typical Civil Use** | Rebar, profiles | Aerospace | Prestressing | Prestressing | Impact panels | Rebar, wraps |

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Trap:** Do not confuse **Carbon HS** (High Strength, 3530 MPa) with **Carbon HM** (High Modulus, 390 GPa). These are different grades optimized for different goals.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**List the three main constituents of an FRP composite and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**
The three main constituents of an FRP composite are:
1. **Fibers (Reinforcement Phase):** Carry the applied tensile load and provide strength and stiffness. Common types include glass (GFRP), carbon (CFRP), aramid (AFRP), and basalt (BFRP) fibers.
2. **Matrix (Polymer Phase):** Binds the fibers together, transfers load to the fibers via interfacial shear, protects fibers from environmental damage and abrasion, and maintains fiber alignment. Typical matrices are epoxy, polyester, vinyl ester, and phenolic resins.
3. **Interphase (Fiber–Matrix Interface):** The critical boundary zone responsible for efficient stress transfer between matrix and fiber. Its quality governs the overall composite performance.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Differentiate between GFRP and CFRP on the basis of (a) tensile strength, (b) modulus of elasticity, and (c) cost.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | GFRP (E-Glass) | CFRP (HS) |
|---|---|---|
| (a) Tensile Strength | 2400 MPa | 3500 MPa |
| (b) Modulus of Elasticity | 72 GPa | 230 GPa |
| (c) Cost | Low (4–5× cheaper) | High |
| Density | 2.55 g/cm³ | 1.78 g/cm³ |
| Typical Civil Application | Rebar, profiles, wraps | Prestressing, seismic retrofit |

**Conclusion:** CFRP offers superior mechanical performance but at significantly higher cost. GFRP is preferred for general civil applications where cost and corrosion resistance dominate over ultimate strength.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q. (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the constituents of an FRP composite. Discuss the role of fibers, matrix, and the interphase in load transfer. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

FRP composites are **two-phase heterogeneous materials** consisting of a reinforcement phase (fibers) embedded in a continuous matrix phase (polymer). A block diagram of constituents is shown below.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    comp["FRP COMPOSITE"]
    fib["FIBER PHASE High Strength"]
    mat["MATRIX PHASE Polymer"]
    intf["INTERPHASE Critical Boundary"]
    fibT["Glass, Carbon, Aramid, Basalt"]
    matT["Epoxy, Polyester, Vinyl Ester, Phenolic"]
    intfT["Mechanical, Chemical, Coupling Agent"]
    comp --> fib
    comp --> mat
    comp --> intf
    fib --> fibT
    mat --> matT
    intf --> intfT
```

**Role of Fibers:**
- Carry the **major portion of applied tensile load** (typically 70 – 90 %).
- Provide high **specific strength** and **specific stiffness**.
- Fibers are anisotropic, with much higher longitudinal strength than transverse.

**Role of Matrix:**
- **Transfers load** to fibers through interfacial shear stresses.
- **Protects** fibers from surface damage, moisture, and chemical attack.
- **Maintains fiber spacing and orientation**, preventing buckling under compression.
- Provides **transverse strength**, **shear strength**, and **toughness** to the composite.

**Role of Interphase:**
- The interphase is a thin region (typically 1 – 100 nm thick) where the fiber surface chemistry meets the bulk matrix.
- It governs the **efficiency of stress transfer** via mechanical interlocking, chemical bonding, and van der Waals forces.
- Poor interphase → fiber pull-out at low load → brittle failure with low strength.
- Strong interphase → fiber fracture at high load → high composite strength.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Marker Insight:** Drawing the block diagram clearly with arrows linking each phase to its sub-types earns at least **2 of the 7 marks**. The remaining 5 marks come from explaining *load transfer* explicitly using the term "interfacial shear stress."

---

**Q. (b)** Derive the expression for the longitudinal Young's modulus ($E_L$) of a unidirectional FRP composite using the **Rule of Mixtures**. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Assumptions (Voigt / Iso-strain model):**
1. Fibers are **continuous, parallel**, and aligned with the loading direction.
2. **Perfect bonding** exists at the fiber–matrix interface (no slip).
3. Both fiber and matrix experience the **same strain** in the loading direction.
4. Loads are applied along the fiber direction only.

**Derivation:**

Consider a unidirectional composite of length $L$ and uniform cross-section $A_c$ containing fibers of cross-section $A_f$ and matrix of cross-section $A_m$, such that $A_c = A_f + A_m$.

**Step 1 — Force equilibrium:**

$$P_{c} = P_{f} + P_{m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_{c} A_{c} = \sigma_{f} A_{f} + \sigma_{m} A_{m}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to volume fractions** (since lengths are equal):

$$V_{f} = \frac{A_{f}}{A_{c}}, \quad V_{m} = \frac{A_{m}}{A_{c}}, \quad V_{f} + V_{m} = 1$$

Therefore:

$$\sigma_{c} = \sigma_{f} V_{f} + \sigma_{m} V_{m}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Hooke's Law under iso-strain:**

$$\sigma_{f} = E_{f} \varepsilon_{1}, \quad \sigma_{m} = E_{m} \varepsilon_{1}, \quad \sigma_{c} = E_{L} \varepsilon_{1}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$E_{L} \varepsilon_{1} = E_{f} \varepsilon_{1} V_{f} + E_{m} \varepsilon_{1} V_{m}$$

Dividing throughout by $\varepsilon_1$:

$$\boxed{E_{L} = E_{f} V_{f} + E_{m} V_{m}}$$

**Conclusion:** The longitudinal modulus is a **linear weighted average** of the fiber and matrix moduli, weighted by their volume fractions. Higher $V_f$ and stiffer fibers increase $E_L$ proportionally.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – May 2022]

**Q. (a)** Compare **GFRP, CFRP, and AFRP** with respect to tensile strength, modulus, density, cost, and typical applications. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | GFRP (E-Glass) | CFRP (HS) | AFRP (Kevlar 49) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tensile Strength (MPa)** | 2400 | 3500 | 3620 |
| **Tensile Modulus (GPa)** | 72 | 230 | 124 |
| **Density (g/cm³)** | 2.55 | 1.78 | 1.44 |
| **Strain to Failure (%)** | 4.0 | 1.5 | 2.5 |
| **Cost (Relative)** | 1× (cheapest) | 8 – 10× | 6 – 8× |
| **Corrosion Resistance** | Good (alkali-sensitive) | Excellent | Good (UV-sensitive) |
| **Fatigue Resistance** | Moderate | Excellent | Very Good |
| **Typical Civil Applications** | Rebar, profiles, wraps, manhole covers | Prestressing tendons, seismic retrofit, high-performance wraps | Impact-resistant panels, blast-resistant structures |
| **Failure Mode** | Brittle, fiber pull-out | Brittle, fiber fracture | Ductile-looking, fibrillation |

**Conclusions:**
- **GFRP** is the workhorse of civil FRP — economical, adequate strength, widely used for rebars and strengthening.
- **CFRP** offers the best strength and stiffness, used where performance justifies cost (seismic retrofit of critical structures, prestressing).
- **AFRP** offers a balance of toughness and strength, used where impact resistance is critical (blast, ballistic).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Loss:** Students write "CFRP is the strongest" — this is **wrong**. AFRP and high-grade S-glass can have **higher tensile strength** than standard CFRP. The correct hierarchy is: **Stiffness: CFRP > AFRP > GFRP; Tensile strength: AFRP ≈ S-Glass > CFRP-HS > GFRP.**

---

**Q. (b)** A unidirectional GFRP composite has fiber volume fraction $V_f = 0.40$, fiber modulus $E_f = 70$ GPa, matrix modulus $E_m = 3.0$ GPa. Determine the **(i)** longitudinal modulus, **(ii)** transverse modulus, and **(iii)** the **anisotropy ratio**. Comment on the result. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $V_f = 0.40$, $V_m = 1 - 0.40 = 0.60$, $E_f = 70$ GPa, $E_m = 3.0$ GPa.

**(i) Longitudinal Modulus (E_L):**

$$E_{L} = E_{f} V_{f} + E_{m} V_{m}$$

$$E_{L} = (70 \times 0.40) + (3.0 \times 0.60)$$

$$E_{L} = 28.0 + 1.80 = 29.80 \text{ GPa}$$

**(ii) Transverse Modulus (E_T):**

$$\frac{1}{E_{T}} = \frac{V_{f}}{E_{f}} + \frac{V_{m}}{E_{m}}$$

$$\frac{1}{E_{T}} = \frac{0.40}{70} + \frac{0.60}{3.0} = 0.00571 + 0.200 = 0.20571 \text{ (GPa)}^{-1}$$

$$E_{T} = \frac{1}{0.20571} = 4.86 \text{ GPa}$$

**(iii) Anisotropy Ratio:**

$$\frac{E_{L}}{E_{T}} = \frac{29.80}{4.86} = 6.13$$

**Comment:** The anisotropy ratio of **6.13** indicates that the composite is approximately **6× stiffer along the fiber direction** than transverse to it. This strong directional dependence is a defining feature of unidirectional FRP and dictates that fibers must be **oriented along the principal stress direction** for efficient structural use. For multi-directional loading, fabrics (woven roving) or cross-ply laminates are preferred.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to compute $V_m$** (assuming it to be equal to $V_f$) — costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing up longitudinal and transverse formulas** — the longitudinal is a *weighted sum*, the transverse is a *weighted harmonic mean*. This swap is a guaranteed 3-mark loss.
> 3. **Skipping the comment on anisotropy** — the final interpretive remark carries 1–2 marks. Always state a *quantitative* engineering implication.
> 4. **Units:** GPa for modulus, dimensionless for ratios. Mixing units leads to silent mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 2 — FRP Composites)**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** FRP = Fiber + Polymer Matrix; two-phase heterogeneous, anisotropic material.
>
> ✅ **Three Constituents:** Fibers (load carrier), Matrix (binder + protector), Interphase (load transfer).
>
> ✅ **Common Fibers:** Glass (GFRP), Carbon (CFRP), Aramid (AFRP), Basalt (BFRP).
>
> ✅ **Common Matrices:** Epoxy, Polyester, Vinyl Ester, Phenolic.
>
> ✅ **Rule of Mixtures — Longitudinal:** $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ (iso-strain).
>
> ✅ **Rule of Mixtures — Transverse:** $1/E_T = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$ (iso-stress).
>
> ✅ **Critical Fiber Length:** $L_c = \sigma_{fu} d_f / (2 \tau_i)$ — governs fiber pull-out vs. fracture.
>
> ✅ **Typical $V_f$ range:** 0.30 – 0.65 (structural FRP).
>
> ✅ **Anisotropy ratio $E_L/E_T$ typically 5 – 10** for unidirectional GFRP.
>
> ✅ **CFRP highest modulus (230 – 390 GPa); GFRP lowest cost; AFRP best impact resistance.**
>
> ✅ **FRP is linear-elastic to failure** — no yielding. Design governed by ultimate strain and environmental reduction factor $\eta$.
>
> ✅ **Civil applications:** External strengthening (EB-FRP), GFRP rebar in marine structures, pultruded profiles, bridge decks, seismic retrofit.
>
> ✅ **Manufacturing routes for civil FRP:** Hand lay-up (most common), pultrusion (rebar/profiles), filament winding (pipes), RTM/VARTM (high-quality laminates).
>
> ✅ **Key disadvantages:** High initial cost (CFRP), brittle failure, poor fire resistance, long-term creep, difficulty of on-site quality control.
>
> ✅ **Design codes to know:** ACI 440.2R (External Strengthening), ACI 440.1R (GFRP Rebar), IS 16141, fib Bulletin 40 (FRP for Concrete Structures).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bond Strength Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT758 - POLYMERS IN CONSTRUCTION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Bond Strength Models # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Bond Strength in Polymer Construction? In polymer-modified concrete (PMC), polymer-modified mortar (PMM), and fibre-reinforced polymer (FRP) strengthening systems, **bond strength** is defined as the maximum sustained stress at the interface between the polymeric material and the substrate (cementitious matrix, steel, brick, or existing concrete) before failure occurs. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** > Bond strength is the interfacial resistance (measured in **MPa** or **N/mm²**) offered by an adhesive polymer film against separation from a cementitious or metallic substrate under mechanical, thermal, or chemical loading. There are **three principal modes** of bond stress: | Mode | Symbol | Description | Typical Test | |---|---|---|---| | **Tensile Bond (Pull-Off)** | $\sigma_t$ | Stress normal to interface | Direct tension / Pull-off | | **Shear Bond** | $\tau$ | Stress tangential to interface | Slant-shear, single-lap shear | | **Flexural Bond** | $\sigma_f$ | Stress from bending | Four-point bending | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Velcro vs. Glue" Imagine two surfaces stuck together. One approach uses **mechanical hooks (Velcro)** — physical interlocking. The other uses **chemical glue** — molecular attraction. Polymer-concrete bonding is a *hybrid* of both: - The polymer film flows into micro-pores of concrete, then hardens → **mechanical interlock (anchoring)**. - Simultaneously, polar groups (–OH, –COO⁻, –CONH₂) form covalent, hydrogen, or van der Waals bonds with calcium silicate hydrate (C–S–H) and Ca(OH)₂ crystals → **specific adhesion**. > [!NOTE] > Think of polymer-cement bond as **"tape on a textured wall"**. The smoother the wall (well-prepared substrate), the better the surface contact (wetting), but some texture is essential for micromechanical anchoring. Polymer science balances these two contributions through **adhesion models**. ## 1.3 Why Bond Models Matter in Construction Bond strength governs the **load-transfer efficiency** in: - **Polymer-modified renders & plasters** (adhesion to masonry) - **Epoxy-resin bonded anchors & grouts** - **FRP laminates** externally bonded for retrofitting (EB-FRP) - **Polymer-cement tile adhesives** (IS 15477 / EN 12004 classifications) - **Sealants and joint fillers** > [!TIP] > For KTU, remember that **bond strength is NOT a material property alone**; it is a *system property* depending on substrate, polymer, surface preparation, environment, and loading rate. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theories of Adhesion — The Four Pillars Bond between polymer and cementitious substrate is explained by **four classical theories**, often acting simultaneously: ### (a) Mechanical Interlocking Theory - Polymer penetrates open pores, micro-cracks, and surface roughness. - After curing, it acts as **microscopic anchors**. - Governed by surface texture (e.g., sandblasted, bush-hammered). - Best described by **anchor depth (h)** and **pore density (n)**. ### (b) Chemical (Specific) Bonding Theory - Covalent, ionic, or hydrogen bonding at the molecular interface. - Epoxy groups react with silanol (Si–OH) groups on C–S–H surfaces. - Polyacrylates (PVA, SBR latex) form hydrogen bonds with Ca²⁺ ions and C–S–H. - Produces **thermodynamically irreversible** strong bonds (≈ 100–400 kJ/mol). ### (c) Thermodynamic (Wetting/ Adsorption) Theory - Work of adhesion $\;W_A = \gamma_S + \gamma_L - \gamma_{SL}\;$ (Dupré equation). - Spreading coefficient $\;S = \gamma_S - \gamma_L - \gamma_{SL}\;$. - Lower contact angle θ ⇒ better wetting ⇒ higher practical adhesion. - Governed by **surface energy of substrate ($\gamma_S$)** and polymer ($\gamma_L$). ### (d) Diffusion (Interpenetration) Theory - Polymer chains inter-diffuse across the interface over a depth *X*. - Time and temperature dependent (often obeys Fick's laws). - Predominant in **thermoplastic polymers** above their $T_g$. - Less significant for thermoset epoxy systems at room temperature. > [!NOTE] > For **epoxy-concrete** systems, **(a) + (c)** dominate. For **latex-modified mortars**, **(a) + (b) + (d)** all contribute. ## 2.2 Failure Modes at the Interface The bond failure can be classified as: 1. **Adhesive failure** — separation exactly at the polymer-substrate interface (poor wetting or contamination). 2. **Cohesive failure** — rupture within the weaker bulk layer (either polymer or substrate). 3. **Mixed (combination) failure** — most common in practice; occurs in both layers. > [!WARNING] > **Cohesive failure within substrate (concrete) is DESIRABLE.** It indicates that the bond strength EXCEEDS the substrate tensile strength — the system is bond-limited by the weaker side, not the adhesive. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Equation | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\sigma_t = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | Direct tensile / pull-off bond strength | MPa | | 2 | $\tau = \dfrac{F}{A_{shear}}$ | Shear bond stress (slant-shear / push-out) | MPa | | 3 | $W_A = \gamma_S + \gamma_L - \gamma_{SL}$ | Dupré work of adhesion | mJ/m² | | 4 | $W_A = \gamma_L(1 + \cos\theta)$ | Young–Dupré equation | mJ/m² | | 5 | $S = \gamma_S - \gamma_L - \gamma_{SL}$ | Spreading coefficient | mJ/m² | | 6 | $\tau(s) = \tau_{\max} \left(\dfrac{s}{s_0}\right)^{\alpha}$ | Popovics-type bond-slip ascending branch | MPa | | 7 | $\tau(s) = \tau_{\max} \left(1-\dfrac{s}{s_u}\right)$ | Linear descending branch (simplified) | MPa | | 8 | $\ell_b = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \dfrac{f_t}{\tau_{avg}}$ | Effective bond length (FRP rod anchor) | mm | | 9 | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{4P}{\pi d^2} \sin^2\theta$ | Slant-shear compressive bond strength | MPa | | 10 | $G_f = \int_0^{s_u} \tau(s)\, ds$ | Interfacial fracture energy | J/m² | > **Key Constants for KTU:** > - Concrete tensile strength $f_t \approx 0.35\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ (Eurocode 2) > - Contact angle θ for epoxy on dry concrete ≈ 10°–25° > - Glass transition temperature $T_g$ of epoxy ≈ 60 °C–80 °C ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Field | Application | Bond Strength Required | |---|---|---| | **Retrofit & Strengthening** | EB-FRP plates on beams | ≥ 1.5 MPa (pull-off) | | **Tile Adhesives (C2TE)** | Polymer-modified tile mortar | ≥ 1.0 MPa (EN 12004) | | **Concrete Repair (R3/R4)** | Polymer mortar patches | ≥ 1.5 MPa after 28 days | | **Bridge Bearings** | Epoxy-grouted anchor bolts | ≥ 7 MPa (shear) | | **Sandwich Panels** | Polymer foam to concrete skin | ≥ 0.25 MPa | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Young–Dupré Equation The classical Young–Dupré equation links **contact angle (θ)**, **interfacial energy ($\gamma_{SL}$)**, **solid surface energy ($\gamma_S$)** and **liquid surface energy ($\gamma_L$)**. ### Step 1 — Mechanical equilibrium at the contact line At the three-phase boundary (solid–liquid–vapour), the vector sum of surface tensions must balance. $$\gamma_S \;=\; \gamma_{SL} \;+\; \gamma_L \cos\theta$$ **Logic:** The solid surface pulls outward (tension $\gamma_S$), the liquid surface pulls along its tangent (tension $\gamma_L \cos\theta$ resolved normal to the solid), and the interfacial tension $\gamma_{SL}$ resists wetting. ### Step 2 — Rearrange for $\gamma_{SL}$ Subtract $\gamma_{SL}$ from both sides: $$\gamma_S \;-\; \gamma_{SL} \;=\; \gamma_L \cos\theta$$ ### Step 3 — Substitute into Dupré's work of adhesion Dupré defined the **work of adhesion** (energy per unit area to separate two phases in vacuum) as: $$W_A \;=\; \gamma_S \;+\; \gamma_L \;-\; \gamma_{SL}$$ **Logic:** To create two new free surfaces ($\gamma_S$ + $\gamma_L$) you must destroy the interface ($\gamma_{SL}$); the deficit equals the work done. ### Step 4 — Eliminate $\gamma_{SL}$ Using $\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_S - \gamma_L \cos\theta$ from Step 2: $$W_A \;=\; \gamma_S \;+\; \gamma_L \;-\; \left[\gamma_S \;-\; \gamma_L \cos\theta\right]$$ $$W_A \;=\; \gamma_L \;+\; \gamma_L \cos\theta$$ $$\boxed{\,W_A \;=\; \gamma_L (1 + \cos\theta)\,}$$ **Engineering interpretation:** Smaller θ ⇒ higher $W_A$ ⇒ better practical adhesion. For θ = 0° (perfect wetting), $W_A = 2\gamma_L$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Slant-Shear Bond Strength In the **slant-shear test (ASTM C882)**, a polymer mortar is cast in the upper half of a prism at an angle $\theta$ from horizontal, with concrete substrate in the lower half. Compressive load $P$ creates both normal and shear stress on the bond plane. ### Step 1 — Geometry The bonded interface has area: $$A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4 \sin\theta}$$ where *d* is the cylinder diameter and θ is the slant angle (typically **30°** in ASTM C882). ### Step 2 — Stress decomposition The compressive force $P$ acting along the cylinder axis can be resolved: - Normal to interface: $N = P \sin\theta$ - Parallel to interface: $V = P \cos\theta$ ### Step 3 — Resultant bond stress The **apparent bond strength** (combined normal + shear, dominated by shear) is: $$\sigma_c \;=\; \dfrac{P}{A \sin\theta}$$ Substituting A: $$\sigma_c \;=\; \dfrac{P \sin\theta}{\dfrac{\pi d^2}{4 \sin\theta} \cdot \sin\theta}$$ $$\boxed{\;\sigma_c \;=\; \dfrac{4P}{\pi d^2}\,\dfrac{1}{\sin^2\theta}\;}$$ **Sanity check:** For θ = 30°, sin²θ = 0.25, so $\sigma_c = 16P/(\pi d^2)$ ≈ 5 × pure uniaxial compressive stress — explaining why slant-shear magnifies bond sensitivity. --- ## 3.3 Derivation of Bond Slip Model — Popovics-Type Curve For FRP-concrete or polymer-concrete interfaces, the local bond stress $\tau(s)$ at slip *s* is often described by: ### Ascending branch (0 ≤ s ≤ s₀): $$\tau(s) \;=\; \tau_{\max}\!\left(\dfrac{s}{s_0}\right)^{\alpha}, \quad 0 \le \alpha \le 1$$ ### Descending branch (s₀ ≤ s ≤ sᵤ): $$\tau(s) \;=\; \tau_{\max}\!\left(1-\dfrac{s - s_0}{s_u - s_0}\right), \quad \text{(linear softening)}$$ ### Step — Interfacial fracture energy $G_f$ Integrate the τ–s curve over the slip range: $$\begin{aligned} G_f &= \int_{0}^{s_0}\tau_{\max}\!\left(\dfrac{s}{s_0}\right)^{\alpha} ds \;+\; \int_{s_0}^{s_u}\tau_{\max}\!\left(1-\dfrac{s - s_0}{s_u - s_0}\right) ds \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{\tau_{\max}\, s_0}{\alpha + 1} \;+\; \dfrac{\tau_{\max}(s_u - s_0)}{2} \end{aligned}$$ **Logic:** First term = work during elastic-bond phase; second term = frictional pull-out phase. $G_f$ is the **interfacial fracture energy**, a key parameter in fracture-mechanics based bond models (Holzenkämpfer, Neubauer, Nakaba). --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Bond Models | Model | Origin | Key Parameter | Best For | |---|---|---|---| | **Dupré / Young–Dupré** | Surface thermodynamics | $\gamma_S$, $\gamma_L$, θ | Wetting prediction, initial bond | | **McBain–Wake (Interpenetration)** | Diffusion kinetics | Diffusion coeff. D | Latex-modified systems | | **Holzenkämpfer (FRP)** | Fracture mechanics | $G_f$, $\beta_w$ | EB-FRP plates | | **Nakaba–Kanakubo** | Fracture mechanics | $B$ (softening exponent) | Carbon-FRP strips | | **Popovics bond-slip** | Empirical fit | α, s₀, sᵤ | General polymer-concrete | | **Lu et al. bilinear** | Simplified bilinear | $\tau_{\max}$, $s_u$ | Design codes (fib 14) | | **Sawada (multi-linear)** | Refined softening | Plateau + descent | High-precision FE | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Bond Strength Evaluation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Substrate Surface
Concrete / Steel / Brick] --> B[Surface Preparation
Cleaning + Roughening] B --> C[Polymer Application
Epoxy / Latex / Acrylic] C --> D[Wetting and Penetration
Contact Angle theta + Pore Flow] D --> E[Curing Phase
Polymerization or Drying] E --> F{Interfacial Zone Forms} F --> G[Mechanical Interlock
Anchor Holes] F --> H[Chemical Bonds
Covalent and H-Bond] F --> I[Thermodynamic Adsorption
Surface Energy Balance] F --> J[Diffusion Interlayer
Chain Entanglement] G --> K[Bond Strength
Sigma t and tau] H --> K I --> K J --> K K --> L{Loading Mode} L --> M[Tensile Bond
Pull-Off Test] L --> N[Shear Bond
Slant Shear Test] L --> O[Flexural Bond
Bending Test] M --> P[Result Classification] N --> P O --> P P --> Q[Adhesive Failure] P --> R[Cohesive Failure] P --> S[Mixed Failure] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Bond-Slip Constitutive Model ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Slip s equals 0] --> B[Linear Elastic Rise] B --> C[Peak tau max at slip s0] C --> D[Frictional Plateau] D --> E[Softening Branch] E --> F[Residual Friction at slip su] F --> G[Complete Debonding] ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Hierarchy of Adhesion Theories ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Adhesion Theories in Polymers] --> B[Mechanical Interlock] A --> C[Chemical Bonding] A --> D[Thermodynamic Wetting] A --> E[Diffusion Theory] A --> F[Electrostatic Theory] B --> G[Anchor Depth h and Porosity] C --> H[Covalent and Ionic and H-Bond] D --> I[Dupre Young Equation] E --> J[Fickian Diffusion] F --> K[Charge Transfer at Interface] G --> L[Bond Strength Outcome] H --> L I --> L J --> L K --> L ``` ## 4.4 Schematic — Failure Mode Classification ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Applied Load on Bonded Joint] --> B{Where does Failure Initiate} B --> C[At Interface
Adhesive Failure] B --> D[Inside Polymer
Cohesive Failure in Adhesive] B --> E[Inside Substrate
Cohesive Failure in Concrete] C --> F[Low Bond Strength
Poor Surface Prep] D --> G[Acceptable Bond
Polymer is Weakest Layer] E --> H[Excellent Bond
Substrate is Weakest Layer] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2] **State and explain the Young–Dupré equation. How is it used to predict bond between epoxy and concrete?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Young–Dupré equation** relates the thermodynamic work of adhesion ($W_A$) to the contact angle (θ) and the liquid surface tension ($\gamma_L$): $$W_A = \gamma_L (1 + \cos\theta)$$ **Logic (1 mark):** When liquid epoxy is applied to concrete, it forms a contact angle θ. Smaller θ means better wetting. The work of adhesion represents the energy per unit area required to separate the epoxy from the substrate. **Application (1 mark):** By measuring θ experimentally (using a sessile drop goniometer) and $\gamma_L$ (Wilhelmy plate), $W_A$ can be estimated. For epoxy on sound concrete, θ ≈ 15° and $\gamma_L \approx 45$ mJ/m², giving $W_A \approx 88.5$ mJ/m² — high practical adhesion. **Predictive use (1 mark):** If the surface is contaminated or oil-soaked, θ rises above 60°, and $W_A$ falls below 67 mJ/m² — flagged as poor bond; surface preparation is needed. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2] **Distinguish between adhesive and cohesive failure of a bonded polymer-concrete joint. Which is preferred and why?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Adhesive Failure | Cohesive Failure | |---|---|---| | **Location of crack** | Exactly at the interface | Within the bulk of either layer | | **Indication** | Poor surface prep / contamination | Polymer–substrate bond exceeds weaker bulk strength | | **Surface appearance** | Smooth, no fibre tearing | Rough, with polymer residue or concrete particles | | **Preferred?** | NO | YES | **Preferred (1 mark):** **Cohesive failure in the substrate (concrete)** is the most desirable outcome in construction practice. It proves the bond strength of the polymer exceeds the tensile strength of the concrete substrate itself (~ 1.5–3 MPa), confirming a robust, system-limited joint rather than a bond-limited one. --- ## PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2] **A (a)** Describe in detail the **four classical theories of adhesion** between polymers and cementitious substrates. For each theory, give one construction example where it dominates. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer A(a):** **(i) Mechanical Interlocking Theory (2 marks)** According to this theory, adhesion arises from the penetration of liquid polymer into micro-pores, capillaries, and surface irregularities of the substrate. Upon hardening, it forms microscopic anchors that resist tensile and shear separation. The bond strength is proportional to the **pore depth (h)** and **surface roughness (Ra)**. *Dominant in:* Polymer-modified cementitious renders on brick masonry — polymer latex flows into brick pores and locks mechanically. **(ii) Chemical (Specific) Bonding Theory (2 marks)** This involves primary (covalent) and secondary (hydrogen, ionic, van der Waals) bonds at the molecular interface. For instance, epoxy resins form covalent C–N bonds with –NH₂ groups in hardeners, while –COOH groups in acrylic latexes bond with Ca²⁺ ions from C–S–H. Strength ≈ 100–400 kJ/mol for covalent bonds. *Dominant in:* Epoxy-bonded anchor bolts in concrete — primary covalent bonds form at the epoxy-concrete molecular interface. **(iii) Thermodynamic (Wetting/Adsorption) Theory (1.5 marks)** The Young–Dupré work of adhesion $W_A = \gamma_L (1 + \cos\theta)$ governs the contact. Lower contact angle ⇒ better wetting ⇒ higher practical adhesion. Surface energy match between polymer and substrate is critical. *Dominant in:* Self-levelling epoxy floor coatings — they wet smooth, dense, polished concrete surfaces by virtue of low contact angle. **(iv) Diffusion (Interpenetration) Theory (1.5 marks)** Polymer chains diffuse across the interface over a depth *X* (~1–100 nm). Predominant above glass transition temperature $T_g$, follows Fickian kinetics with diffusion coefficient *D*. Not significant for room-temperature-cured thermosets but vital in latex films forming on cementitious substrates. *Dominant in:* SBR-latex modified cementitious waterproofing coatings — SBR chains interdiffuse with hydrated cement paste. --- **A (b)** Derive the **Young–Dupré equation** starting from the force-balance condition at the three-phase contact line. Show that perfect wetting (θ = 0°) leads to $W_A = 2\gamma_L$. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer A(b):** **Step 1 (2 marks):** Mechanical equilibrium of surface tensions at the three-phase line (solid–liquid–vapour) gives Young's equation: $$\gamma_S = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_L \cos\theta$$ This comes from vector force balance — solid surface tension pulls outward, liquid surface tension acts tangentially, and interfacial tension acts along the interface. **Step 2 (1 mark):** Rearrange to obtain $\gamma_{SL}$: $$\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_S - \gamma_L \cos\theta$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Dupré's thermodynamic work of adhesion is defined as: $$W_A = \gamma_S + \gamma_L - \gamma_{SL}$$ This represents the energy needed to create two new surfaces ($\gamma_S + \gamma_L$) and destroy the interface ($\gamma_{SL}$). **Step 4 (2 marks):** Substituting the expression for $\gamma_{SL}$: $$\begin{aligned} W_A &= \gamma_S + \gamma_L - (\gamma_S - \gamma_L \cos\theta) \\ &= \gamma_L + \gamma_L \cos\theta \\ &= \gamma_L (1 + \cos\theta) \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Special case of perfect wetting: θ = 0° ⇒ cosθ = 1. $$\boxed{W_A = \gamma_L (1 + 1) = 2\gamma_L}$$ This is the **maximum possible work of adhesion**, occurring when the polymer spreads spontaneously over the substrate. --- ### QUESTION B (Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2] **B (a)** Explain the **slant-shear bond test** (ASTM C882). Derive the expression for slant-shear bond strength in terms of applied load *P*, specimen diameter *d*, and slant angle θ. Why is θ = 30° preferred? **(7 marks)** **Model Answer B(a):** **Test Description (3 marks):** The slant-shear test evaluates the bond between polymer mortar (overlay) and concrete substrate under combined compression and shear. A cylindrical specimen (75 mm × 150 mm typically) is cast such that the bonded plane is inclined at angle θ (commonly 30°) from the horizontal. A uniaxial compressive load *P* is applied, producing simultaneous normal and shear stresses on the bonded interface. The bonded area for diameter *d* is: $$A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4 \sin\theta}$$ The failure load $P_{max}$ is recorded. **Derivation (3 marks):** Resolving *P* normal and tangential to the bond plane: - Normal component: $N = P \sin\theta$ - Shear component: $V = P \cos\theta$ The slant-shear bond strength (apparent, dominated by shear) is: $$\sigma_c = \dfrac{P}{A \sin\theta} = \dfrac{4P \sin\theta}{\pi d^2 \sin^2\theta} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sin\theta} = \dfrac{4P}{\pi d^2 \sin^2\theta}$$ **Why θ = 30°? (1 mark):** - At 30°, sin²θ = 0.25, magnifying the bond stress by 4× → high sensitivity. - Below 25°, the failure mode becomes predominantly compressive (not bond-specific). - Above 45°, the bonded area becomes small and stress concentrations dominate. - Hence **30°** is the **ASTM-recommended optimum** for bond discrimination. --- **B (b)** A **pull-off bond test** was conducted on a polymer-modified repair mortar applied to an existing concrete substrate. The applied tensile load at failure was **P = 8.5 kN**, and the steel dolly diameter was **d = 50 mm**. Compute the pull-off tensile bond strength in MPa. Comment on whether the result is acceptable for an **R4-class repair** as per EN 1504-3. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer B(b):** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Area of the steel dolly: $$A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (50)^2}{4} = 1963.5\ \text{mm}^2$$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Pull-off tensile bond strength: $$\sigma_t = \dfrac{P}{A} = \dfrac{8500\ \text{N}}{1963.5\ \text{mm}^2} = 4.33\ \text{MPa}$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Compare with EN 1504-3: EN 1504-3 specifies minimum **≥ 1.5 MPa** (R3 class) and **≥ 2.0 MPa** (R4 class) for structural repair mortars. **Step 4 (1 mark):** **Comment:** $$\sigma_t = 4.33\ \text{MPa} \;\geq\; 2.0\ \text{MPa (R4 requirement)} \;✓$$ The result **comfortably exceeds** the R4-class requirement (≈ 116% above minimum). Provided failure mode is **cohesive in substrate** (which should be verified visually), the polymer-modified repair mortar is **structurally acceptable** for load-bearing restoration. **Step 5 (1 mark):** Failure mode assessment: The student should visually inspect the fracture surface. Acceptable outcomes: - **Substrate cohesive failure** (preferred) — bond strength exceeds concrete tensile strength $f_t \approx 0.35\sqrt{f_{ck}}$. - **Polymer cohesive failure** — acceptable if adhesive bond is intact. - **Adhesive failure at interface** — not acceptable; surface prep was inadequate. **Step 6 (1 mark):** Additional notes: Pull-off strength > concrete tensile strength is a quality control criterion in IS 15916 and ACI 546R for repair works. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** > 1. **Confusing $W_A$ with practical bond strength** — $W_A$ is interfacial energy (mJ/m²), not stress (MPa). They are related through fracture mechanics but not numerically equal. > 2. **Using wrong slant-shear formula** — students often write $P/A$ directly, forgetting the $\sin^2\theta$ term. This gives an **incorrect 4× over-estimation** at θ = 30°. > 3. **Forgetting units in Pull-Off test** — load must be in **Newtons**, area in **mm²**, giving bond strength in **N/mm² = MPa** directly. > 4. **Cohesive vs. Adhesive** confusion in answers — examiners specifically check if students identify which layer failed. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER - **Bond strength is a SYSTEM property** — substrate + polymer + surface + environment. - The **4 adhesion theories**: Mechanical, Chemical, Thermodynamic (Wetting), Diffusion. - **Young–Dupré equation:** $W_A = \gamma_L (1 + \cos\theta)$ — perfect wetting → $W_A = 2\gamma_L$. - **Pull-off test:** $\sigma_t = P/A$ — simplest direct bond measure. - **Slant-shear:** $\sigma_c = 4P/(\pi d^2 \sin^2\theta)$ — magnifies bond sensitivity 4× at 30°. - **Bond-slip model:** $\tau = \tau_{\max}(s/s_0)^\alpha$ ascending + linear softening descending. - **Fracture energy:** $G_f = \int_0^{s_u}\tau(s)\, ds$ — central to FRP bond design (fib 14 / ACI 440.2R). - **Cohesive substrate failure > cohesive polymer failure > adhesive failure** (preference order). - **Contact angle θ < 30°** is generally required for adequate practical wetting on dry concrete. - **EN 1504-3:** R4 repair mortar pull-off ≥ **2.0 MPa**. - **ASTM C882:** 30° slant angle, 75×150 mm cylinder, polymer mortar on concrete half. - **FRP bond length:** $\ell_b = (\pi d^2/4)(f_t/\tau_{avg})$ — effective anchorage beyond which no benefit. - **Popovics exponent α** typically **0.5–1.0** for FRP-concrete; **< 0.5** indicates brittle interface. - **Key models to remember:** Popovics (empirical), Holzenkämpfer (fracture-mech FRP), Lu et al. (bilinear), Nakaba–Kanakubo (softening). - **Adhesion-promoter primers** (e.g., silane coupling agents) act at the *thermodynamic* level by reducing θ. - For KTU: always pair **theory + numerical example** to score full marks in 14-mark questions.

topics.title: Strengthening of RC Beams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT758 - POLYMERS IN CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Strengthening of RC Beams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Strengthening of RC Beams** is the process of restoring or enhancing the load-carrying capacity, stiffness, ductility, and serviceability of existing reinforced concrete (RC) flexural members whose structural performance has deteriorated due to factors such as corrosion of steel reinforcement, increased service loads, design/code revisions, accidental damage, construction defects, or seismic deficiency. In the context of **Polymers in Construction**, strengthening is predominantly achieved through the application of **Fiber Reinforced Polymer (FRP) composites** and **polymer-modified repair mortars**, which are bonded to the tension face, sides, or wrapping the cross-section of the beam.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus (PEPOT758 Module 2):**
> The course emphasizes *polymer-based* strengthening. Students must understand the role of **epoxy resins**, **FRP laminates/wraps**, **polymer-modified cementitious mortars**, and **structural adhesives** in beam rehabilitation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine an old wooden table whose leg has become weak and wobbly. You don't throw the table away — you **wrap a steel band or apply fiberglass bandage** around the weak leg, glue it firmly, and the leg becomes stronger than before. 

**Strengthening an RC beam is exactly this idea**, but at an engineering scale:
- The **concrete beam** = the weak table leg
- The **steel reinforcement inside** = the original structure
- The **FRP (Fiber Reinforced Polymer) wrap/laminate** = the steel band you wrapped around it
- The **epoxy resin adhesive** = the glue that bonds them together
- The **polymer mortar** = the filler that repairs surface damage first

> [!NOTE]
> **Why polymers?**
> Polymers (especially epoxies and FRPs) offer: high **strength-to-weight ratio** (≈5–10× steel), **corrosion resistance**, **ease of application**, **minimal added dead weight**, and the ability to be applied in **restricted access sites** where traditional concrete jacketing is impractical.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Material Properties

| Material | Tensile Strength (MPa) | Elastic Modulus (GPa) | Density (kg/m³) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mild Steel** | 250–500 | 200 | 7850 |
| **CFRP (Carbon FRP)** | 1500–3500 | 150–500 | 1600 |
| **GFRP (Glass FRP)** | 500–1500 | 30–55 | 1900–2100 |
| **AFRP (Aramid FRP)** | 1000–2500 | 50–130 | 1250–1400 |
| **Epoxy Resin (cured)** | 30–90 | 2–6 | 1100–1200 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway:** Although polymers have lower modulus than steel in some cases (e.g., GFRP), their **corrosion immunity** and **ease of installation** make them the preferred choice in aggressive environments (coastal Kerala, chemical plants, bridge piers).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Do RC Beams Need Strengthening?

The structural deficiencies that trigger strengthening can be grouped into four categories:

1. **Deterioration-based** — corrosion of rebar, carbonation, chloride ingress, freeze-thaw damage, alkali-silica reaction (ASR).
2. **Demand-based** — change in building use, installation of heavy machinery, increased live loads, traffic upgradation on bridges.
3. **Deficiency-based** — original design error, insufficient reinforcement, outdated code provisions (e.g., pre-IS 456 designs lacking seismic ductility).
4. **Damage-based** — fire exposure, impact, earthquake damage, blast loading.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 expects students to **diagnose the root cause first** before selecting a strengthening method. A corroded beam cannot be FRP-wrapped without first arresting the corrosion and repairing the cover concrete.

### 2.2 Polymer-Based Strengthening Methods — Operational Logic

The four primary polymer-based strengthening techniques for RC beams are:

#### A. Externally Bonded FRP (EB-FRP) Reinforcement
- FRP fabric/laminate is bonded to the **tension face** (for flexure) or **sides/web** (for shear) using epoxy adhesive.
- Used for both **flexural** and **shear** strengthening.
- Most common technique globally.

#### B. Near-Surface Mounted (NSM) FRP
- A groove is cut into the concrete cover of the beam.
- FRP bar/laminate is inserted into the groove and **embedded in epoxy paste**.
- Provides superior bond and protection against debonding and fire.

#### C. FRP Wrapping / Jacketing (for shear and confinement)
- FRP fabric is wrapped around the **cross-section** of the beam (U-wrap or full wrap).
- Provides shear strengthening and confinement of compressed concrete.

#### D. Polymer-Modified Cementitious Overlays
- The damaged cover concrete is removed and replaced with **polymer-modified mortar** (epoxy mortar, SBR/latex-modified mortar, acrylic-modified mortar).
- Restores section dimensions and passivates the rebar environment.

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Method Selection

| Condition | Recommended Method | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| **Flexural deficiency only** | EB-FRP on tension face | Maximum lever arm; efficient use of FRP |
| **Shear deficiency** | U-wrap or full FRP wrap | Mimics external stirrups; acts in tension |
| **Limited access / negative moment region** | NSM FRP | Discrete, hidden, superior bond |
| **Severe corrosion damage** | Polymer mortar repair + EB-FRP | Mortar restores section; FRP adds capacity |
| **Active corrosion ongoing** | Avoid FRP until corrosion arrest | FRP can mask ongoing corrosion (incompatible repair) |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are based on **ACI 440.2R-17 / IS 15912 / fib Bulletin 14** design recommendations. These are the most-tested formulas for KTU examinations.

#### Flexural Strengthening (EB-FRP on Tension Face)

$$M_n = A_s f_s \left(d - \frac{a}{2}\right) + A_f f_f \left(d_f - \frac{a}{2}\right)$$

$$a = \frac{A_s f_s + A_f f_f}{0.85 f_c' b}$$

**Strain compatibility in FRP:**

$$\varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_{cu} \left(\frac{d_f - c}{c}\right) - \varepsilon_{bi}$$

where:
- $A_s$ = area of steel reinforcement
- $A_f$ = area of FRP reinforcement
- $f_s$ = stress in steel at ultimate
- $f_f$ = effective stress in FRP
- $f_c'$ = compressive strength of concrete
- $d, d_f$ = effective depths of steel and FRP
- $\varepsilon_{bi}$ = substrate strain at time of FRP installation (important — substrate already carries load)
- $c$ = neutral axis depth

#### Shear Strengthening (FRP Wraps)

$$V_n = V_c + V_s + V_f$$

$$V_f = \frac{A_f f_f (\sin\alpha + \cos\alpha) d_f}{s_f}$$

where:
- $V_c$ = concrete contribution
- $V_s$ = steel stirrup contribution
- $V_f$ = FRP contribution
- $A_f$ = area of FRP shear reinforcement
- $s_f$ = spacing of FRP strips
- $\alpha$ = angle of FRP fibers with beam axis

#### Effective FRP Stress Limit (Debonding Control)

$$f_{fe} = E_f \varepsilon_{fe}$$

$$\varepsilon_{fe} = \kappa_v \varepsilon_{fu}^* \le 0.004$$

where $\kappa_v$ is a bond-dependent reduction factor (typically 0.30–0.65).

#### FRP Material Properties — Design Limits

| Property | Formula / Limit |
|---|---|
| **Design tensile strength** | $f_{fu} = C_E f_{fu}^*$ (environmental factor $C_E$ = 0.85–1.00) |
| **Design rupture strain** | $\varepsilon_{fu} = C_E \varepsilon_{fu}^*$ |
| **Elastic modulus** | $E_f$ from manufacturer (CFRP ≈ 165–500 GPa) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note for KTU:** Always apply the **environmental reduction factor $C_E$**. Examiners frequently award full marks only when $C_E$ is correctly applied for external exposure conditions.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bridge rehabilitation** in coastal Kerala (NH-66 corridors) — GFRP/CFRP used to retrofit aging girders subjected to chloride-induced corrosion.
- **Industrial floor beams** in chemical plants — polymer-modified mortars resist acidic/alkaline exposure.
- **Seismic retrofit** of pre-code RC frames in urban Kerala — FRP wraps enhance shear capacity and ductility at column-beam joints.
- **Heritage building conservation** — NSM CFRP preserves architectural aesthetics while upgrading load capacity.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Nominal Flexural Capacity of an FRP-Strengthened RC Beam

**Given:**
- Beam width $b = 250$ mm
- Effective depth to steel $d = 450$ mm
- Effective depth to FRP $d_f = 480$ mm
- Steel area $A_s = 942$ mm² (3 bars of 20 mm Ø)
- FRP area $A_f = 100$ mm² (single CFRP laminate 50 mm × 2 mm)
- $f_y = 415$ MPa (steel yield strength)
- $f_{fu} = 2800$ MPa (CFRP ultimate strength)
- $E_f = 165$ GPa
- $f_c' = 25$ MPa
- $E_s = 200$ GPa
- Substrate strain $\varepsilon_{bi} = 0$ (unloaded beam case)
- Environmental factor $C_E = 0.95$

**Step 1 — Compute ultimate design strain of FRP:**

$$\varepsilon_{fu}^* = \frac{f_{fu}}{E_f} = \frac{2800}{165000} = 0.01697$$

Apply environmental factor:

$$\varepsilon_{fu} = C_E \cdot \varepsilon_{fu}^* = 0.95 \times 0.01697 = 0.01612$$

**Step 2 — Compute effective FRP strain (debonding limit):**

$$\varepsilon_{fe} = \kappa_v \varepsilon_{fu} = 0.55 \times 0.01612 = 0.008866$$

(Using $\kappa_v = 0.55$ for U-wrap configuration per ACI 440.2R.)

Check against the 0.004 strain cap:

$$\varepsilon_{fe} = \min(0.008866,\ 0.004) = 0.004 \text{ (governs)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute effective FRP stress:**

$$f_{fe} = E_f \cdot \varepsilon_{fe} = 165000 \times 0.004 = 660 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Assume steel yields (verify later):**

$$f_s = f_y = 415 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for neutral axis depth $c$ using force equilibrium:**

$$0.85 f_c' \cdot a \cdot b = A_s f_s + A_f f_{fe}$$

$$0.85 \times 25 \times a \times 250 = 942 \times 415 + 100 \times 660$$

$$5312.5 \cdot a = 390930 + 66000 = 456930$$

$$a = \frac{456930}{5312.5} = 86.01 \text{ mm}$$

Since $a = \beta_1 c$ and for $f_c' = 25$ MPa, $\beta_1 = 0.85$:

$$c = \frac{a}{\beta_1} = \frac{86.01}{0.85} = 101.19 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Verify steel yields:**

$$\varepsilon_s = \varepsilon_{cu} \left(\frac{d - c}{c}\right) = 0.003 \times \left(\frac{450 - 101.19}{101.19}\right)$$

$$\varepsilon_s = 0.003 \times 3.447 = 0.01034 > \varepsilon_y = \frac{415}{200000} = 0.002075$$

✅ **Steel yields — assumption valid.**

**Step 7 — Verify FRP strain is below debonding limit:**

$$\varepsilon_{f,actual} = \varepsilon_{cu} \left(\frac{d_f - c}{c}\right) = 0.003 \times \left(\frac{480 - 101.19}{101.19}\right)$$

$$\varepsilon_{f,actual} = 0.003 \times 3.7437 = 0.01123$$

But this exceeds $\varepsilon_{fe} = 0.004$, so **debonding governs**, and we use $f_{fe} = 660$ MPa (already capped).

**Step 8 — Compute nominal moment capacity $M_n$:**

$$M_n = A_s f_s \left(d - \frac{a}{2}\right) + A_f f_{fe} \left(d_f - \frac{a}{2}\right)$$

$$M_n = 942 \times 415 \times \left(450 - \frac{86.01}{2}\right) + 100 \times 660 \times \left(480 - \frac{86.01}{2}\right)$$

$$M_n = 390930 \times 406.995 + 66000 \times 436.995$$

$$M_n = 159{,}097{,}300 + 28{,}841{,}670 = 187{,}938{,}970 \text{ N·mm}$$

$$\boxed{M_n \approx 187.94 \text{ kN·m}}$$

**Step 9 — Design moment capacity (with strength reduction factor $\phi = 0.90$):**

$$\phi M_n = 0.90 \times 187.94 = 169.15 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 10 — Percentage increase over unstrengthened capacity:**

Unstrengthened capacity (FRP removed):

$$M_{n,original} = A_s f_s \left(d - \frac{a_0}{2}\right)$$

$$a_0 = \frac{390930}{5312.5} = 73.59 \text{ mm}$$

$$M_{n,original} = 390930 \times \left(450 - 36.795\right) = 390930 \times 413.205$$

$$M_{n,original} = 161{,}547{,}400 \text{ N·mm} \approx 161.55 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$\% \text{ increase} = \frac{187.94 - 161.55}{161.55} \times 100 \approx 16.3\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Adding only 100 mm² of CFRP laminate (a 50 mm × 2 mm strip) increased the flexural capacity by **~16%** — a powerful illustration of why FRP is preferred for strengthening.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Failure Modes of FRP-Strengthened Beams

| Failure Mode | Description | Governing Equation / Limit | Prevention |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FRP Rupture** | FRP fibers break in tension | $\varepsilon_f \le \varepsilon_{fu}$ | Adequate FRP section |
| **Concrete Crushing** | Top concrete crushes | $c/d \le$ balance limit | Increase $f_c'$, add compression FRP |
| **FRP Debonding** | FRP peels off substrate | $\varepsilon_{fe} \le \kappa_v \varepsilon_{fu}$ | Use U-wraps, NSM, mechanical anchors |
| **End Peeling** | Premature peel at FRP cut-off | Anchorage length $l_e$ | Extend FRP beyond development length |
| **Cover Delamination** | Concrete cover splits off | Bond stress limit | Surface prep, primer, groove in NSM |
| **Shear Failure** | Beam fails in shear before flexure | $V_n \ge M_n / a$ | Provide shear FRP wrap |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Shear Strengthening

**Given:** RC beam requires additional shear capacity $\Delta V = 80$ kN. Section: $b = 250$ mm, $d = 450$ mm, $d_f = 450$ mm. CFRP U-wrap: 100 mm wide, 1 mm thick, 2-ply, spacing $s_f = 200$ mm, fibers at 90° (vertical). $E_f = 165$ GPa, $f_{fu}^* = 2800$ MPa, $C_E = 0.95$.

**Step 1 — Area of FRP shear reinforcement per spacing:**

$$A_f = 2 \times 100 \times 1 = 200 \text{ mm}^2 \text{ (per spacing)}$$

**Step 2 — Effective FRP stress:**

$$f_{fu} = 0.95 \times 2800 = 2660 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\varepsilon_{fu} = 0.95 \times 2800/165000 = 0.01532$$

$$\varepsilon_{fe} = 0.55 \times 0.01532 = 0.008426, \text{ but capped at } 0.004$$

$$f_{fe} = 165000 \times 0.004 = 660 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — FRP shear contribution:**

$$V_f = \frac{A_f f_{fe} (\sin 90° + \cos 90°) d_f}{s_f} = \frac{200 \times 660 \times (1+0) \times 450}{200}$$

$$V_f = \frac{200 \times 660 \times 450}{200} = 660 \times 450 = 297{,}000 \text{ N} = 297 \text{ kN}$$

$$\boxed{V_f = 297 \text{ kN} \gg \Delta V = 80 \text{ kN} \quad \text{(Adequate)}}$$
