## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*List any three advantages of using FRP composites over steel plates for strengthening RC beams.*

**Model Answer (3 key points × 1 mark each):**

1. **High strength-to-weight ratio:** CFRP has tensile strength 5–10× that of structural steel at roughly 1/5th the density, adding negligible dead load to the existing structure.

2. **Corrosion resistance:** FRP does not corrode like steel, making it ideal for aggressive environments (marine/coastal structures, chemical plants, de-icing salt zones).

3. **Ease of installation:** FRP laminates/fabrics can be applied in confined spaces without heavy lifting equipment, scaffolding for welding, or extended shutdown periods — leading to lower labour and downtime costs.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define the term "debonding" in the context of FRP-strengthened RC beams. Mention one method to prevent it.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Debonding is the premature failure mode in which the FRP laminate/fabric separates from the concrete substrate before the FRP reaches its ultimate tensile capacity. It occurs at the concrete-adhesive interface (or within the concrete cover itself) due to high interfacial shear and normal stresses, typically initiated at flexural or shear cracks or at the FRP cut-off points.

**Prevention method (1 mark):** Provide **mechanical anchorage** at the FRP ends (e.g., U-wraps, FRP anchor spikes, bolted plates) or use **Near-Surface Mounted (NSM) FRP** instead of externally bonded systems, since the embedded FRP is protected from peeling stresses.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — *Design-Oriented Question*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** A simply supported RC beam of span 6 m, cross-section 250 mm × 500 mm, is reinforced with 4 bars of 16 mm diameter at the bottom. The existing flexural capacity is found to be 120 kN·m, but the revised service demand is **180 kN·m**. Design a **CFRP laminate strengthening scheme** using externally bonded FRP on the tension face. Given: $f_y = 415$ MPa, $f_c' = 25$ MPa, $f_{fu}^* = 2800$ MPa, $E_f = 165$ GPa, $C_E = 0.95$, $\kappa_v = 0.55$, $\beta_1 = 0.85$. Take $d = 450$ mm and $d_f = 480$ mm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the construction sequence for **externally bonded FRP strengthening** of an RC beam, including surface preparation, primer application, and quality control checks. List any three **failure modes** of FRP-strengthened beams. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Required additional moment:**

$$\Delta M = 180 - 120 = 60 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 2 — Effective FRP strain cap:**

$$\varepsilon_{fu}^* = \frac{2800}{165000} = 0.01697$$

$$\varepsilon_{fu} = 0.95 \times 0.01697 = 0.01612$$

$$\varepsilon_{fe} = \min(0.55 \times 0.01612,\ 0.004) = \min(0.008866,\ 0.004) = 0.004$$

$$f_{fe} = 165000 \times 0.004 = 660 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Steel parameters:**

$$A_s = 4 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 16^2 = 4 \times 201.06 = 804.25 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$T_s = A_s f_y = 804.25 \times 415 = 333{,}764 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for required $A_f$ iteratively:**

Assume $a$ from steel alone:

$$a_0 = \frac{333764}{0.85 \times 25 \times 250} = 62.83 \text{ mm}$$

Required $A_f$ to provide additional moment of 60 kN·m:

$$A_f \approx \frac{\Delta M}{f_{fe}(d_f - a/2)} = \frac{60 \times 10^6}{660 \times (480 - 31.4)} = \frac{60 \times 10^6}{660 \times 448.6}$$

$$A_f \approx \frac{60 \times 10^6}{296{,}076} = 202.6 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 5 — Select practical laminate:**

Use **CFRP laminate 100 mm wide × 2.5 mm thick**:

$$A_{f,provided} = 100 \times 2.5 = 250 \text{ mm}^2 \quad \text{(Adequate)}$$

**Step 6 — Verify final capacity:**

$$a = \frac{333764 + 250 \times 660}{5312.5} = \frac{333764 + 165000}{5312.5} = \frac{498764}{5312.5} = 93.89 \text{ mm}$$

$$c = 93.89/0.85 = 110.46 \text{ mm}$$

$$\varepsilon_s = 0.003 \times \frac{450 - 110.46}{110.46} = 0.00922 > 0.002075 \quad \text{(Steel yields ✓)}$$

$$M_n = 333764 \times (450 - 46.945) + 165000 \times (480 - 46.945)$$

$$M_n = 333764 \times 403.055 + 165000 \times 433.055$$

$$M_n = 134{,}527{,}000 + 71{,}454{,}000 = 205{,}981{,}000 \text{ N·mm} = 206 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$\phi M_n = 0.90 \times 206 = 185.4 \text{ kN·m} > 180 \text{ kN·m} \quad \text{✓ SAFE}$$

**Final Design:** **CFRP laminate 100 mm wide × 2.5 mm thick** on the tension face.

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Construction Sequence (5 marks):**

1. **Inspection and substrate preparation:** Identify and mark all cracks, spalls, and corroded reinforcement. Remove loose/delaminated concrete. Expose corroded rebar and treat with corrosion inhibitor.

2. **Surface preparation:** Grind the concrete surface to expose aggregate (CSP 3–5 per ICRI). Repair spalls with **polymer-modified mortar**. Round all sharp corners to minimum 20 mm radius to prevent stress concentrations. Clean with compressed air; substrate must be dry (< 4% moisture for epoxy).

3. **Primer application:** Apply epoxy primer to seal the substrate pore structure and improve adhesion. Allow tack-free cure.

4. **Putty/resin layer (if needed):** Apply epoxy putty to level surface irregularities exceeding 1 mm.

5. **FRP application:** For wet layup systems, saturate the fabric with epoxy resin and apply to the substrate. For laminates, apply structural adhesive and press the laminate firmly using a roller to remove entrapped air.

6. **Curing:** Maintain temperature 15–35 °C for at least 24 hours. Avoid mechanical loading during cure.

7. **Quality control:** Conduct pull-off tests (minimum 1.7 MPa substrate failure per ACI 440.2R), visual inspection for voids/delaminations, tap-test for hollow areas.

**Three Failure Modes (2 marks):**

1. **FRP Debonding** — separation at the adhesive-concrete interface before FRP reaches ultimate strain.
2. **Concrete Cover Delamination** — peeling off of a layer of concrete cover along the plane of the steel reinforcement.
3. **FRP End Peeling** — premature debonding initiated at the FRP termination point due to high interfacial shear and normal stresses.

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Conceptual + Application Question*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Compare the four polymer-based strengthening methods for RC beams: **(i)** Externally Bonded FRP, **(ii)** Near-Surface Mounted FRP, **(iii)** FRP Wrapping, **(iv)** Polymer-Modified Mortar Overlay. Discuss their **mechanism of action**, **typical applications**, and **limitations** in a comparative table. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An RC beam of section 300 mm × 600 mm is deficient in shear. The required additional shear capacity is **120 kN**. Design a **CFRP U-wrap** for shear strengthening. Given: $b = 300$ mm, $d = 540$ mm, $f_{fu}^* = 2800$ MPa, $E_f = 165$ GPa, $C_E = 0.95$, $\kappa_v = 0.55$. FRP strip width = 100 mm, thickness = 1.2 mm, 2-ply, vertical orientation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

| Method | Mechanism of Action | Typical Application | Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|
| **EB-FRP** | FRP bonded externally on tension face acts as additional tension reinforcement, increasing lever arm and moment capacity. | Flexural strengthening of slabs, beams, and bridge girders from below. | Vulnerable to debonding; exposed to fire/UV; requires surface prep. |
| **NSM FRP** | FRP bar/laminate is embedded in a groove cut into the cover, surrounded by epoxy. Bond is mechanical + chemical, far superior to EB-FRP. | Negative moment regions of continuous beams, slabs with overlays, seismic retrofits. | Limited to cover zone; groove cutting may hit existing rebar. |
| **FRP Wrapping** | FRP fabric wrapped around cross-section acts as external stirrups (shear) or confinement (columns). | Shear strengthening of beams, confinement of columns, lap-splice clamping. | Full wrap impractical for T-beams (top flange access issues); U-wrap less efficient. |
| **Polymer Mortar** | Polymer-modified cementitious material replaces damaged cover, restoring geometry and re-passivating rebar. | Spall repair, cover restoration, substrate preparation for FRP. | Does not add structural capacity by itself; only restores original section. |

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1 — FRP parameters:**

$$A_f = 2 \times 100 \times 1.2 = 240 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$\varepsilon_{fu} = 0.95 \times 2800/165000 = 0.01612$$

$$\varepsilon_{fe} = \min(0.55 \times 0.01612,\ 0.004) = 0.004$$

$$f_{fe} = 165000 \times 0.004 = 660 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Required spacing for $V_f = 120$ kN:**

$$V_f = \frac{A_f f_{fe} (\sin 90° + \cos 90°) d_f}{s_f} = \frac{240 \times 660 \times 1 \times 540}{s_f}$$

$$120{,}000 = \frac{240 \times 660 \times 540}{s_f} = \frac{85{,}536{,}000}{s_f}$$

$$s_f = \frac{85{,}536{,}000}{120{,}000} = 712.8 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Apply code limits:**

$$s_f \le d/4 = 540/4 = 135 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(governs)}$$

$$s_f \le 300 \text{ mm (max spacing per ACI)}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Provide CFRP U-wraps at } s_f = 135 \text{ mm c/c}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\varepsilon_{fe} = 0.004$ cap:** Students often use the full $\kappa_v \varepsilon_{fu}$ and massively over-design the FRP. Always cap at 0.004.
> 2. **Ignoring the environmental factor $C_E$:** Markers deduct 1 mark per sub-part if $C_E$ is omitted.
> 3. **Not verifying code-based spacing limits:** The calculated $s_f = 712$ mm is geometrically valid but **violates shear spacing rules** ($s_f \le d/4$). Students must apply the governing minimum.
> 4. **Confusing $f_{fu}$ with $f_{fu}^*$:** Always use **design strength** (with $C_E$ applied), not the manufacturer's ultimate value.
> 5. **Missing substrate strain $\varepsilon_{bi}$:** For beams that remain loaded during retrofit (typical in real projects), $\varepsilon_{bi} \neq 0$. KTU expects students to at least **mention this term** even if numerical value is taken as zero for simplicity.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Strengthening of RC Beams**

- ✅ Strengthening of RC beams is needed due to **deterioration, increased demand, design deficiency, or damage**.
- ✅ **FRP (Fiber Reinforced Polymer)** is the primary polymer-based strengthening material: types are **CFRP, GFRP, AFRP**.
- ✅ Four main polymer-based methods: **EB-FRP, NSM FRP, FRP Wrapping, Polymer-Modified Mortar Overlay**.
- ✅ **Surface preparation is critical** — concrete must be clean, dry, sound, with rounded corners (≥ 20 mm radius).
- ✅ **Epoxy resin** is the standard adhesive for FRP bonding; provides the shear transfer at the FRP-concrete interface.
- ✅ **Strain compatibility** governs the design: $\varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_{cu}\left(\frac{d_f - c}{c}\right) - \varepsilon_{bi}$.
- ✅ **Debonding is the most common failure mode** — always limit $\varepsilon_{fe} \le 0.004$ and $\varepsilon_{fe} \le \kappa_v \varepsilon_{fu}$.
- ✅ **Environmental reduction factor $C_E$** must be applied for external exposure (typically 0.85–0.95).
- ✅ **Anchor the FRP ends** — U-wraps, NSM, or mechanical anchors to prevent end-peeling debonding.
- ✅ **Shear strengthening** uses U-wraps or full wraps; spacing limited to **$d/4$** and **300 mm maximum**.
- ✅ **Polymer-modified mortar** is used to **repair spalled concrete** before FRP application — it is a *preparatory* step, not a stand-alone strengthening method.
- ✅ Always check: **steel yields** ($\varepsilon_s \ge \varepsilon_y$), **concrete crushes** ($c/d$ within limits), **FRP doesn't debond** ($\varepsilon_f \le \varepsilon_{fe}$).
- ✅ **Substrate strain $\varepsilon_{bi}$** accounts for the existing dead load carried by the beam at the time of FRP installation — critical for non-propped retrofit.
- ✅ For **Kerala/coastal projects**: prefer **CFRP** (highest durability) over GFRP in chloride-rich environments; GFRP is cost-effective for indoor/less aggressive zones.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RC Columns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT758 - POLYMERS IN CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** RC Columns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reinforced Cement Concrete (RC) Column:** A vertical compression member whose effective length exceeds **three times the least lateral dimension**, in which the longitudinal reinforcement is taken into account in the calculation of load-carrying capacity, designed primarily to resist axial compressive forces combined with bending moments and shear as per **IS 456:2000**.

An **RC column** is a structural element that transfers loads from beams and slabs to the foundation. In the context of **Polymers in Construction (PEPOT758)**, RC columns are studied with special emphasis on polymer-modified concrete matrices, **Glass Fibre Reinforced Polymer (GFRP)** rebars, **Carbon Fibre Reinforced Polymer (CFRP)** wrapping, and polymer-based corrosion-protection systems used to enhance durability and load capacity.

---

### 1.2 Classification of RC Columns (KTU Board Classification)

> [!NOTE]
> As per IS 456:2000 (Clause 25.1.1 & 25.1.2), columns are classified based on **slenderness ratio** ($\lambda = \text{Leff} / D$).

| Type | Slenderness Ratio ($\lambda$) | Behaviour |
|------|------------------------------|-----------|
| **Short Column** | $\lambda \leq 12$ | Failure by crushing of concrete (material failure) |
| **Long Column** | $\lambda > 12$ | Failure by buckling (elastic instability) |

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Standing Soldier"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a tall, thin **bamboo pole** vs. a short, thick **oak log**, both standing vertically and loaded from the top.
> - The **short oak log** simply gets crushed under load — the material gives way. This is a **short column**.
> - The **tall bamboo pole**, however, bends sideways (buckles) long before the material itself crushes. This is a **long column** failure.
> - In RC columns, **polymer fibres** act like internal "scaffolding," and **FRP wrapping** acts like an external "armour shell," preventing the column from splitting outward (lateral bursting) under heavy axial loads.

---

### 1.4 Role of Polymers in RC Columns

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In modern KTU-aligned construction practice, polymers contribute to RC columns in **four critical ways**:
> 1. **Polymer Rebars (GFRP/CFRP/AFRP)** — replace steel to eliminate corrosion (especially in coastal Kerala).
> 2. **FRP Wrapping / Jacketing** — external confinement to enhance compressive strength.
> 3. **Polymer-Modified Concrete (PMC)** — using epoxy/styrene-butadiene latex to improve bond and reduce permeability.
> 4. **Polymer Coatings** — anti-corrosion barriers on conventional steel reinforcement.

Key standard code references (IS guidelines):

- **IS 456:2000** — Plain and Reinforced Concrete Code of Practice.
- **IS 15916:2010** — Strengthening of RC structures using FRP composites.
- **ACI 440.2R-17** — Guide for Design of RC Columns Strengthened with FRP.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Column Behaviour

The behaviour of an RC column depends on the **interaction of three parameters**:

1. **Axial Load (P)** — vertical compressive force.
2. **Moment (M)** — caused by eccentricity of load or end restraints.
3. **Slenderness ($\lambda$)** — governs whether failure is by crushing or buckling.

#### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown:

- **Step 1:** Determine the **effective length (Leff)** of the column based on end conditions.
- **Step 2:** Compute **slenderness ratio** $\lambda = L_{\text{eff}} / D$ (for rectangular) or $L_{\text{eff}} / d$ (for circular).
- **Step 3:** Classify as **short** ($\lambda \leq 12$) or **long** ($\lambda > 12$).
- **Step 4:** For short columns, design using **IS 456 equation** combining axial load and moment (uniaxial/biaxial bending).
- **Step 5:** For long columns, reduce design strength using the **minimum eccentricity** clause and additional moment due to deflection.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **exam-ready** for KTU University Exams. All quantities are in SI units (N, mm, MPa) unless stated.

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Description / Boundary Conditions |
|---|----------------------|---------|-----------------------------------|
| 1 | Slenderness Ratio (Rectangular) | $\lambda = L_{\text{eff}} / D$ | $D$ = least lateral dimension |
| 2 | Slenderness Ratio (Circular) | $\lambda = L_{\text{eff}} / d$ | $d$ = diameter |
| 3 | Effective Length | $L_{\text{eff}} = k \cdot L$ | $k$ depends on end conditions |
| 4 | Minimum Eccentricity | $e_{\min} = \dfrac{L}{500} + \dfrac{D}{30}$ | $\geq 20$ mm (IS 456 Cl. 25.4) |
| 5 | Axial Load Capacity (Short, Pure Axial) | $P_u = 0.45 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot A_c + 0.75 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{sc}$ | IS 456 Cl. 38.3 (working stress) |
| 6 | Ultimate Axial Load (Limit State) | $P_u = 0.45 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot A_c + 0.90 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{sc}$ | IS 456 Cl. 39.3 (limit state) |
| 7 | Additional Moment (Long Column) | $M_a = \dfrac{P_u \cdot D}{2000} \cdot \left( \dfrac{L_{\text{eff}}}{D} \right)^2$ | IS 456 Cl. 39.7.1 |
| 8 | Reduced Allowable Stress (Long Column) | $P_{allow}' = P_{allow} \cdot C_r$ | $C_r$ = reduction factor |
| 9 | Reduction Factor (IS 456) | $C_r = 1.25 - \dfrac{L_{\text{eff}}}{48 D}$ | for $12 < \lambda \leq 24$ |
| 10 | FRP Confinement Strength | $f_{cc}' = f_{ck} \cdot \left( 2.254 \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{7.94 f_l}{f_{ck}}} - 2 \dfrac{f_l}{f_{ck}} - 1.254 \right)$ | Lam & Teng model (1999) |
| 11 | Confining Pressure (FRP Wrap) | $f_l = \dfrac{2 \cdot n \cdot t_f \cdot E_f \cdot \varepsilon_{fe}}{D}$ | $n$ = number of FRP layers |
| 12 | Effective Strain in FRP | $\varepsilon_{fe} = 0.004 \leq \kappa_\varepsilon \cdot \varepsilon_{fu} \leq 0.75 \cdot \varepsilon_{fu}$ | ACI 440.2R-17 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** Kerala's coastal humidity (e.g., Kochi, Kozhikode) causes severe corrosion in steel-reinforced columns. FRP-reinforced columns achieve a service life of **75–100 years** versus **30–40 years** for conventional RC columns, making polymers economically essential in marine environments.

---

### 2.3 Why Polymers Matter — Engineering Utility

- **Corrosion resistance** in chloride-rich environments (bridges, marine structures).
- **High strength-to-weight ratio** (CFRP = 5× stronger than steel, 5× lighter).
- **Non-magnetic, non-conductive** — useful in hospital and electronic infrastructure.
- **Fatigue resistance** under cyclic loads (industrial buildings, flyovers).
- **Rapid retrofitting** of damaged columns via FRP wrapping without major demolition.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of Minimum Eccentricity (IS 456 Cl. 25.4)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statement:** For columns, an eccentricity equal to or greater than the **minimum eccentricity** ($e_{\min}$) shall be considered in design.

**Mathematical Derivation (Exhaustive Step-by-Step):**

Starting from the fundamental principle of **safety against accidental eccentricities** and **construction tolerances**, IS 456 (based on empirical studies) prescribes:

$$
e_{\min} = \dfrac{L}{500} + \dfrac{D}{30}
$$

**Step 1:** Consider a column of unsupported length $L$ (in mm). The constructional imperfection introduces an unintended lateral offset proportional to length.

$$
\Delta_{\text{length}} = \dfrac{L}{500}
$$

**Step 2:** Consider the cross-sectional imperfection proportional to the lateral dimension $D$.

$$
\Delta_{\text{section}} = \dfrac{D}{30}
$$

**Step 3:** Superimpose both imperfections (worst-case assumption — they may align in the same direction):

$$
e_{\min} = \Delta_{\text{length}} + \Delta_{\text{section}}
$$

$$
e_{\min} = \dfrac{L}{500} + \dfrac{D}{30}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the lower-bound condition that $e_{\min}$ shall never be less than **20 mm** (to prevent designing members as pure-axial when construction tolerances exist):

$$
e_{\min} = \max\left( \dfrac{L}{500} + \dfrac{D}{30},\ 20 \text{ mm} \right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This value of $e_{\min}$ is then used to compute the design moment $M = P_u \cdot e_{\min}$, ensuring that **all columns are designed for combined axial + bending** regardless of declared loading.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of IS 456 Axial Load Capacity (Limit State Method)

For a **short, axially-loaded tied column**, the ultimate load-carrying capacity is derived from equilibrium of forces:

**Step 1:** Write equilibrium between applied load $P_u$ and internal resisting forces (concrete + steel):

$$
P_u = P_{c} + P_{s}
$$

**Step 2:** Express concrete contribution. The IS 456 partial safety factor for concrete ($\gamma_m = 1.5$) is applied to the design compressive strength $f_{ck}$:

$$
P_c = 0.45 \cdot \dfrac{f_{ck}}{\gamma_m} \cdot A_c
$$

Wait — for **limit state**, the design strength is already $f_{ck}$ (characteristic), so:

$$
P_c = 0.45 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot A_c
$$

where the factor **0.45** accounts for the effective strength of concrete under compression (due to eccentricity, slenderness, and partial safety).

**Step 3:** Express steel contribution with partial safety factor for steel ($\gamma_m = 1.15$):

$$
P_s = 0.90 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{sc}
$$

**Step 4:** Combine:

$$
P_u = 0.45 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot A_c + 0.90 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{sc}
$$

**Step 5:** Note that for **helical (spiral) columns**, the coefficient for $f_{ck}$ becomes **0.45** with additional benefit from spiral confinement (Clause 39.4.1 of IS 456):

$$
P_{u,\text{spiral}} = 0.45 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot A_c + 0.90 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{sc} + \text{spiral contribution}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Additional Moment for Long Columns (IS 456 Cl. 39.7.1)

For **long slender columns**, the deflection under load amplifies the bending moment. IS 456 prescribes an empirical additional moment:

$$
M_a = \dfrac{P_u \cdot D}{2000} \cdot \left( \dfrac{L_{\text{eff}}}{D} \right)^2
$$

**Step 1:** Consider an initial imperfection $e_0 = D/30 + L/500$ producing first-order moment $M_1 = P_u \cdot e_0$.

**Step 2:** The slenderness introduces second-order deflection $\delta$ which, through moment-curvature, multiplies the moment. Empirical fitting to test data yields:

$$
M_a = \dfrac{P_u \cdot D}{2000} \cdot \left( \dfrac{L_{\text{eff}}}{D} \right)^2
$$

**Step 3:** The total design moment for the slender column becomes:

$$
M_{\text{total}} = M_u + M_a
$$

where $M_u = P_u \cdot e$ is the first-order moment.

**Numerical Worked Example:**

Given: A rectangular RC column with $D = 300$ mm, $L = 4$ m, $f_{ck} = 20$ MPa, $f_y = 415$ MPa, area of steel $A_{sc} = 2400$ mm², gross area $A_g = 300 \times 300 = 90000$ mm².

**Step 1:** Check slenderness. Assuming both ends hinged, $L_{\text{eff}} = 1.0 L = 4000$ mm.

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{L_{\text{eff}}}{D} = \dfrac{4000}{300} = 13.33
$$

Since $\lambda > 12$, this is a **long column**.

**Step 2:** Compute minimum eccentricity:

$$
e_{\min} = \dfrac{4000}{500} + \dfrac{300}{30} = 8 + 10 = 18 \text{ mm}
$$

Apply lower bound:

$$
e_{\min} = \max(18,\ 20) = 20 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute gross area of concrete:

$$
A_c = A_g - A_{sc} = 90000 - 2400 = 87600 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 4:** Compute ultimate axial capacity (ignoring slenderness reduction as a first estimate):

$$
P_{u0} = 0.45 \cdot 20 \cdot 87600 + 0.90 \cdot 415 \cdot 2400
$$

$$
P_{u0} = 788400 + 896400 = 1684800 \text{ N} \approx 1684.8 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute reduction factor (for $12 < \lambda \leq 24$):

$$
C_r = 1.25 - \dfrac{L_{\text{eff}}}{48 D} = 1.25 - \dfrac{4000}{48 \times 300} = 1.25 - 0.278 = 0.972
$$

**Step 6:** Reduced capacity:

$$
P_u = C_r \cdot P_{u0} = 0.972 \times 1684.8 = 1637.6 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 7:** Compute additional moment:

$$
M_a = \dfrac{1637600 \cdot 300}{2000} \cdot (13.33)^2
$$

$$
M_a = 245640 \cdot 177.69 = 4.36 \times 10^7 \text{ N·mm} = 43.6 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 8:** Final design moment:

$$
M_{\text{total}} = P_u \cdot e_{\min} + M_a = 1637.6 \times 0.020 + 43.6 = 32.75 + 43.6 = 76.35 \text{ kN·m}
$$

This combined ($P_u$, $M_{\text{total}}$) point is then checked against the **P-M interaction diagram** for the column section.

---

### 3.4 FRP Confinement — Lam & Teng (1999) Model Derivation

When a column is wrapped with FRP, the confining pressure $f_l$ enhances concrete strength $f_{cc}'$.

**Step 1:** Equilibrium of a wrapped circular column section under radial pressure:

$$
f_l = \dfrac{2 \cdot n \cdot t_f \cdot E_f \cdot \varepsilon_{fe}}{D}
$$

where $n$ = number of FRP layers, $t_f$ = thickness per layer, $E_f$ = elastic modulus of FRP, $D$ = column diameter, $\varepsilon_{fe}$ = effective strain.

**Step 2:** Confinement effectiveness ratio:

$$
f_l / f_{ck}
$$

**Step 3:** Strength enhancement (Lam & Teng):

$$
f_{cc}' = f_{ck} + 3.3 \cdot f_l \quad \text{(for } f_l/f_{ck} \geq 0.07 \text{)}
$$

Or more generally:

$$
f_{cc}' / f_{ck} = 2.254 \sqrt{1 + 7.94 \cdot (f_l/f_{ck})} - 2 \cdot (f_l/f_{ck}) - 1.254
$$

**Step 4:** Ultimate axial capacity of FRP-wrapped column:

$$
P_u^{\text{FRP}} = f_{cc}' \cdot A_c + 0.90 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{sc}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS


```
            (with Polymer Admixture)
```

**Component Legend:**

- Outer black boundary = **FRP wrap** (provides confinement).
- `[===]` = **Longitudinal rebars** (steel or GFRP).

- Inner fill = **Polymer-modified concrete** core.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define minimum eccentricity as per IS 456:2000. Why is it specified even for axially-loaded columns?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Minimum eccentricity is the smallest design eccentricity to be considered for all RC columns. As per IS 456 Cl. 25.4:
>
> $$e_{\min} = \max\left(\dfrac{L}{500} + \dfrac{D}{30},\ 20\ \text{mm}\right)$$
>
> **Reason (1.5 marks):** It accounts for (i) constructional imperfections, (ii) unintentional load eccentricities, and (iii) ensures that all columns are designed as **beam-columns** (combined axial + bending) rather than purely axially loaded, providing a **factor of safety against unforeseen moments**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three advantages of using GFRP rebars over conventional steel rebars in RC columns.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Corrosion resistance:** GFRP does not rust in chloride-rich or marine environments (critical for coastal Kerala).
2. **High strength-to-weight ratio:** GFRP is approximately 4× lighter than steel for equivalent tensile strength.
3. **Non-magnetic and non-conductive:** Ideal for structures housing sensitive electronic/medical equipment.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]`

**(a)** A short reinforced concrete column of size $400 \text{ mm} \times 400 \text{ mm}$ is reinforced with **6 bars of 20 mm diameter** of Fe415 steel. The column is subjected to an **axial working load of 1500 kN**. Using **Limit State Method** as per IS 456:2000, check the adequacy of the column. Use $f_{ck} = 20$ MPa. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with neat sketches the **behaviour of short and long RC columns** under axial loading. How does **slenderness ratio** influence the failure mode? **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Solution

**Step 1: Compute areas (1 mark)**

$$
A_{sc} = 6 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 20^2 = 6 \times 314.16 = 1884.96 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
A_g = 400 \times 400 = 160000 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
A_c = A_g - A_{sc} = 160000 - 1884.96 = 158115.04 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2: Apply load factor (1 mark)**

For limit state, factored load:

$$
P_u = 1.5 \times 1500 = 2250 \text{ kN} = 2250 \times 10^3 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3: Compute ultimate axial capacity (2 marks)**

$$
P_{u,\text{capacity}} = 0.45 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot A_c + 0.90 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{sc}
$$

$$
P_{u,\text{capacity}} = 0.45 \times 20 \times 158115.04 + 0.90 \times 415 \times 1884.96
$$

$$
P_{u,\text{capacity}} = 1423035 + 704023 = 2127058 \text{ N} \approx 2127.06 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4: Compare with applied factored load (1 mark)**

Since $P_u = 2250 \text{ kN} > 2127.06 \text{ kN}$, the column is **NOT adequate**.

**Step 5: Recommendation (2 marks)**

The column needs to be **redesigned** with either:
- Larger section size (e.g., $450 \times 450$ mm), or
- Increased steel area (use 8 bars of 20 mm or higher diameter bars), or
- Higher grade concrete ($f_{ck} = 25$ MPa).

**Final Conclusion (boxed for KTU valuation):**

> **The column is inadequate by a margin of** $\Delta = 2250 - 2127 = 123\ \text{kN}$. Redesign required.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Answer

**Short Column Behaviour (3 marks):**

- Failure occurs by **crushing of concrete** (material failure) before buckling.
- Strain in steel and concrete reaches **ultimate simultaneously**.
- Stress-strain behaviour remains **linear up to 70–80% of ultimate load**.
- Failure is sudden, accompanied by concrete spalling.

**Long Column Behaviour (2 marks):**

- Failure occurs by **buckling** (elastic instability) at loads below crushing capacity.
- Column **deflects laterally** before material reaches ultimate stress.
- Secondary moments amplify due to $P-\Delta$ effect.

**Influence of Slenderness Ratio (2 marks):**

| Slenderness | Effect |
|-------------|--------|
| $\lambda \leq 12$ | Material failure (crushing) dominates |
| $12 < \lambda \leq 24$ | Combined crushing + buckling; IS 456 reduction factor applies |
| $\lambda > 24$ | Pure buckling; design governed by Euler's formula |

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Describe the various **methods of strengthening RC columns** using **FRP composites**. Compare **CFRP, GFRP, and AFRP** with respect to their material properties and applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A short circular column of **300 mm diameter** is wrapped with **3 layers of GFRP** each of thickness $t_f = 1.2$ mm and elastic modulus $E_f = 30$ GPa. The concrete grade is $f_{ck} = 25$ MPa. Estimate the **enhanced compressive strength** using the Lam & Teng model. Assume effective strain $\varepsilon_{fe} = 0.004$. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Model Answer

**Methods of Strengthening RC Columns Using FRP (4 marks):**

1. **Full Wrapping (Complete Confinement):** FRP sheets wrapped continuously around the column — most effective.
2. **Partial Wrapping (Strip Wrapping):** FRP applied in horizontal strips at intervals — for partial confinement.
3. **FRP-Reinforced Concrete Jacketing:** Layer of new concrete + FRP cage around existing column.
4. **Near-Surface Mounted (NSM) FRP:** FRP rods embedded into grooves cut into column surface.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Property | CFRP (Carbon) | GFRP (Glass) | AFRP (Aramid) |
|----------|---------------|---------------|----------------|
| Tensile Strength | **3000–5000 MPa** | **1500–2500 MPa** | **2000–3500 MPa** |
| Elastic Modulus | **150–250 GPa** | **30–50 GPa** | **50–80 GPa** |
| Density | 1.5–1.6 g/cm³ | 1.8–2.0 g/cm³ | 1.3–1.4 g/cm³ |
| Corrosion Resistance | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent |
| Cost | High | Low | Medium |
| Application | High-stress retrofits | General strengthening | Impact-resistant structures |

---

#### Part (b) — Solution

**Step 1: Compute confining pressure (2 marks)**

$$
f_l = \dfrac{2 \cdot n \cdot t_f \cdot E_f \cdot \varepsilon_{fe}}{D}
$$

$$
f_l = \dfrac{2 \times 3 \times 1.2 \times 30000 \times 0.004}{300}
$$

$$
f_l = \dfrac{864}{300} = 2.88 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2: Compute confinement ratio (1 mark)**

$$
\dfrac{f_l}{f_{ck}} = \dfrac{2.88}{25} = 0.1152
$$

**Step 3: Apply Lam & Teng formula (2 marks)**

$$
\dfrac{f_{cc}'}{f_{ck}} = 2.254 \sqrt{1 + 7.94 \cdot (f_l / f_{ck})} - 2 \cdot (f_l / f_{ck}) - 1.254
$$

$$
\dfrac{f_{cc}'}{f_{ck}} = 2.254 \sqrt{1 + 7.94 \times 0.1152} - 2 \times 0.1152 - 1.254
$$

$$
\dfrac{f_{cc}'}{f_{ck}} = 2.254 \sqrt{1.9147} - 0.2304 - 1.254
$$

$$
\dfrac{f_{cc}'}{f_{ck}} = 2.254 \times 1.3837 - 1.4844
$$

$$
\dfrac{f_{cc}'}{f_{ck}} = 3.119 - 1.4844 = 1.6346
$$

**Step 4: Compute enhanced strength (1 mark)**

$$
f_{cc}' = 1.6346 \times 25 = 40.87 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5: Interpretation (1 mark)**

> **The FRP wrapping increases compressive strength from 25 MPa to approximately 40.87 MPa — a strength enhancement of ~63%.** This demonstrates the effectiveness of FRP confinement for retrofitting deficient columns.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cause Loss of Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the load factor (1.5)** when converting working load to ultimate load — KTU examiners deduct **2–3 marks** for this.
> 2. **Mixing up $f_y = 415$ MPa with Fe500** — always read the problem statement carefully.
> 3. **Not applying the 0.45 coefficient** to $f_{ck} A_c$ — students often write $f_{ck} A_c + f_y A_{sc}$ directly.
> 4. **Skipping the lower-bound $e_{\min} = 20$ mm** check — must compare and take maximum.
> 5. **In FRP problems, not converting units** — GPa to MPa ($E_f = 30$ GPa = $30000$ MPa) is a frequent error.
> 6. **Writing $A_{sc}$ as gross area** instead of net concrete area — deduct 1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Things to Remember for RC Columns (PEPOT758, Module 2):**

- **Definition:** RC column is a compression member with $\lambda > 3$ (effective length > 3× least lateral dimension).
- **Short column:** $\lambda \leq 12$ → material (crushing) failure.
- **Long column:** $\lambda > 12$ → buckling failure + reduction factor $C_r$.
- **Minimum eccentricity:** $e_{\min} = L/500 + D/30 \geq 20$ mm (IS 456 Cl. 25.4).
- **Axial capacity (LSM):** $P_u = 0.45 f_{ck} A_c + 0.90 f_y A_{sc}$.
- **Effective length factors:** $k = 0.65$ (fixed-fixed), $0.80$ (fixed-pinned), $1.0$ (pinned-pinned), $1.2$ (fixed-free).
- **Reduction factor:** $C_r = 1.25 - L_{\text{eff}}/(48D)$ for $12 < \lambda \leq 24$.
- **Additional moment:** $M_a = P_u \cdot D/2000 \cdot (L_{\text{eff}}/D)^2$.
- **Polymer roles in RC columns:** GFRP rebars, CFRP wraps, PMC, anti-corrosion coatings.
- **Lam & Teng model:** Used to compute enhanced $f_{cc}'$ for FRP-confined circular columns.
- **IS codes to remember:** IS 456:2000 (main), IS 15916:2010 (FRP strengthening), ACI 440.2R-17 (FRP design).
- **Kerala context:** Coastal/marine structures → polymer solutions are practically mandatory.
- **Design procedure:** Step 1 (effective length) → Step 2 (slenderness) → Step 3 (capacity) → Step 4 (P-M interaction check).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Masonry Wall and Infills Strengthening
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT758 - POLYMERS IN CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Masonry Wall and Infills Strengthening

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Masonry Wall Strengthening — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Masonry Wall Strengthening** refers to the set of engineering interventions that enhance the **load-bearing capacity**, **ductility**, **stiffness**, and **durability** of existing masonry walls (brick, stone, or block masonry) that have either deteriorated over time or require upgraded performance to meet current design codes (e.g., **IS 1905, IS 15916, ACI 440.7R, Eurocode 6**).

**Masonry Infill Strengthening**, specifically, addresses the retrofitting of non-structural partition walls built *within* the bays of a reinforced concrete (RC) or steel frame. While the frame resists gravity and lateral loads, the infill contributes to **stiffness**, **energy dissipation**, and **lateral load-sharing** — but only when properly bonded and strengthened.

## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Crumbling Skeleton and the Polymer Skin**
> Imagine an elderly person (the **masonry wall**) trying to walk. Their bones (the **mortar joints and bricks**) are brittle and crack under pressure. Now imagine wrapping their torso with a flexible, ultra-strong carbon-fiber corset (a **Fiber Reinforced Polymer / FRP jacket**). When the person moves, the corset shares the load, prevents sudden collapse, and allows graceful deformation. This is *exactly* what polymer-based strengthening does — it provides an external "tendon-skin" that confines, carries tensile stress, and arrests crack propagation in masonry.

A second analogy: **infill walls in RC frames** are like a **passenger sitting in a moving bus**. If the passenger is loose (unreinforced infill), they slide and cause damage during sudden braking (earthquake). If strapped in with a seat-belt (**polymer ties, FRP diagonal strips, or textile mesh**), they move with the bus safely.

## 1.3 Role of Polymers in Masonry Strengthening

Polymers serve **four primary structural functions** in masonry retrofitting:

| Function | Polymer Form | Real-World Effect |
|----------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Tensile Reinforcement** | FRP laminates, NSM bars | Resists bending & in-plane shear |
| **Confinement** | FRP wraps, textile jackets | Increases compressive capacity & ductility |
| **Crack Sealing / Bonding** | Epoxy resins, polyurethane | Restores monolithic action |
| **Matrix Modification** | Polymer-modified mortar, TRM | Improves substrate cohesion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEPOT758 Module 2):**
> The focus is on **polymer-based** strengthening — distinguishing it from traditional methods like **jacketing with concrete, grout injection, or steel mesh**. The course emphasizes *why polymers are preferred*: corrosion resistance, high strength-to-weight ratio, ease of application, and minimal architectural disruption.

---

## 1.4 Typical Deficiencies in Masonry That Justify Strengthening

Before strengthening, a structural audit identifies the **deficiency mode**. Common failure indicators:

- **Diagonal (X-pattern) cracking** → in-plane shear failure
- **Horizontal bed-joint sliding** → low friction / weak mortar
- **Vertical splitting** → axial overstress or buckling
- **Out-of-plane toppling** → inadequate wall-frame connection
- **Corner crushing** → stress concentration at openings
- **Debonding of plaster** → moisture ingress and steel corrosion in RCC elements

> [!NOTE]
> **Why polymers excel here:** Masonry is **anisotropic**, **brittle**, and **weak in tension** (~**0.5–2 MPa**). Polymers can provide tensile strengths of **2,000–4,000 MPa** (CFRP) with a density of only **1.5–1.6 g/cm³** — making them ideal "external tendons."

---

## 1.5 Why Masonry Infills Need Special Attention

In RC frame buildings, **unreinforced masonry (URM) infills** account for a major share of earthquake damage globally (e.g., **1999 Kocaeli, 2001 Bhuj, 2015 Nepal earthquakes**). The reasons are:

1. **Brittle separation** from the surrounding frame under lateral drift
2. **Soft-story mechanism** when ground-floor infills are absent
3. **Short-column effect** induced by partial-height infills
4. **Out-of-plane collapse** due to lack of tying

Polymer-based strengthening is the **preferred modern retrofit** because it adds negligible mass (avoiding seismic mass penalty) and can be applied from *one face only* — preserving the building's occupancy during works.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Polymer Strengthening in Masonry

Polymers improve masonry performance through **three interrelated mechanisms**, which must be understood before applying any formula:

### Mechanism A — Tensile Carrying (Flexural & Shear Reinforcement)
FRP strips bonded to the wall surface carry tensile stresses that masonry cannot. The load transfer occurs through **shear at the polymer–substrate interface**.

### Mechanism B — Confinement (Compressive Enhancement)
When FRP is wrapped around a masonry column or pier, it applies a **passive lateral confining pressure** as the masonry dilates under axial load. This shifts the failure mode from **brittle crushing** to **ductile confinement failure**.

### Mechanism C — Crack Bridging & Stress Redistribution
Polymer resins (epoxy, polyester) injected into cracks restore continuity. Textile Reinforced Mortar (TRM) systems use a polymer-modified cementitious matrix that **bridges cracks** and redistributes stress over a wider area than epoxy-bonded FRP.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of FRP Strengthening Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **KTU-recommended design philosophy** follows ACI 440.7R (Strengthening of Concrete and Masonry Structures with FRP). The key steps are:

**Step 1 — Demand Determination**
- Compute the **required strengthening force** $F_{req}$ from analysis (factored loads per **IS 456 / IS 1893**).
- Determine the **existing capacity** $F_{exist}$ from material testing (non-destructive tests: rebound hammer, UPV, core tests).
- Calculate the **deficit**: $\Delta F = F_{req} - F_{exist}$

**Step 2 — FRP Selection**
- Choose FRP type (CFRP, GFRP, AFRP) based on **modulus**, **strain capacity**, and **environmental exposure**.
- CFRP: $E_f = 230$ GPa (typical), $\varepsilon_{fu} = 1.5\%$
- GFRP: $E_f = 35$ GPa, $\varepsilon_{fu} = 2.5\%$
- AFRP: $E_f = 70$ GPa, $\varepsilon_{fu} = 2.0\%$

**Step 3 — Effective Strain Determination**
- FRP cannot reach its ultimate strain in masonry due to **premature debonding**. Use a **reduction factor**.

**Step 4 — Capacity Verification**
- Confirm: $F_{frp,provided} \geq \Delta F$ with appropriate safety factor.

**Step 5 — Detailing & Anchorage**
- Ensure development length, end anchorage, and fire protection per code.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula compendium** for masonry wall and infill strengthening using polymers. Master these — they appear directly in KTU university exam Part B questions.

### Table 2.A — FRP Material Properties & Reduction Factors

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value | Unit | Remarks |
|--------|----------|---------------|------|---------|
| $f_{fu}$ | Ultimate tensile strength of FRP | **2,800–4,000** (CFRP); **500–1,500** (GFRP) | MPa | Manufacturer-tested |
| $\varepsilon_{fu}$ | Ultimate tensile strain | **1.0–1.7** (CFRP); **2.0–2.8** (GFRP) | % | Limited by debonding |
| $E_f$ | Elastic modulus | **230** (CFRP); **35–40** (GFRP) | GPa | Linear-elastic to failure |
| $t_f$ | FRP thickness | **0.11–1.4** | mm | Single-ply typical |
| $\kappa_v$ | Bond-dependent shear coefficient | **0.40–0.75** | — | Lower for masonry than concrete |
| $\kappa_m$ | Masonry substrate modifier | **0.65–0.85** | — | Based on substrate quality |
| $\psi_f$ | FRP strength reduction factor | **0.65–0.85** | — | Per ACI 440.7R |
| $f'_m$ | Masonry compressive strength | **4–15** | MPa | From prism tests |
| $f_t$ | Masonry tensile strength | **0.2–0.7** | MPa | ~$5\%$ of $f'_m$ |

### Table 2.B — Governing Design Equations

| Equation | Formula | Application | Units |
|----------|---------|-------------|-------|
| Effective FRP strain | $\varepsilon_{fe} = \kappa_v \cdot \varepsilon_{fu} \le 0.004$ | All masonry FRP design | dimensionless |
| FRP design stress | $f_{fe} = E_f \cdot \varepsilon_{fe}$ | Tensile capacity of FRP | MPa |
| FRP axial force | $F_{frp} = A_f \cdot f_{fe}$ | Total force in FRP reinforcement | N |
| Bond stress (debonding) | $\tau_{max} = 0.25 \cdot \sqrt{f'_c \cdot f'_m}$ | EB-FRP interface check | MPa |
| Development length | $L_d = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{fe} \cdot E_f \cdot t_f}{2 \cdot \tau_{max}}$ | Anchorage verification | mm |
| FRP shear contribution | $V_{frp} = \dfrac{2 \cdot n \cdot t_f \cdot w_f \cdot \varepsilon_{fe} \cdot E_f \cdot d}{s_f}$ | In-plane shear strengthening | N |
| Confined masonry strength | $f'_{mc} = f'_m \cdot \left(1 + k_1 \cdot k_s \cdot \dfrac{2 t_f}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\varepsilon_{fe}}{\varepsilon'_m}\right)$ | FRP-wrapped columns/piers | MPa |
| Out-of-plane moment | $M_n = \varepsilon_{fe} \cdot E_f \cdot A_f \cdot d_f$ | Wall plate bending | N·mm |
| Strain compatibility | $\varepsilon_{fe} = \varepsilon_{mu} \cdot \dfrac{d_f - c}{c}$ | Sectional analysis | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Notice the **upper bound** $\varepsilon_{fe} \le 0.004$ — this is a code-imposed ductility safeguard. Even if the FRP can stretch more, masonry cannot accommodate the deformation without losing integrity.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

These equations are not academic — they directly determine **how many square meters of CFRP** to procure for a hospital retrofit in seismic Zone V (Kerala is **Zone III**, parts of **Zone II/III boundary**, with rising seismic considerations). Engineers use them to:

- **Size FRP strips** on the diagonals of infill panels
- **Determine wrap spacing** for column confinement
- **Verify fire endurance** (typically epoxy loses strength above **60–80 °C**, so a **gypsum or cementitious fire board** of 25–40 mm is added)
- **Calculate life-cycle cost** vs. demolition-rebuild
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Effective Strain in FRP-Strengthened Masonry

The **effective strain** in the FRP at ultimate is *not* the ultimate strain of the material itself because **debonding** always intervenes first.

### Step-by-Step:

**Step 1 — Start with the basic FRP stress-strain relation** (linear elastic to failure):

$$f_f = E_f \cdot \varepsilon_f$$

This is valid because FRPs do not yield — they fail in brittle fracture.

**Step 2 — Define the effective strain** as the strain at which debonding occurs at the FRP-substrate interface:

$$\varepsilon_{fe} = \kappa_v \cdot \varepsilon_{fu}$$

where $\kappa_v$ is the **bond-dependent coefficient for shear** (accounts for masonry being weaker than concrete). For masonry:

$$\kappa_v = \frac{k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot L_e}{11{,}900 \cdot \varepsilon_{fu}} \le 0.75$$

with $L_e = \sqrt{\dfrac{E_f \cdot t_f}{2 \cdot f'_m}}$ being the **effective bond length** (mm).

**Step 3 — Compute $L_e$ for a typical case**: Assume $E_f = 230$ GPa, $t_f = 0.165$ mm, $f'_m = 7$ MPa.

$$L_e = \sqrt{\frac{230{,}000 \cdot 0.165}{2 \cdot 7}} = \sqrt{2710.7} \approx 52 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Plug into $\kappa_v$**: Using $k_1 = 1.0$, $k_2 = 0.85$ (masonry), $\varepsilon_{fu} = 0.015$:

$$\kappa_v = \frac{1.0 \cdot 0.85 \cdot 52}{11{,}900 \cdot 0.015} = \frac{44.2}{178.5} = 0.248$$

But $\kappa_v$ has a lower bound of 0.40 for masonry (per code). So:

$$\kappa_v = 0.40$$

**Step 5 — Compute effective strain**:

$$\varepsilon_{fe} = 0.40 \cdot 0.015 = 0.006 = 0.6\%$$

**Step 6 — Apply code upper bound**:

$$\varepsilon_{fe} = \min(0.006,\ 0.004) = 0.004 = 0.4\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The FRP can only be stressed to $0.4\%$ strain, despite being capable of $1.5\%$. This represents a **$73\%$ reduction** in usable capacity — a critical KTU exam takeaway.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shear Capacity Enhancement of Infill Walls

A common KTU problem: **strengthen an infill wall for in-plane shear using diagonal FRP strips.**

### Problem Setup:
- Wall dimensions: $L_w = 4$ m, $h_w = 3$ m, $t_w = 230$ mm
- Mortar class: $f'_m = 5$ MPa
- Two diagonal CFRP strips of width $w_f = 300$ mm, thickness $t_f = 0.165$ mm
- Strip spacing (effective): $s_f = 1{,}500$ mm (one strip per diagonal direction)
- $E_f = 230$ GPa, $\varepsilon_{fu} = 0.015$, $\kappa_v = 0.40$
- Wall depth to centroid of FRP: $d = 4{,}000$ mm

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Effective strain** (from Derivation 1):

$$\varepsilon_{fe} = 0.004$$

**Step 2 — Effective FRP stress**:

$$f_{fe} = E_f \cdot \varepsilon_{fe} = 230{,}000 \cdot 0.004 = 920 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Number of FRP layers**: $n = 1$.

**Step 4 — Compute FRP shear contribution**:

$$V_{frp} = \frac{2 \cdot n \cdot t_f \cdot w_f \cdot \varepsilon_{fe} \cdot E_f \cdot d}{s_f}$$

$$V_{frp} = \frac{2 \cdot 1 \cdot 0.165 \cdot 300 \cdot 0.004 \cdot 230{,}000 \cdot 4{,}000}{1{,}500}$$

**Step 5 — Numerator evaluation**:

$$\text{Numerator} = 2 \cdot 0.165 \cdot 300 \cdot 0.004 \cdot 230{,}000 \cdot 4{,}000$$

$$= 2 \cdot 0.165 = 0.330$$

$$0.330 \cdot 300 = 99.0$$

$$99.0 \cdot 0.004 = 0.396$$

$$0.396 \cdot 230{,}000 = 91{,}080$$

$$91{,}080 \cdot 4{,}000 = 3.6432 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 6 — Division by $s_f$**:

$$V_{frp} = \frac{3.6432 \times 10^{8}}{1{,}500} = 2.4288 \times 10^{5} \text{ N} = 242.9 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 7 — Existing masonry shear capacity** (for comparison):

$$V_m = 0.125 \cdot f'_m \cdot t_w \cdot d = 0.125 \cdot 5 \cdot 230 \cdot 4{,}000 = 5.75 \times 10^{5} \text{ N} = 575 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 8 — Total enhanced capacity**:

$$V_{total} = V_m + \psi_f \cdot V_{frp} = 575 + 0.85 \cdot 242.9 = 575 + 206.5 = 781.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 9 — Enhancement ratio**:

$$\frac{V_{total}}{V_m} = \frac{781.5}{575} = 1.36 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 36\% \text{ increase}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Two diagonal CFRP strips boost in-plane shear capacity by ~**$36\%$** while adding only **$\approx 0.8$ kg/m²** — a remarkable strength-to-weight gain that defines polymer retrofitting.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Confined Masonry Pier Capacity (FRP Wrap)

For a masonry pier ($D = 400$ mm, height $= 3$ m) wrapped with one-ply GFRP ($t_f = 1.4$ mm, $E_f = 35$ GPa, $\varepsilon_{fu} = 0.025$).

**Step 1 — Effective strain**:

$$\varepsilon_{fe} = \kappa_v \cdot \varepsilon_{fu} = 0.40 \cdot 0.025 = 0.010$$

Apply upper bound: $\varepsilon_{fe} = \min(0.010,\ 0.004) = 0.004$

**Step 2 — Confinement parameters**: $k_1 = 1.0$ (circular), $k_s = 0.85$ (masonry).

**Step 3 — Compute confined strength**:

$$f'_{mc} = f'_m \cdot \left(1 + k_1 \cdot k_s \cdot \frac{2 t_f}{D} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_{fe}}{\varepsilon'_m}\right)$$

Assume $\varepsilon'_m = 0.002$ (peak strain of unconfined masonry), $f'_m = 7$ MPa.

$$\frac{2 t_f}{D} = \frac{2 \cdot 1.4}{400} = 0.007$$

$$\frac{\varepsilon_{fe}}{\varepsilon'_m} = \frac{0.004}{0.002} = 2.0$$

$$f'_{mc} = 7 \cdot \left(1 + 1.0 \cdot 0.85 \cdot 0.007 \cdot 2.0\right)$$

$$= 7 \cdot (1 + 0.0119) = 7 \cdot 1.0119 = 7.083 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The confined strength gain appears modest here because the **section is squat** ($D/h = 0.13$). For slender piers with smaller $D$, confinement becomes far more effective. This subtlety is a favorite KTU examiner question.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Strengthening Techniques

| Technique | Polymer Form | Best For | Capacity Gain | Limitation |
|-----------|--------------|----------|---------------|------------|
| **EB-CFRP Strips** | Carbon fiber + epoxy | In-plane shear, flexure | **30–80%** | Brittle debonding |
| **EB-GFRP Sheets** | Glass + epoxy | Out-of-plane bending | **20–50%** | Lower modulus |
| **NSM-FRP Bars** | FRP rods in slots | Flexural upgrade of beams/walls | **40–100%** | Slot cutting weakens section |
| **FRP Wrap** | Continuous fiber + resin | Confinement of piers/columns | **20–60%** axial | Fire vulnerability |
| **TRM / FRCM** | Textile + polymer-modified mortar | Multi-directional, seismic | **25–70%** | Lower efficiency than EB-FRP |
| **Epoxy Injection** | Low-viscosity resin | Crack repair only | **Restores original** | Not a strength upgrade |
| **Polymer Grouting** | Polyurethane / acrylic | Void filling, waterproofing | **Indirect** | Limited structural role |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Practice Problem (Self-Assessment)

A brick infill wall in an RC frame needs to resist a factored in-plane shear demand of **$V_u = 650$ kN**. Existing masonry provides **$V_m = 380$ kN**. The engineer proposes CFRP diagonal strips ($E_f = 230$ GPa, $t_f = 0.131$ mm, $\varepsilon_{fu} = 0.017$, $w_f = 250$ mm, $s_f = 1{,}500$ mm, $d = 3{,}500$ mm). Verify adequacy.

**Solution Outline:**

$$\varepsilon_{fe} = \min(0.40 \cdot 0.017,\ 0.004) = \min(0.0068,\ 0.004) = 0.004$$

$$V_{frp} = \frac{2 \cdot 1 \cdot 0.131 \cdot 250 \cdot 0.004 \cdot 230{,}000 \cdot 3{,}500}{1{,}500} = 1.406 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 140.6 \text{ kN}$$

$$V_{total} = 380 + 0.85 \cdot 140.6 = 380 + 119.5 = 499.5 \text{ kN}$$

Since $V_{total} = 499.5$ kN $< V_u = 650$ kN → **NOT ADEQUATE**. Engineer must increase strip width or add a second ply.

$$w_{f,\text{new}} = \frac{(650 - 380) \cdot 1{,}500}{0.85 \cdot 2 \cdot 0.131 \cdot 0.004 \cdot 230{,}000 \cdot 3{,}500} \approx 397 \text{ mm}$$

Provide **$w_f = 400$ mm** in two diagonals. ✅
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Decision Flow for Selecting Polymer Strengthening Technique

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Assess Masonry Deficiency] --> B{Deficiency Type}
    B -->|In-plane Shear| C[Diagonal CFRP Strips]
    B -->|Out-of-plane Flexure| D[Vertical GFRP or CFRP Strips]
    B -->|Axial Confinement| E[FRP Wrap or TRM Jacket]
    B -->|Crack Repair Only| F[Epoxy or Polyurethane Injection]
    C --> G[Verify Bond and Anchorage]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> H[Quality Control and Pull-off Test]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Apply Fire Protection Board]
    I --> J[Commissioning]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Polymer Strengthening System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Masonry Substrate]
        M1[Brick or Block]
        M2[Mortar Joints]
    end
    subgraph S2[Polymer Strengthening Layer]
        P1[Primer Coat]
        P2[Resin Matrix]
        P3[FRP Reinforcement]
        P4[Topcoat or Fire Board]
    end
    subgraph S3[Load Transfer Zone]
        L1[Adhesion]
        L2[Shear at Interface]
        L3[Anchorage]
    end
    M1 --> L1
    M2 --> L1
    L1 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> P4
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Force Flow in FRP-Strengthened Infill

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Lateral Seismic Load] --> B[RC Frame Top Beam]
    B --> C[Infill Panel]
    C --> D[Diagonal Compression Strut]
    C --> E[FRP Diagonal Tension Tie]
    D --> F[RC Frame Bottom Beam]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Foundation]
    E -.debonds.-> H[Premature Failure Avoided by Anchorage]
```

## 4.4 Schematic Description (Text Representation)

Since masonry wall detailing is best understood graphically, here is a **structured text schematic** for FRP strengthening layouts:

```
   ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │   RC Top Beam                           │
   ├════════════════════════════════════════┤  ← CFRP horizontal strip (anchor)
   │ ┌──┐                                  ┌──┐ │
   │ │  │\                              /│  │ │
   │ │  │ \   Diagonal CFRP Strip     /  │  │ │
   │ │  │  \                          /   │  │ │
   │ │  │   \                        /    │  │ │
   │ │  │    \                      /     │  │ │
   │ │  │     \                    /      │  │ │
   │ │  │      \    [Masonry       /       │  │ │
   │ │  │       \    Infill]      /        │  │ │
   │ │  │        \              /          │  │ │
   │ │  │         \            /           │  │ │
   │ │  │          \          /            │  │ │
   │ │  │           \        /             │  │ │
   │ │  │            \      /              │  │ │
   │ │  │             \    /               │  │ │
   │ │  │              \  /                │  │ │
   │ │  │               \/                 │  │ │
   │ │  │              /\                  │  │ │
   │ │  │             /  \                 │  │ │
   │ └──┘────────────/────\───────────────└──┘ │
   ├════════════════════════════════════════┤  ← CFRP horizontal strip (anchor)
   │   RC Bottom Beam                        │
   └────────────────────────────────────────┘

LEGEND:
═══  CFRP anchor strip (horizontal)
\ \  Diagonal CFRP tension tie
[ ]  Unreinforced masonry infill
```

## 4.5 Schematic — TRM (Textile Reinforced Mortar) Application on Wall Face

```
   ┌───────────────────────────────────────┐
   │  Polymer-modified plaster (10-15 mm)  │
   │  ──────────────────────────────       │
   │  Glass / Carbon / Basalt Textile      │
   │  (single or double layer, mesh 5x5 mm)│
   │  ──────────────────────────────       │
   │  Bonding primer coat                  │
   │  ──────────────────────────────       │
   │  EXISTING MASONRY SUBSTRATE           │
   │  (possibly roughened by sandblasting) │
   └───────────────────────────────────────┘

Anchorage: Textile is wrapped around corners
           and lapped ≥ 200 mm at splices.
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any three polymer-based techniques used for strengthening masonry walls. Mention one limitation of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Externally Bonded FRP (EB-FRP) Laminates:** Carbon or glass fiber sheets bonded with epoxy. Used for flexural and shear strengthening.
   - *Limitation:* Susceptible to debonding failure and poor fire resistance (epoxy softens above **60 °C**).

2. **Textile Reinforced Mortar (TRM) / FRCM Systems:** Fiber textile embedded in polymer-modified cementitious mortar. Used for seismic upgrade.
   - *Limitation:* Lower strengthening efficiency (~**60–70%** of EB-FRP for same fiber weight).

3. **Near-Surface Mounted (NSM) FRP Bars:** FRP rods inserted into masonry grooves and bonded with epoxy or grout.
   - *Limitation:* Groove cutting reduces the cross-section and may damage masonry units; requires careful detailing.

*(1 mark for listing each technique + limitation)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Externally Bonded (EB) and Near-Surface Mounted (NSM) FRP techniques for masonry.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | EB-FRP | NSM-FRP |
|--------|--------|---------|
| **Placement** | Bonded on wall surface | Embedded in grooves cut into masonry |
| **Bond Mechanism** | Surface adhesion (epoxy) | Mechanical interlock + adhesion |
| **Debonding Risk** | High | Low (better bond utilization) |
| **Aesthetic Impact** | Visible unless covered | Hidden within grooves |
| **Fire Resistance** | Poor (epoxy exposure) | Better (matrix cover protects) |
| **Strain Efficiency** | ~40–60% of $\varepsilon_{fu}$ | ~70–90% of $\varepsilon_{fu}$ |

*(1.5 marks for table; 1.5 marks for explaining EB vs NSM bond mechanics)*

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Discuss in detail the various polymer-based techniques used for strengthening masonry walls, with their field of application and limitations. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Polymer-based masonry strengthening techniques can be classified into four families based on the polymer form and structural function:

**1. Fiber Reinforced Polymer (FRP) Systems**

FRP systems use high-strength fibers (carbon, glass, aramid, basalt) embedded in a polymer matrix (typically epoxy). They are available as **laminates, sheets, and bars**.

- **EB-CFRP Strips:** High stiffness (E = 230 GPa), used for flexural and shear strengthening of beams, slabs, and walls. Field application includes heritage building retrofit where minimal cross-section increase is desired.
  - *Limitation:* Brittle debonding; fire vulnerability; expensive.

- **EB-GFRP Sheets:** Lower modulus (E = 35 GPa) but high strain capacity. Used where cost is a constraint and where corrosion resistance is paramount.
  - *Limitation:* Lower stiffness leads to larger deflections.

- **NSM-FRP Bars:** Inserted into slits cut into masonry, bonded with epoxy. Ideal for negative moment regions of beams and for confining corners of walls.
  - *Limitation:* Requires substrate preparation; groove cutting disturbs masonry.

**2. Textile Reinforced Mortar (TRM) / Fiber Reinforced Cementitious Matrix (FRCM)**

Combines a fiber textile (carbon, glass, basalt, PBO) with a **polymer-modified inorganic mortar** matrix. The matrix is cement-based with acrylic or styrene-butadiene polymer latex additives.

- **Application:** Multi-directional strengthening, seismic upgrade of infill walls, strengthening of masonry vaults and domes.
- *Advantage:* Compatible with masonry substrates; better fire resistance; vapor permeability.
- *Limitation:* Lower strengthening efficiency; requires skilled labor for multi-layer application.

**3. Polymer-Modified Mortars and Grouts**

Used as **substrate repair** before applying FRP, and as **matrix component** of TRM systems.

- **Styrene-Butadiene Rubber (SBR) modified mortar:** Improves adhesion by ~**40%** and reduces shrinkage cracking.
- **Acrylic-modified mortar:** Enhances tensile bond strength and weather resistance.
- *Application:* Surface levelling, joint repointing, substrate consolidation.

**4. Polymer Resins for Crack Injection**

Low-viscosity **epoxy resins** (for structural cracks) and **polyurethane resins** (for water-bearing cracks) are pressure-injected into masonry cracks.

- **Application:** Restoration of monolithic action, sealing of active and dormant cracks.
- *Limitation:* Not a strength upgrade; only restores original capacity; resin may yellow or degrade under UV.

**Summary (1 mark for tabulation):**

| Technique | Best For | Limitation |
|-----------|----------|------------|
| EB-FRP | Flexure, shear | Debonding, fire |
| NSM-FRP | Flexure, anchorage | Substrate damage |
| TRM/FRCM | Seismic, multi-axial | Lower efficiency |
| Polymer mortar | Substrate prep | Limited structural role |
| Resin injection | Crack repair | No strength upgrade |

*(Distribution: 1 mark per major family + 1 mark for application + 1 mark for limitation + 1 mark for tabulation/closure)*

---

**(b) Explain the design procedure for FRP strengthening of masonry infill walls, including the relevant IS / ACI code provisions. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The design of FRP-strengthened masonry infill walls follows a **demand-capacity** philosophy codified in **ACI 440.7R-10**, **fib Bulletin 14**, and informed by **IS 15916 (Seismic Retrofit of Buildings)**.

**Step 1 — Structural Assessment (1 mark)**

- Conduct in-situ tests: rebound hammer, ultrasonic pulse velocity, flat-jack tests for $f'_m$.
- Determine existing in-plane and out-of-plane capacity using strut-and-tie or equivalent diagonal strut models.
- Identify the demand from **IS 1893 (Part 1)** seismic analysis.

**Step 2 — Selection of FRP System (1 mark)**

- Decide on EB-CFRP / EB-GFRP / TRM based on exposure, fire, and aesthetic requirements.
- Verify material properties per **ACI 440.7R Table 5.1** (typical values).

**Step 3 — Effective Strain Calculation (2 marks)**

$$\varepsilon_{fe} = \kappa_v \cdot \varepsilon_{fu} \le 0.004$$

For masonry, $\kappa_v$ ranges from **0.40 to 0.75**, depending on bond quality and fiber type.

**Step 4 — Capacity Verification — In-Plane Shear (2 marks)**

$$V_{frp} = \frac{2 \cdot n \cdot t_f \cdot w_f \cdot \varepsilon_{fe} \cdot E_f \cdot d}{s_f}$$

- Total capacity: $\phi V_n = \phi (V_m + \psi_f \cdot V_{frp})$
- Strength reduction factor $\phi = 0.75$; FRP factor $\psi_f = 0.85$.

**Step 5 — Detailing & Anchorage (1 mark)**

- Provide **development length** $L_d \geq 200$ mm at FRP ends.
- Use **mechanical anchors** (FRP spike anchors or steel through-bolts) at critical locations.
- Apply **fire protection board** of 25–40 mm gypsum or calcium silicate over FRP in fire-critical zones.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) Explain the mechanism of FRP-to-masonry bond and the factors affecting bond strength. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Mechanism of Bond (3 marks):**

The bond between FRP and masonry is the **load-transfer interface** that enables composite action. The mechanism involves three sequential stages:

1. **Mechanical Adhesion:** The epoxy resin penetrates the porous mortar joints and surface irregularities of bricks, forming micro-mechanical interlocks (~**0.1–0.5 mm** penetration depth).

2. **Chemical Adhesion:** Polar groups in the epoxy (-OH, -NH-) form hydrogen bonds and van der Waals forces with the mineral substrate (Si-O-Si, Ca-OH groups in mortar).

3. **Friction Transfer:** After initial debonding, residual load is carried by interfacial friction ($\mu \approx 0.6$–$0.8$).

**Bond Stress Distribution:** Along the bond length, the shear stress is **non-uniform**, peaking near the loaded end and dropping to zero at the far end. This justifies the **effective bond length** $L_e$ concept — beyond this length, no additional force can be transferred.

**Factors Affecting Bond Strength (4 marks):**

| Factor | Influence |
|--------|-----------|
| **Substrate Strength ($f'_m$)** | Higher $f'_m$ → higher $\tau_{max}$. Approximately $\tau_{max} \propto \sqrt{f'_m}$ |
| **Surface Preparation** | Sandblasting, water-jetting, or grinding improves bond by **40–80%** |
| **FRP Stiffness ($E_f \cdot t_f$)** | Stiffer FRP concentrates stress, reducing effective bond length but raising peak shear |
| **Bond Length** | Effective only up to $L_e$; further extension has no benefit |
| **Adhesive Type** | Epoxy > Polyester > Vinylester for masonry |
| **Moisture Content** | Damp substrates reduce bond by **20–50%** |
| **Temperature** | Bond degrades above glass transition ($T_g \approx$ 60–80 °C) |
| **Number of Plies** | Each additional ply concentrates stress near the loaded end; multi-ply debonds at ~70% of single-ply efficiency |

---

**(b) Describe in-plane and out-of-plane strengthening of infill walls using Textile Reinforced Mortar (TRM) systems. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**

TRM (also called FRCM — Fabric Reinforced Cementitious Matrix) systems consist of a fiber textile (carbon, glass, basalt, or PBO) embedded in a polymer-modified inorganic mortar. Unlike EB-FRP, the matrix is cement-based, making it **compatible with masonry** in terms of vapor permeability, stiffness, and thermal expansion.

**In-Plane Strengthening (3 marks):**

For in-plane shear resistance of infill walls, TRM is applied on **both faces** of the wall in the following configurations:

- **Diagonal Strip Configuration:** Textile strips (width 300–600 mm) bonded along the diagonals of the infill panel. These resist tensile stresses that the masonry strut cannot carry alone.
- **X-Cross Configuration:** Two perpendicular textile layers covering the full panel area; provides bidirectional reinforcement.
- **Frame-Joint Configuration:** TRM strips applied along the wall-frame interface to prevent premature separation.

**Design Equation for In-Plane Shear (TRM):**

$$V_{TRM} = 0.5 \cdot \alpha_t \cdot t_f \cdot E_f \cdot \varepsilon_{fe} \cdot b_w$$

where $\alpha_t$ is the textile efficiency factor (typically **0.40–0.60**), reflecting partial composite action.

**Out-of-Plane Strengthening (3 marks):**

Out-of-plane failure is a **life-safety** concern during earthquakes. TRM strengthening involves:

- **Vertical Strips on Wall Faces:** Textile strips bonded vertically (and sometimes horizontally in a mesh pattern) on the wall face. These act as a **tensile membrane** when the wall bends out-of-plane.
- **Through-Thickness Connectors:** Carbon or glass spikes passing through the wall to connect front and back TRM layers, creating a sandwich action.
- **Arching Action Enhancement:** When properly anchored at top and bottom, TRM helps develop **arching action** in the wall, dramatically increasing out-of-plane capacity.

**Design Equation for Out-of-Plane Bending:**

$$M_n = \varepsilon_{fe} \cdot E_f \cdot A_f \cdot d_f \cdot \psi_f$$

with $\varepsilon_{fe}$ typically limited to **0.003–0.004** for TRM (slightly lower than EB-FRP due to matrix stiffness).

**Advantages of TRM over EB-FRP (bonus 1 mark):**
- Better fire resistance (inorganic matrix)
- Vapor permeability matches masonry
- Applicable on damp substrates
- Easier quality control on uneven surfaces

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfalls That Cost Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\varepsilon_{fe} \le 0.004$ cap:** Many students compute the full ultimate strain and lose **2–3 marks** for not applying the ductility safeguard.
> 2. **Confusing masonry $\kappa_v$ with concrete $\kappa_v$:** Concrete uses $\kappa_v \approx 0.40$–0.65; masonry is **0.40–0.75** depending on fiber type. Mixing up these values is a top deduction reason.
> 3. **Ignoring substrate preparation:** Design answers that omit surface preparation (sandblasting, primer) are marked down **1–2 marks** — a *very common omission*.
> 4. **No fire protection discussion:** KTU explicitly emphasizes fire vulnerability of epoxy-based FRP. Omit this and lose marks.
> 5. **Using $f'_c$ instead of $f'_m$:** Masonry uses $f'_m$ (masonry prism strength), not $f'_c$ (concrete cylinder strength). Numerical errors from this swap are penalised heavily.
> 6. **No units in final answer:** Always state N, kN, mm, MPa — never leave unitless results.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Masonry Wall and Infills Strengthening**

### Key Polymer Forms (recall instantly)
- **FRP** — Fiber (CFRP/GFRP/AFRP) + Polymer resin (epoxy)
- **TRM / FRCM** — Textile + Polymer-modified mortar
- **NSM** — FRP bars embedded in slots
- **Polymer-modified mortar** — SBR, acrylic latex additives
- **Resin injection** — Epoxy / polyurethane

### Top 10 Formulas to Memorize
1. $\varepsilon_{fe} = \kappa_v \cdot \varepsilon_{fu} \le 0.004$
2. $f_{fe} = E_f \cdot \varepsilon_{fe}$
3. $V_{frp} = \dfrac{2 \cdot n \cdot t_f \cdot w_f \cdot \varepsilon_{fe} \cdot E_f \cdot d}{s_f}$
4. $V_{total} = V_m + \psi_f \cdot V_{frp}$ with $\psi_f = 0.85$
5. $\tau_{max} = 0.25 \cdot \sqrt{f'_c \cdot f'_m}$
6. $L_d = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{fe} \cdot E_f \cdot t_f}{2 \cdot \tau_{max}}$
7. $f'_{mc} = f'_m \left(1 + k_1 k_s \cdot \dfrac{2 t_f}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\varepsilon_{fe}}{\varepsilon'_m}\right)$
8. $M_n = \varepsilon_{fe} \cdot E_f \cdot A_f \cdot d_f$
9. $\phi = 0.75$ (masonry), $\phi = 0.90$ (concrete)
10. Effective bond length: $L_e = \sqrt{\dfrac{E_f t_f}{2 f'_m}}$

### Five Code Limits to Never Forget
- $\varepsilon_{fe} \le 0.004$ (masonry, ACI 440.7R)
- $\kappa_v \ge 0.40$ for masonry
- Fire protection mandatory above **$T_g \approx 60$ °C**
- Minimum FRP development length: **$L_e \ge 200$ mm**
- Maximum FRP strengthening gain in shear: **$\le 2V_m$** (avoid over-strengthening)

### Failure Modes Checklist
- **Debonding** — most common FRP failure
- **FRP rupture** — desirable (ductile if preceded by debonding)
- **Masonry crushing** — substrate-governed
- **Sliding shear** — bed joint failure
- **Out-of-plane toppling** — wall-frame separation

### Real-World Engineering Hooks
- CFRP retrofit for **Kerala heritage buildings** in seismic Zone III
- TRM preferred for **temple masonry and lime-mortar walls**
- GFRP chosen where **cost and corrosion resistance** dominate
- Epoxy injection standard for **post-flood crack repair** in Kerala
- Polymer-modified mortar essential before FRP application on **weak substrates**

### One-Line Exam Punch Lines
- *"Masonry is strong in compression, weak in tension — polymers fill the tensile gap."*
- *"EB-FRP gives capacity, NSM gives ductility, TRM gives compatibility."*
- *"The bond is the system — without it, FRP is just wallpaper."*
- *"$\varepsilon_{fe} \le 0.004$ — masonry governs, not the FRP."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polymer Foams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT758 - POLYMERS IN CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Polymer Foams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **polymer foam** is a two-phase composite material consisting of a **solid polymer matrix** (continuous phase) and a **gaseous phase** (dispersed phase, typically air or a blowing agent gas) entrapped within a cellular structure of cells (also called voids, pores, or bubbles). In the context of KTU's Polymers in Construction syllabus, polymer foams are classified as **cellular polymeric materials** with a cellular morphology that can be either:

- **Closed-cell foam**: Each cell is completely enclosed by thin polymer membranes (cell walls + cell faces), trapping the blowing gas inside.
- **Open-cell foam**: The cell walls are ruptured or absent, allowing free communication of gas/fluid between adjacent cells, creating an interconnected porous network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact Terminology):**
> "Polymer foams are lightweight, cellular engineering materials produced by the expansion of a polymer matrix through the action of physical or chemical blowing agents, resulting in a controlled porous morphology used primarily for thermal insulation, acoustic damping, lightweight structural fill, and energy absorption in civil engineering applications."

**Key Engineering Metrics Used in KTU Evaluations:**
- **Density (ρ):** Expressed in **kg/m³** (typical range 10–200 kg/m³ for construction foams).
- **Cell Size (d):** Typically **0.1 mm to 5 mm**.
- **Porosity (φ):** Volume fraction of gas phase, dimensionless (0 to 1).
- **Thermal Conductivity (k):** Measured in **W/m·K** (typical rigid PU foam: **0.020–0.028 W/m·K**).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Bread vs. Sponge" Analogy:**
> Think of a polymer foam as a **baked cake or bread slice**:
> - The **cake body (crumb)** represents the solid polymer walls (struts and faces).
> - The **air pockets** inside the cake represent the gas-filled cells.
> - A **closed-cell foam** is like a cake with thin, intact membranes sealing each air pocket (e.g., a chocolate-coated marshmallow).
> - An **open-cell foam** is like a **natural sea sponge** — water (or air) flows freely from one hole to the next because the walls are torn open.
>
> **Why this matters in construction:** A sponge absorbs water and sound (open-cell), while a sealed marshmallow traps air and insulates thermally (closed-cell). This single analogy captures **~70% of all KTU questions** on foam structure-property relationships.

## 1.3 Classification of Polymer Foams (KTU-Mandated)

| Classification Basis | Type | Construction Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Cell Structure** | Closed-Cell | Rigid **Polyurethane (PU)** insulation boards |
| | Open-Cell | Flexible **Polyethylene (PE)** acoustic batts |
| **Rigidness** | Rigid foams | XPS (extruded polystyrene) roof insulation |
| | Flexible foams | PU seat cushions (rarely used in load-bearing) |
| **Polymer Base** | Thermoplastic foams | EPS, XPS, PVC foams |
| | Thermoset foams | PU, Phenolic, Urea-formaldehyde foams |
| **Density** | Low (<50 kg/m³) | Lightweight void fillers |
| | Medium (50–200 kg/m³) | Insulation panels |
| | High (>200 kg/m³) | Structural foams (e.g., structural PU) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Fact:** The single most-examined foam in Module 3 is **Polyurethane (PU) rigid foam**, owing to its **lowest thermal conductivity among commercial insulators (~0.020 W/m·K)**. Always remember this value for Part A questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Steps of Foam Formation (The "How" Behind the Bubbles)

The formation of a polymer foam follows a well-defined sequence that examiners love to test:

1. **Nucleation:** The blowing agent (gas) becomes supersaturated in the polymer melt/precursor. Tiny gas bubbles form at nucleation sites (often aided by **nucleating agents** like talc or calcium carbonate).
   - *Why it matters:* Nucleation density controls **cell size and uniformity** — finer cells = better mechanical and insulating properties.

2. **Growth:** Gas diffuses into the nucleated bubbles, causing them to expand. The polymer matrix deforms viscoelastically to accommodate the growth.
   - *Why it matters:* If growth is uncontrolled, cells coalesce → non-uniform, weak foam.

3. **Stabilization (Rheological Lock-in):** As the polymer cools (thermoplastic) or crosslinks (thermoset), its viscosity and modulus rise sharply, freezing the cellular structure in place.

4. **Curing/Solidification:** Final crosslinking or cooling locks the cells permanently. Surface tension, viscosity, and blowing-agent pressure are now in equilibrium.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Competing Forces During Foaming:**
> - **Blowing-agent vapor pressure** → tries to *expand* the cell.
> - **Polymer melt elasticity/surface tension** → tries to *shrink* the cell.
> - **External atmospheric pressure** → *compresses* the cell.
> Equilibrium between these three determines the final cell size.

## 2.2 Blowing Agents — The "Engine" of Foam Formation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Blowing agents are the most-tested sub-topic in KTU Module 3.** Memorize the two main categories:

| Type | Mechanism | Examples | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Physical Blowing Agents (PBA)** | Volatilize due to heat (phase change) | **CFCs, HCFCs, HFCs, n-pentane, cyclopentane, CO₂** | CO₂ and pentane are eco-friendly replacements for CFCs |
| **Chemical Blowing Agents (CBA)** | Decompose thermally releasing gas | **Azodicarbonamide (ADC), Sodium bicarbonate, Zinc carbonate** | Used in PVC, PE foams; gas (N₂, CO₂, NH₃) is a by-product of reaction |

- **Endothermic CBA**: Absorb heat during decomposition (e.g., sodium bicarbonate + citric acid). Gives finer cells.
- **Exothermic CBA**: Release heat during decomposition (e.g., ADC). Gives higher expansion ratios.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations form the **analytical backbone** of all KTU numerical problems on polymer foams. Note the use of `\vert` for absolute values to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho_f = \rho_s (1 - \phi_g)$ | Foam density from solid density $\rho_s$ and gas fraction $\phi_g$ |
| 2 | $\phi_g = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_f}{\rho_s}$ | Porosity (gas volume fraction) of foam |
| 3 | $\rho_{rel} = \dfrac{\rho_f}{\rho_s}$ | Relative density (dimensionless, 0 to 1) |
| 4 | $E_f = E_s \cdot C_E \left(\dfrac{\rho_f}{\rho_s}\right)^2$ | **Gibson-Ashby model** for Young's modulus of open-cell foam |
| 5 | $\sigma_{pl} = \sigma_{ys} \cdot C_\sigma \left(\dfrac{\rho_f}{\rho_s}\right)^{3/2}$ | Plastic collapse stress of open-cell foam |
| 6 | $k_{foam} = k_s (1-\phi) + k_g \phi + k_{rad} + k_{conv}$ | Thermal conductivity (parallel-series model) |
| 7 | $\dfrac{1}{k_{series}} = \dfrac{1-\phi}{k_s} + \dfrac{\phi}{k_g}$ | Series thermal model (struts in line with heat flow) |
| 8 | $N_c = \left(\dfrac{n \cdot M^2}{A}\right)^{3/2}$ | Cell density (cells/cm³) from 2D micrograph analysis |
| 9 | $R = \dfrac{d}{\lambda_f}$ | Thermal resistance of foam layer of thickness $d$ |
