<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define thermoplastic foams. List any four commonly used thermoplastic resins in foam manufacturing.*

**Model Answer:**
Thermoplastic foams are lightweight, cellular polymeric materials produced by the expansion of a thermoplastic resin using a blowing agent, resulting in a structure composed of a solid polymer phase and a gaseous phase. The thermoplastic character allows the material to be repeatedly softened by heating and hardened by cooling.

**Four commonly used thermoplastic resins for foam manufacturing:**
1. Polystyrene (PS) — for EPS and XPS insulation boards.
2. Polyethylene (PE) — for pipe insulation and expansion joint fillers.
3. Polypropylene (PP) — for high-temperature applications.
4. Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC) — for rigid foamed profiles.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Explain the difference between open-cell and closed-cell foams with one construction application of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Open-Cell Foam | Closed-Cell Foam |
|---------|----------------|------------------|
| Cell Structure | Interconnected pores; gas can flow between cells | Each cell is sealed; gas trapped inside |
| Density | Lower (10–50 kg/m³) | Slightly higher (15–80 kg/m³) |
| Water Absorption | High (acts as sponge) | Very low (waterproof) |
| Sound Absorption | Excellent | Poor |
| Thermal Insulation | Moderate | Excellent |

**Applications:**
- **Open-cell:** Acoustic insulation panels in auditoriums and office partitions.
- **Closed-cell:** External wall insulation boards (XPS) and roof insulation.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **four sequential stages** involved in the manufacture of thermoplastic foams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **total thermal conductivity of a closed-cell thermoplastic foam** and identify the contribution of each heat-transfer mechanism. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four Sequential Stages of Foam Manufacture:**

**Stage 1 — Polymer Melting and Plastication:**
The thermoplastic resin (e.g., polystyrene pellets) is fed into an extruder or batch autoclave, where it is heated above its glass transition temperature ($T_g$) to form a homogeneous, viscous polymer melt.

**Stage 2 — Blowing Agent Impregnation:**
A physical blowing agent (e.g., pentane, CO₂) or chemical blowing agent (e.g., azodicarbonamide) is introduced under high pressure. The gas dissolves into the melt, forming a single-phase polymer-gas solution governed by Henry's Law: $C = H \cdot P$.

**Stage 3 — Nucleation and Cell Growth:**
When the pressure is suddenly released (exit from the die) or temperature is raised, the system becomes **supersaturated**. The dissolved gas precipitates, forming embryonic bubbles. These bubbles grow according to the Rayleigh-Plesset equation.

**Stage 4 — Stabilization and Solidification:**
The polymer melt cools, viscosity rises sharply, and the cellular structure is "frozen" in place. The result is a stable foam with closed or open cells depending on formulation.

**(b) Derivation of Total Thermal Conductivity:**

The total thermal conductivity of a closed-cell foam is the sum of four contributions:

$$k_{total} = k_{solid} + k_{gas} + k_{radiation} + k_{convection}$$

**Solid-Phase Conduction:** Heat flows through the polymer cell walls and struts. For closed-cell foams, the polymer is concentrated in thin cell walls, so:

$$k_{solid} = (1 - \phi_{gas}) \cdot k_{polymer} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3} - f_s\right)$$

**Gas-Phase Conduction:** The trapped gas inside each cell conducts heat across its diameter:

$$k_{gas} = \phi_{gas} \cdot k_{trapped\_gas}$$

**Radiative Heat Transfer:** Inside each cell, radiation occurs between the two opposing walls separated by the cell diameter $L$:

$$k_{rad} = \frac{4 \sigma T^3 L}{3}$$

**Convective Heat Transfer:** For cell sizes $L < 4$ mm, natural convection is suppressed by the high viscosity of the trapped gas. Therefore $k_{convection} \approx 0$.

**Final Expression:**

$$k_{total} = (1 - \phi_{gas}) \cdot k_{polymer} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3} - f_s\right) + \phi_{gas} \cdot k_{gas} + \frac{4 \sigma T^3 L}{3}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Describe the **classification of thermoplastic foams** based on cell structure and density. Give two construction applications for each class. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An **EPS foam board** used for wall insulation has the following data:
- Foam density: $\rho_{foam} = 25$ kg/m³
- Solid polystyrene density: $\rho_{PS} = 1050$ kg/m³
- $k_{PS} = 0.14$ W/(m·K), $k_{air} = 0.025$ W/(m·K)
- Cell diameter: $L = 0.5$ mm, temperature: $T = 300$ K
- Strut fraction: $f_s = 0.8$

**Calculate:**
(i) The gas volume fraction $\phi_{gas}$.
(ii) The total thermal conductivity of the foam.
(iii) The R-value of a 50 mm thick board. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of Thermoplastic Foams:**

**By Cell Structure:**
- **Closed-Cell Foam:** Applications — (1) External wall insulation boards, (2) Roof insulation.
- **Open-Cell Foam:** Applications — (1) Acoustic panels, (2) Vibration dampers.

**By Density:**
- **Low-Density (<40 kg/m³):** Applications — (1) Cavity wall insulation, (2) Void fillers in construction joints.
- **Medium-Density (40–100 kg/m³):** Applications — (1) Underfloor heating insulation, (2) Sandwich panel cores.
- **High-Density (>100 kg/m³):** Applications — (1) Load-bearing structural panels, (2) Permanent formwork.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Gas Volume Fraction:**

$$\phi_{gas} = 1 - \frac{\rho_{foam}}{\rho_{PS}} = 1 - \frac{25}{1050} = 1 - 0.0238 = 0.9762$$

**(ii) Total Thermal Conductivity:**

**Solid-phase contribution:**

$$k_{solid} = (1 - 0.9762) \times 0.14 \times \left(\frac{2}{3} - 0.8\right) = 0.0238 \times 0.14 \times (-0.1333)$$

$$k_{solid} \approx 0.000444 \text{ W/(m·K)} \approx 0 \text{ (negligible)}$$

**Gas-phase contribution:**

$$k_{gas} = 0.9762 \times 0.025 = 0.02441 \text{ W/(m·K)}$$

**Radiation contribution:**

$$k_{rad} = \frac{4 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (300)^3 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{3}$$

$$k_{rad} = \frac{4 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 2.7 \times 10^7 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{3}$$

$$k_{rad} = \frac{3.062 \times 10^{-3}}{3} = 1.021 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W/(m·K)}$$

**Convection contribution:** Negligible for $L = 0.5$ mm < 4 mm.

**Total thermal conductivity:**

$$k_{total} = 0 + 0.02441 + 0.00102 = 0.02543 \text{ W/(m·K)}$$

$$\boxed{k_{total} \approx 0.0254 \text{ W/(m·K)}}$$

**(iii) R-value for 50 mm board:**

$$R = \frac{t}{k} = \frac{0.050}{0.0254} = 1.97 \text{ (m}^2\cdot\text{K)/W}$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 1.97 \text{ (m}^2\cdot\text{K)/W}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign convention** in the Rayleigh-Plesset equation — the $2\sigma/r$ term resists expansion; students often write it as $+2\sigma/r$ in the driving force. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Conflating "negligible" with "zero"** — for $L < 4$ mm convection is small but not strictly zero. Students who write $k_{convection} = 0$ without justification lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Wrong units in R-value** — KTU expects $(m^2 \cdot K)/W$, not $K/W$ or $W/m^2$. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 4. **Using the wrong Stefan-Boltzmann constant** — students often forget that in the radiation formula, $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴) — a unit error gives a numerical error. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 5. **Skipping the strut fraction $f_s$** in the solid-phase conduction term — KTU examiners specifically check this. **Loss: 1 mark.**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Thermoplastic Foams**

- **Definition:** Cellular polymer produced by expanding a thermoplastic resin with a blowing agent; **re-meltable** (key contrast with thermoset foams).
- **Major Resins:** PS (EPS/XPS), PE, PP, PVC — each with a distinct $T_g$ and cell structure.
- **Four Stages of Foam Formation:** Melting → Impregnation → Nucleation → Stabilization.
- **Rayleigh-Plesset Equation:** Describes bubble growth rate; surface tension and viscosity resist expansion.
- **Henry's Law:** $C = H \cdot P$ — governs the maximum dissolved gas concentration.
- **Relative Density:** $\rho_{rel} = \rho_{foam} / \rho_{polymer}$ — central design parameter.
- **Thermal Conductivity Formula:** $k_{total} = k_{solid} + k_{gas} + k_{rad} + k_{conv}$ (4 contributions).
- **Radiative Term:** $k_{rad} = \frac{4\sigma T^3 L}{3}$ — depends on cube of temperature and cell size.
- **R-value:** $R = t/k$ — used for insulation thickness design.
- **Closed-Cell vs Open-Cell:** Closed = waterproof + insulation; Open = acoustic + vapour-permeable.
- **EPS vs XPS:** EPS = pre-expanded beads; XPS = extruded continuous process, lower water absorption.
- **Cell Size Effect:** Smaller cells → lower radiation contribution → better insulation.
- **Density Range:** Construction foams typically 15–100 kg/m³.
- **Common Blowing Agents:** Pentane (EPS), CO₂ (XPS), azodicarbonamide (chemical).
- **Convection Threshold:** Negligible for cell diameter $L < 4$ mm — remember this for derivations.
- **Stefan-Boltzmann Constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴) — must be cited with correct units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermosetting Foams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT758 - POLYMERS IN CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thermosetting Foams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **thermosetting foam** is a cellular polymeric material produced by the controlled expansion and simultaneous irreversible crosslinking (curing) of a thermosetting resin precursor system. The three-dimensional covalent network formed during cure cannot be remelted or reprocessed upon reheating, distinguishing it fundamentally from thermoplastic foams. Common engineering thermosetting foams used in construction include **Polyurethane (PU) foam**, **Phenolic (PF) foam**, **Urea-Formaldehyde (UF) foam**, **Melamine foam**, and **Epoxy foam**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The crosslinking reaction and the blowing (gas-generation) reaction occur **simultaneously**. This co-reaction is the defining engineering feature — it locks the cellular structure permanently once the resin gels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Density Range (KTU Reference Value):** **15 – 200 kg/m³** for construction-grade foams. Rigid PU foams typically lie in the **30 – 50 kg/m³** band, which is the sweet spot for insulation applications.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hard Sponge Cake" Model

Imagine baking a **sponge cake**:

1. A liquid batter (polyol + isocyanate) is poured into a mould.
2. A chemical leavening agent releases gas bubbles (the *blowing agent*) — the cake **rises**.
3. At the same time, the batter **bakes (crosslinks)** into a rigid, permanent solid.

If you reheat the baked cake, it does **not** melt back into a batter — it burns. That irreversible setting is the hallmark of a **thermosetting foam**. A thermoplastic foam, in contrast, is like a "jelly that you can re-melt" — same cellular structure, but reversible. The chemistry locks the cell walls into a permanent 3D covalent mesh.

## 1.3 Why Construction Engineers Care

| Property | Typical Value | Construction Benefit |
|---|---|---|
| **Thermal Conductivity (k)** | **0.020 – 0.040 W/m·K** | Superior insulation |
| **Density (ρ)** | **15 – 200 kg/m³** | Lightweight — reduces dead load |
| **Closed-cell content** | **> 90 %** (rigid PU) | Low water absorption |
| **Compressive strength** | **100 – 1000 kPa** | Walkable roof insulation |
| **Fire performance (Phenolic)** | **LOI ≈ 35 – 40 %** | Self-extinguishing |

> [!TIP]
> **Cell Memory Trick:** *Closed cells = Closed pores = Closed to water.* Open-cell foams breathe (good for acoustics); closed-cell foams block water and vapour (good for roofs and walls).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Dual-Reaction Engineering Principle

A thermosetting foam is the result of **two competing/parallel reactions** that must be carefully balanced by the formulator:

**Reaction A — Gelation (Polymerisation / Crosslinking)**
- The polyol resin reacts with the isocyanate (or hardener) to build the polymer network.
- Releases heat (exothermic). If too fast → foam cracks. If too slow → foam collapses.
- Once the network reaches the *gel point*, cell walls become load-bearing.

**Reaction B — Blowing (Gas Generation)**
- A blowing agent decomposes or reacts to liberate gas (CO₂, pentane vapour, HFC).
- Bubbles nucleate, grow, and stabilise within the still-fluid resin.
- If blowing finishes **after** gelation → cells rupture (collapse). If it finishes **too early** → gas escapes before lock-in.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Cream Time / Rise Time / Tack-Free Time" Triad** is monitored in practice:
> - **Cream time** = bubble nucleation begins.
> - **Rise time** = foam reaches maximum volume.
> - **Tack-free time** = surface is no longer sticky — gelation is complete.
> These three timers MUST follow the sequence: cream < rise < tack-free. KTU questions frequently test this ordering.

## 2.2 Classification of Blowing Agents

| Type | Mechanism | Examples | Environmental Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Chemical** | Reaction with isocyanate liberates CO₂ | Water (H₂O + R-NCO → R-NH₂ + CO₂) | Zero ODP / GWP, most common today |
| **Physical (Liquid)** | Latent-heat vaporisation | n-Pentane, cyclo-pentane | Low GWP, flammable |
| **Physical (Gas)** | Pressure-induced expansion | HFC-245fa, HFC-365mfc | Phase-out under Kigali Amendment |
| **Historical (Banned)** | Volatile halocarbons | CFC-11, HCFC-141b | Banned — Montreal Protocol |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation convention:** All units are SI. Vertical bars use `\vert` to avoid markdown table breakage.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho_f = \dfrac{m_{foam}}{V_{foam}}$ | Foam bulk density | Quality control of foamed panels |
| 2 | $\phi_{gas} = \dfrac{V_{gas}}{V_{foam}} \approx 1 - \dfrac{\rho_f}{\rho_{solid}}$ | Gas volume fraction (porosity) | Predicts insulation performance |
| 3 | $R = \dfrac{t}{k}$ | Thermal resistance (R-value) | Wall / roof U-value calculation |
| 4 | $U = \dfrac{1}{R_{total}} = \dfrac{1}{\sum \frac{t_i}{k_i}}$ | Overall heat-transfer coefficient | Building Energy Code compliance |
| 5 | $k_{foam} = k_{solid} \cdot (1-\phi_{gas}) + k_{air} \cdot \phi_{gas} + k_{radiation}$ | Parallel model for thermal conductivity | Estimating k from porosity |
| 6 | $\dot{Q} = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{t}$ | Steady-state Fourier heat flow | Heat-loss through foam slab |
| 7 | $S = \dfrac{\mu_{gas}}{\mu_{air}} \approx \dfrac{k_{gas}}{k_{air}}$ | Relative closed-cell gas permeability | Ageing / thermal drift analysis |
| 8 | $f_s = \dfrac{A_{strut}}{A_{cell}} \times 100$ | Solid fraction (1 − φ_gas) | Mechanical strength correlation |
| 9 | $\sigma_c = \sigma_0 \cdot (f_s)^n$ | Compressive strength vs solid fraction | n ≈ 1.5 – 2 for PU foam |
| 10 | $OI = \dfrac{\%O_2}{100 - \%O_2 - \%N_2} \times \text{approx. factor}$ | Limiting Oxygen Index proxy | Fire performance rating |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Building envelope (walls/roofs):** PU spray foam achieves **U-values ≤ 0.20 W/m²·K** with only **80–100 mm** thickness — meeting ECBC / SuperECBC targets.
- **Pipe insulation:** Factory-moulded PU half-shells reduce chilled-water pipe heat-gain by **> 90 %**.
- **Cavity wall fill:** UF foam is injected as a liquid that foams in-situ, sealing every void.
- **Acoustic control:** Open-cell melamine foam achieves **NRC ≈ 0.95** at 50 mm thickness.
- **Fire-stopping:** Intumescent phenolic foam char protects structural steel during a fire.

> [!TIP]
> **Kerala Climate Note:** The hot-humid climate of Kerala makes **closed-cell PU foam (PIR variant)** the most appropriate choice, as it resists both water-vapour ingress and fungal growth. Open-cell UF foam can absorb moisture and is now discouraged for coastal districts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Foam Density from Formulation Mass Balance

**Given:** A polyurethane foam is made by mixing:
- Polyol blend: $m_p = 100$ g
- Isocyanate (MDI): $m_i = 110$ g
- Water (chemical blowing agent): $m_w = 2$ g
- Surfactant + catalyst: $m_s = 1$ g

**Total liquid mass before reaction:**
$$m_{liquid} = m_p + m_i + m_w + m_s = 100 + 110 + 2 + 1 = 213 \text{ g}$$

**Step 1 — Mass of CO₂ produced by water–isocyanate reaction.**

The stoichiometry is:
$$\text{R-NCO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{R-NH}_2 + \text{CO}_2 \uparrow$$

Molecular masses: H₂O = 18 g/mol, CO₂ = 44 g/mol. So 18 g of water releases 44 g of CO₂.

$$m_{CO_2} = m_w \times \frac{44}{18} = 2 \times \frac{44}{18} = 4.889 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of solid polymer formed.**

By conservation of mass (ignoring minor side products):
$$m_{solid} = m_{liquid} - m_{CO_2} = 213 - 4.889 = 208.111 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3 — Volume of the moulded foam (assume the foam just fills a 1 L mould).**
$$V_{foam} = 1 \text{ L} = 0.001 \text{ m}^3 = 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Foam bulk density.**
$$\rho_f = \frac{m_{solid}}{V_{foam}} = \frac{208.111 \text{ g}}{1000 \text{ cm}^3} = 0.2081 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 208.1 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> This density is **higher than typical construction PU foam (30–50 kg/m³)** because the water content is too low. To lower density, the formulator increases water or adds a physical blowing agent.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Thermal Conductivity of a Foam Slab in Steady State

**Setup:** A PU foam slab of thickness **t = 50 mm = 0.05 m** is sandwiched between two faces at temperatures $T_1 = 40^\circ$C and $T_2 = 25^\circ$C. Foam thermal conductivity $k = 0.024$ W/m·K. Face area $A = 1$ m².

**Step 1 — Apply Fourier's Law in 1-D steady state.**
$$\dot{Q} = \frac{k \cdot A \cdot (T_1 - T_2)}{t}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**
$$\dot{Q} = \frac{0.024 \times 1 \times (40 - 25)}{0.05}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify numerator and denominator.**
$$\dot{Q} = \frac{0.024 \times 15}{0.05} = \frac{0.36}{0.05} = 7.2 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4 — Compute thermal resistance.**
$$R = \frac{t}{k} = \frac{0.05}{0.024} = 2.083 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

**Step 5 — Express in U-value form (for KTU building-physics question).**
$$U_{foam} = \frac{1}{R} = \frac{1}{2.083} = 0.480 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

**Step 6 — Compare to an uninsulated concrete slab.** Plain 150 mm concrete (k ≈ 1.4 W/m·K):
$$R_{concrete} = \frac{0.150}{1.4} = 0.107 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W} \quad \Rightarrow \quad U = 9.33 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the U-value reduction factor.**
$$\eta_{red} = \frac{U_{concrete}}{U_{foam,total}} = \frac{9.33}{0.480} \approx 19.4$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The foam slab reduces the heat-transfer rate by a factor of **≈ 19×**. This is the kind of headline result KTU questions expect in a 14-mark Part B answer.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Thermosetting Foam

| Application / Requirement | Best Foam Choice | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Roof insulation (high humidity) | **Rigid PU / PIR** | Closed-cell, low k, water-resistant |
| Cavity-wall retrofit | **UF foam** | Flows into voids, expands, sets in-situ |
| Fire-rated partition | **Phenolic foam** | High LOI, low smoke emission |
| Acoustic studio lining | **Melamine foam** | Open-cell, NRC ≈ 0.95 |
| Structural sandwich core | **Epoxy foam** | High compressive strength, thermal stability |
| Pipe lagging | **Rigid PU** | Factory-moulded half-shells, low k |
| Cold-storage warehouse | **PIR foam + vapour barrier** | Very low k + foil facer |

> [!TIP]
> **Decision Heuristic for KTU Problems:**
> 1. Is fire performance the priority? → **Phenolic / PIR**.
> 2. Is moisture the priority? → **Closed-cell PU / PIR / Epoxy**.
> 3. Is sound absorption the priority? → **Open-cell Melamine / UF**.
> 4. Is in-situ filling the priority? → **UF / Spray PU**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Cell-Structure Taxonomy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Thermosetting Foam] --> B[Cell Geometry]
    A --> C[Cell Opening]
    A --> D[Polymer Matrix]

    B --> B1[Spherical]
    B --> B2[Polyhedral]
    B --> B3[Elongated]

    C --> C1[Closed Cell]
    C --> C2[Open Cell]

    D --> D1[Polyurethane PU]
    D --> D2[Polyisocyanurate PIR]
    D --> D3[Phenolic PF]
    D --> D4[Urea Formaldehyde UF]
    D --> D5[Melamine MF]
    D --> D6[Epoxy EP]

    C1 --> S1[Rigid PU, PIR, PF, Epoxy]
    C2 --> S2[Melamine, UF, Flexible PU]

    style A fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#6a1b9a,color:#ffffff
    style S1 fill:#ffb300,color:#000000
    style S2 fill:#ffb300,color:#000000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Manufacturing Flow (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Polyol Resin] --> M1[Mixing Head]
    P2[Isocyanate MDI] --> M1
    P3[Blowing Agent] --> M1
    P4[Surfactant and Catalyst] --> M1
    M1 --> R1[Reactor Mould or Spray Gun]
    R1 --> R2[Cream Time 5 to 10 s]
    R2 --> R3[Rise Time 60 to 120 s]
    R3 --> R4[Tack Free Time 120 to 240 s]
    R4 --> R5[Demould and Cure 24 h]
    R5 --> P5[Finished Foam Panel]
    P5 --> Q1[Density Check]
    P5 --> Q2[Thermal Conductivity Test]
    P5 --> Q3[Compressive Strength Test]
    P5 --> Q4[Fire LOI Test]
    style M1 fill:#0277bd,color:#ffffff
    style R1 fill:#d84315,color:#ffffff
    style P5 fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style Q1 fill:#6a1b9a,color:#ffffff
    style Q2 fill:#6a1b9a,color:#ffffff
    style Q3 fill:#6a1b9a,color:#ffffff
    style Q4 fill:#6a1b9a,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Heat-Flow Path Through a Foam Cell

```mermaid
graph LR
    H1[Hot Face] --> H2[Polymer Strut]
    H2 --> H3[Cell Gas Bubble]
    H3 --> H4[Polymer Strut 2]
    H4 --> H5[Cold Face]
    H2 -. Conduction .-> H3
    H3 -. Convection tiny and Radiation .-> H4
    style H1 fill:#ff6f00,color:#ffffff
    style H5 fill:#1565c0,color:#ffffff
    style H3 fill:#fdd835,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Heat must travel **through the solid polymer strut**, then across the **trapped gas bubble** (where it is partially blocked by the low gas conductivity and minimal convection), then through the next strut. This series of low-k gas + thin solid struts is what makes foam an outstanding insulator.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a thermosetting foam. Why is it preferred over a thermoplastic foam for high-temperature insulation applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A thermosetting foam is a cellular polymer produced by simultaneous gas-blowing and irreversible crosslinking of a thermosetting resin (PU, PF, UF, melamine, or epoxy). (1 mark) The crosslinked 3D network cannot soften on reheating, giving a high service temperature (PU ≈ 110 °C, phenolic ≈ 150 °C, PIR ≈ 150 °C). (1 mark) Thermoplastic foams (e.g., EPS, XPS) soften at 80–100 °C, deform, and may release toxic styrene; thermosetting foams retain cell geometry and insulation value even under thermal stress, and phenolic/PIR variants offer inherent fire resistance. (1 mark)

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List three chemical blowing agents used in polyurethane foam manufacture. Write the reaction that shows CO₂ liberation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Three chemical blowing agents: (i) **water**, (ii) **formic acid**, (iii) **carbamates / amine-formaldehyde adducts**. (1½ marks) Reaction:
$$\text{R-NCO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{R-NH}_2 + \text{CO}_2 \uparrow$$
(1½ marks) The liberated CO₂ is trapped in the reacting mass, nucleating bubbles that grow into the final cellular structure.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **manufacturing process of rigid polyurethane foam** used as roof insulation. Discuss the role of polyol, isocyanate, blowing agent, surfactant, and catalyst. (7 marks)

**(b)** A factory produces PU foam panels of dimensions **1.2 m × 0.6 m × 0.05 m**. The formulation uses 100 g polyol, 105 g MDI, 1.5 g water, and 1 g additives (total liquid mass = 207.5 g). The CO₂ released expands the foam to fill the mould exactly. Calculate: (i) mass of CO₂ produced, (ii) mass of solid polymer, (iii) **foam bulk density**, and (iv) the **U-value of a roof assembly** if the panel is laid over a 150 mm concrete deck (k_concrete = 1.4 W/m·K). (7 marks)

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3, Alt Choice]**

**(a)** Compare the properties and construction applications of **Phenolic foam, Polyurethane (PU) foam, and Urea-Formaldehyde (UF) foam**. Tabulate density, thermal conductivity, fire rating, moisture behaviour, and typical uses. (7 marks)

**(b)** A residential building in Kerala uses a wall assembly (outside to inside): 12 mm cement plaster (k = 0.72 W/m·K), 230 mm brick (k = 0.81 W/m·K), **40 mm PU foam** (k = 0.024 W/m·K), and 6 mm gypsum (k = 0.21 W/m·K). Surface resistances: R_si = 0.13, R_se = 0.04 m²·K/W. Compute the **overall U-value** and comment on its compliance with ECBC 2017 residential wall limit (U ≤ 0.40 W/m²·K). (7 marks)

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### **Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Manufacturing Process (7 marks):**
1. **Raw-material storage:** Polyol blend and MDI are stored in temperature-controlled tanks (20–25 °C). (½ mark)
2. **Metering & mixing:** High-pressure impingement mixing head brings polyol + additives and MDI together at **100–150 bar**. (1 mark)
3. **Pouring / spraying:** The reactive mix is either (i) poured into a closed mould (slabstock / panel line) or (ii) sprayed on the substrate (roofs, walls). (1 mark)
4. **Creaming:** Within 5–10 s, dissolved gas / newly formed CO₂ nucleates bubbles — *cream time*. (½ mark)
5. **Rising:** Bubbles grow as more gas is liberated; the foam reaches its free-rise volume — *rise time* 60–120 s. (½ mark)
6. **Curing:** Polymer network reaches gel point; surface becomes tack-free. (½ mark)
7. **Demoulding & post-cure:** Panels are demoulded after 10–20 min and stored for 24 h to complete crosslinking. (½ mark)

**Role of each component:**
- **Polyol:** Provides –OH groups that react with isocyanate to form the urethane linkage. (½ mark)
- **Isocyanate (MDI):** Crosslinks polyol chains; also reacts with water to liberate CO₂. (½ mark)
- **Blowing agent:** Generates gas (chemical = water; physical = pentane/HFC) for cell formation. (½ mark)
- **Surfactant (silicone):** Lowers surface tension, stabilises cell walls, prevents collapse. (½ mark)
- **Catalyst (amine + tin):** Balances gelation and blowing reactions. (½ mark)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

(i) **Mass of CO₂:**
$$m_{CO_2} = 1.5 \times \frac{44}{18} = 3.667 \text{ g} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

(ii) **Mass of solid polymer:**
$$m_{solid} = 207.5 - 3.667 = 203.833 \text{ g} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

(iii) **Foam bulk density:** Volume $V = 1.2 \times 0.6 \times 0.05 = 0.036 \text{ m}^3$
$$\rho_f = \frac{203.833 \text{ g}}{36000 \text{ cm}^3} = 0.00566 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 5.66 \text{ kg/m}^3$$
(1 mark) — *Note: This is below the practical minimum; the foam would collapse. In practice, physical blowing agent is added.*

(iv) **U-value of roof assembly:**

| Layer | t (m) | k (W/m·K) | R = t/k (m²·K/W) |
|---|---|---|---|
| PU foam | 0.05 | 0.024 | 2.083 |
| Concrete | 0.15 | 1.4 | 0.107 |
| R_si | — | — | 0.13 |
| R_se | — | — | 0.04 |
| **Total R** | | | **2.360** |

$$U = \frac{1}{R_{total}} = \frac{1}{2.360} = 0.424 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

(1 mark for table, 1 mark for arithmetic.)

---

### **Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Property | Phenolic (PF) | Polyurethane (PU) | Urea-Formaldehyde (UF) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density (kg/m³) | 30 – 80 | 30 – 200 | 8 – 20 |
| Thermal conductivity (W/m·K) | 0.020 – 0.025 | 0.022 – 0.028 | 0.030 – 0.040 |
| Fire rating (LOI %) | 35 – 40 | 18 – 22 | 17 – 20 |
| Moisture behaviour | Low absorption (closed cell) | Low absorption (closed cell) | High (open cell, friable) |
| Typical use | Fire-rated panels, duct insulation | Roof / wall insulation, spray foam | Retro-fit cavity wall fill |
| Cost (relative) | High | Medium | Low |

(1 mark per correctly filled row × 5 rows + 2 marks for narrative summary)

**(b) U-value Calculation (7 marks):**

| Layer | t (m) | k (W/m·K) | R = t/k (m²·K/W) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Plaster | 0.012 | 0.72 | 0.0167 |
| Brick | 0.230 | 0.81 | 0.2840 |
| PU foam | 0.040 | 0.024 | 1.6667 |
| Gypsum | 0.006 | 0.21 | 0.0286 |
| R_si | — | — | 0.13 |
| R_se | — | — | 0.04 |
| **Total R** | | | **2.165** |

(1 mark for table)

$$U = \frac{1}{2.165} = 0.462 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Compliance Check:** The calculated **U = 0.462 W/m²·K > 0.40 W/m²·K** ECBC residential limit. (1 mark) The wall is therefore **non-compliant**; to meet ECBC the PU foam thickness must increase to approximately 50 mm, or a higher-performance insulation (PIR / phenolic, k ≈ 0.020) must be substituted. (2 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing closed-cell with open-cell properties.** Closed-cell foams resist water; open-cell foams absorb it. Mixing these up costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting surface resistances (R_si and R_se).** A wall U-value without R_si/R_se is mathematically incomplete — KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Wrong stoichiometry for water → CO₂.** Always write $18 \to 44$, not $1 \to 1$. A 1-mark deduction is common.
> 4. **Mis-ordering the timers.** Cream time < Rise time < Tack-free time. Reversing these loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting the catalyst in PU formulation.** Many answers list polyol, isocyanate, blowing agent, surfactant, and skip the **catalyst** — a guaranteed ½ mark loss.
> 6. **Writing "PU is fireproof"** — it is **combustible** by default; **PIR or Phenolic** is the fire-resistant variant.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

- A **thermosetting foam** = blown **+** irreversibly crosslinked cellular polymer. ✔
- Key resins in construction: **PU, PIR, PF, UF, Melamine, Epoxy**. ✔
- Blowing agents: **water (chemical)**, **pentane (physical)**, HFCs (legacy). ✔
- **Cream < Rise < Tack-free** — the three timing milestones. ✔
- Density range: **15 – 200 kg/m³**; sweet spot for insulation: **30 – 50 kg/m³**. ✔
- Thermal conductivity: **0.020 – 0.040 W/m·K** (best = closed-cell PU/PIR). ✔
- **Closed cell** = water barrier, vapour barrier, structural. **Open cell** = acoustic, breathable. ✔
- **Phenolic foam** = best fire performance (LOI 35–40). ✔
- **UF foam** = in-situ cavity-wall injection. ✔
- **Melamine foam** = acoustic, open-cell, NRC ≈ 0.95. ✔
- **Epoxy foam** = high-strength structural core. ✔
- Key formulas: $\rho_f = m/V$, $R = t/k$, $U = 1/\sum R_i$, $k_{foam}$ parallel model. ✔
- For KTU numericals: **always include R_si and R_se** in wall / roof U-value problems. ✔
- Stoichiometry for CO₂: **18 g H₂O → 44 g CO₂**. ✔
- For Kerala's hot-humid climate, **prefer closed-cell PU / PIR**; avoid open-cell UF in coastal districts. ✔

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In any 14-mark Part B question on thermosetting foams, the KTU-expected structure is:
> 1. *Define* the foam (1–2 marks)
> 2. *Explain* the dual-reaction principle (3 marks)
> 3. *List* components and their roles (3 marks)
> 4. *Compute* a property (density, U-value, or k) (4–5 marks)
> 5. *Comment* on construction suitability (1–2 marks)
> Following this 5-step structure is the single biggest determinant of full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ageing Studies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT758 - POLYMERS IN CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ageing Studies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ageing Studies** in polymer engineering refer to the systematic investigation of the **time-dependent degradation** of polymeric materials under the combined and synergistic influences of environmental stressors encountered during service life. For construction polymers, ageing encompasses the irreversible changes in **chemical structure, molecular weight distribution, morphology, mechanical properties, optical characteristics, and surface chemistry** caused by **photo-oxidation, thermal oxidation, hydrolysis, chemical attack, mechanical fatigue, and biological action** throughout the operational lifespan of a structure.

In the context of **Polymers in Construction (PEPOT758)**, ageing studies are critical because construction polymers such as **PVC pipes, HDPE geomembranes, EPDM roofing membranes, FRP (Fiber Reinforced Polymer) rebars, polymer-modified concrete, epoxy coatings, polyurethane sealants, and acrylic paints** are exposed to decades of outdoor weathering. The KTU 2024 syllabus frames ageing as a multi-factor, multi-scale phenomenon governed by **free-radical chain scission reactions** and **crosslinking phenomena** that ultimately dictate the **service life, durability index, and replacement cycle** of polymer-based construction systems.

The fundamental governing equation of polymer ageing is the **Arrhenius relationship**, which links the rate of degradation to absolute temperature:

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

Where:
- $k$ = rate constant of the degradation reaction
- $A$ = pre-exponential (frequency) factor
- $E_a$ = activation energy of the ageing process (kJ/mol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant (**8.314 J/(mol·K)**)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Ageing is NOT a single reaction — it is a **synergistic cascade** of chemical, physical, and biological processes. A polymer outdoor service life prediction requires **multivariate acceleration models** that combine UV dose, temperature, humidity, and mechanical stress factors.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Polymer Sun-Burn" Metaphor

Imagine a polymer exposed outdoors as analogous to **a fair-skinned person sitting on a beach for 20 years**:

| Human Sun-Burn | Polymer Ageing |
|---|---|
| UV photons penetrate skin | UV photons break C–C and C–H bonds in polymer backbone |
| Skin turns red, then peels | Polymer **chalk**s, **cracks**, becomes **brittle** |
| Long-term sun exposure causes wrinkles & cancer | Long-term UV causes **chain scission, crosslinking, discoloration** |
| Sunscreens (SPF) block UV | **UV stabilizers + HALS + antioxidants** protect polymer |
| Doctor checks moles yearly | Engineer performs **accelerated weathering tests** |

Just as we apply sunscreen to extend healthy skin life, polymer engineers incorporate **HALS (Hindered Amine Light Stabilizers), UV absorbers (benzophenones, benzotriazoles), and antioxidants (phenolics, phosphites)** to delay the inevitable "sun-burn" of construction polymers.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Ageing studies are essentially **forensic longevity science** — they allow engineers to predict how a polymer will perform in 20, 30, or even 50 years by accelerating degradation in **weeks or months** in the laboratory.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Polymer Ageing in Construction

### Pillar 1 — **Photo-Oxidation (UV Degradation)**
The dominant ageing mechanism for outdoor-exposed construction polymers. UV photons (wavelength **< 400 nm**, energy **> 300 kJ/mol**) carry enough energy to homolytically cleave covalent bonds.

**Mechanism (Simplified Free-Radical Chain):**
1. **Initiation:** Polymer (PH) absorbs UV → free radicals (P• + H•)
2. **Propagation:** P• + O₂ → POO• (peroxy radical)
3. **Chain branching:** POO• + PH → POOH + P• (hydroperoxide formation)
4. **Termination:** P• + P• → P–P (crosslinking) or disproportionation

### Pillar 2 — **Thermal Oxidation**
Occurs in polymers used in warm climates or near heat sources (e.g., bitumen roofing in tropical Kerala). Follows **Boltzmann statistics** for bond activation. Critical above the **glass transition temperature ($T_g$)** or **crystalline melting point ($T_m$)**.

### Pillar 3 — **Hydrolytic Ageing**
Dominant for **polyesters, polyamides, polyurethanes, polycarbonates** in humid/marine environments. Water molecules cleave ester, amide, and urethane linkages via **nucleophilic acyl substitution**.

$$\text{R–CO–O–R'} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{H}^+ \text{ or OH}^-} \text{R–COOH} + \text{HO–R'}$$

### Pillar 4 — **Chemical Ageing**
Exposure to acids (acid rain), alkalis (concrete pore water, pH 12–13), chlorides (marine/coastal), and solvents. Particularly critical for **PVC, polystyrene, and PMMA** in industrial/urban settings.

### Pillar 5 — **Mechanical & Physical Ageing**
- **Stress cracking:** Environmental stress cracking (ESC) from combined load + fluid
- **Creep rupture:** Long-term loading below yield
- **Fatigue:** Cyclic wind/thermal loading
- **Physical ageing:** Densification below $T_g$ (volumetric relaxation in amorphous polymers)

## 2.2 Key Ageing Indicators & Diagnostic Markers

| Indicator | What it Reveals | Measurement Technique |
|---|---|---|
| **Yellowing Index (YI)** | Photo-oxidation onset | ASTM E313 (spectrophotometry) |
| **Carbonyl Index (CI)** | Degree of oxidation | FTIR peak at 1710 cm⁻¹ |
| **Molecular Weight ($M_w$)** | Chain scission | GPC (Gel Permeation Chromatography) |
| **Tensile Strength Retention** | Mechanical integrity | ASTM D638 / ISO 527 |
| **Elongation at Break** | Embrittlement | ASTM D638 |
| **Gloss Retention** | Surface erosion | ASTM D523 (60° gloss) |
| **Glass Transition Temp ($T_g$)** | Crosslinking / plasticization | DSC / DMA |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Formula | Application / Engineering Use | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$ | Arrhenius rate of degradation | $k$ in s⁻¹, $E_a$ in J/mol |
| 2 | $\ln k = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Linearized Arrhenius (plot ln k vs 1/T) | Slope gives $-E_a/R$ |
| 3 | $\dfrac{k_2}{k_1} = \exp\left[\dfrac{E_a}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]$ | Acceleration factor between two temperatures | Dimensionless ratio |
| 4 | $t_{f} = \dfrac{C}{\sum_i a_i \cdot f_i}$ | Service life prediction (factor superposition) | $a_i$ = acceleration factor, $f_i$ = stress fraction |
| 5 | $D = I_0 \cdot t \cdot (1 - e^{-\alpha x})$ | UV dose absorbed in polymer layer | Beer-Lambert, $\alpha$ = absorption coeff. |
| 6 | $M_t = M_n^{-1} - M_{n,0}^{-1}$ | Chain scission quantification | Charlesby-Pinner relation |
| 7 | $\text{YI} = \dfrac{100(C_x X - C_z Z)}{Y}$ | Yellowing Index (ASTM E313) | X, Y, Z = tristimulus values |
| 8 | $\text{CI} = \dfrac{A_{1710}}{A_{1370}}$ | Carbonyl Index (FTIR internal standard) | Reference peak at 1370 cm⁻¹ |
| 9 | $\text{Q}_{10} = 2^{\Delta T / 10}$ | Rule of thumb: rate doubles every 10°C rise | Approximation only |
| 10 | $t_{50\%} = \dfrac{[P]_0}{2k}$ | Half-life of polymer property | For first-order decay |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** KTU frequently tests the **Arrhenius equation and the superposition principle**. Memorize forms (1), (2), and (3) above — they appear in nearly every Part B ageing question.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Ageing studies are the **backbone of durability design** in modern infrastructure:

- **PVC pipes for water supply** (ISI IS 4985, ISO 1452) — must withstand **50 years** of hydrostatic pressure at 20°C
- **HDPE geomembranes for landfills** — require accelerated oxidative induction time (OIT) ≥ 100 minutes per ASTM D3895
- **FRP rebars in concrete** — must survive alkaline pore water (pH ≥ 12.5) for 75+ years (ACI 440 design life)
- **EPDM roofing membranes** — 20–30 year warranty backed by QUV and Xenon-arc ageing data
- **Epoxy floor coatings** in chemical plants — chemical resistance tests per ASTM D1308
- **Polymer-modified mortar** in marine bridges — chloride penetration + UV exposure studies

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Arrhenius-Based Service Life Prediction

**Problem Setup:** A PVC pipe shows a hydrostatic pressure strength of $P_0$ at time zero. After accelerated ageing at three elevated temperatures, the failure times ($t_f$) were recorded. Derive the relationship that allows extrapolation to service temperature.

### Step 1: Start with Arrhenius Rate Law

$$\begin{aligned}
k &= A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT} \\
\text{Taking natural log:} \quad \ln k &= \ln A - \frac{E_a}{RT}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2: Assume Failure Criterion is a Fixed Degree of Degradation

For a critical property (e.g., 50% tensile retention), the **failure time $t_f$ is inversely proportional to the rate constant**:

$$t_f = \frac{C}{k} \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \frac{C}{t_f}$$

Where $C$ is a constant representing the critical damage threshold (e.g., carbonyl index value at failure).

### Step 3: Substitute into Arrhenius Equation

$$\begin{aligned}
\ln\left(\frac{C}{t_f}\right) &= \ln A - \frac{E_a}{RT} \\
\ln C - \ln t_f &= \ln A - \frac{E_a}{RT} \\
\ln t_f &= \ln C - \ln A + \frac{E_a}{RT}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Recognize the Form as Linear in 1/T

$$\ln t_f = \text{constant} + \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T}$$

This is a **straight line** when $\ln t_f$ is plotted against $1/T$. The slope equals $E_a/R$.

### Step 5: Calculate the Acceleration Factor Between Two Temperatures

For an accelerated test at $T_{acc}$ and service at $T_{service}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\ln t_{f, \text{service}} &= \ln C - \ln A + \frac{E_a}{R \cdot T_{\text{service}}} \\
\ln t_{f, \text{acc}} &= \ln C - \ln A + \frac{E_a}{R \cdot T_{\text{acc}}}
\end{aligned}$$

Subtracting the two:

$$\ln\left(\frac{t_{f, \text{service}}}{t_{f, \text{acc}}}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_{\text{service}}} - \frac{1}{T_{\text{acc}}}\right)$$

### Step 6: Final Working Formula for Acceleration Factor (AF)

$$\boxed{\;\text{AF} = \frac{t_{f, \text{service}}}{t_{f, \text{acc}}} = \exp\left[\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_{\text{service}}} - \frac{1}{T_{\text{acc}}}\right)\right]\;}$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Given: $E_a = 90$ kJ/mol for PVC oxidation, $T_{\text{service}} = 30°C = 303.15$ K, $T_{\text{acc}} = 80°C = 353.15$ K, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{AF} &= \exp\left[\frac{90{,}000}{8.314} \left(\frac{1}{303.15} - \frac{1}{353.15}\right)\right] \\
&= \exp\left[10825.1 \times (0.003299 - 0.002832)\right] \\
&= \exp\left[10825.1 \times 0.000467\right] \\
&= \exp[5.055] \\
&\approx 157.5
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** One week of accelerated ageing at 80°C ≈ **2.4 years of service life at 30°C**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Photo-Oxidation Rate and UV Dose Relationship

The rate of carbonyl formation (a primary photo-oxidation product) is proportional to the absorbed photon flux times quantum yield:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Rate of oxidation} &= \Phi \cdot I_{\text{abs}} \\
\text{where} \quad I_{\text{abs}} &= I_0 \left(1 - e^{-\alpha x}\right) \\
\text{For thin films } (\alpha x \ll 1): \quad I_{\text{abs}} &\approx I_0 \cdot \alpha x \\
\therefore \quad \frac{d[\text{CO}]}{dt} &= \Phi \cdot I_0 \cdot \alpha \cdot x
\end{aligned}$$

Integrating over exposure time $t$:

$$[\text{CO}](t) = \Phi \cdot I_0 \cdot \alpha \cdot x \cdot t$$

This shows **linear growth** of carbonyl concentration with time — the basis of the **FTIR carbonyl index** method for tracking ageing.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Stress Cracking under Combined Load and Environment

For Environmental Stress Cracking (ESC) in HDPE pipes, the time to failure follows:

$$\log t_f = A' - n \log \sigma - m \log C_{\text{agent}}$$

Where $\sigma$ = applied stress, $C_{\text{agent}}$ = active chemical concentration. KTU examiners often ask for the **derivation and interpretation of constants $n$ and $m$**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Polymer Ageing Mechanism Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Construction Polymer in Service] --> B{Environmental Exposure}
    
    B --> C[UV Radiation]
    B --> D[Heat / Thermal Cycling]
    B --> E[Water / Humidity]
    B --> F[Chemical Agents]
    B --> G[Mechanical Load]
    
    C --> C1[Photo Initiation]
    C1 --> C2[Free Radical Formation]
    
    D --> D1[Thermo Initiation]
    D1 --> D2[Peroxide Decomposition]
    
    E --> E1[Hydrolysis Reaction]
    E1 --> E2[Ester Bond Cleavage]
    
    F --> F1[Acid / Alkali Attack]
    F1 --> F2[Crosslink Breakdown]
    
    G --> G1[Stress Concentration]
    G1 --> G2[Crazing and Cracking]
    
    C2 --> H[Chain Scission]
    C2 --> I[Crosslinking]
    D2 --> H
    D2 --> I
    E2 --> J[Molecular Weight Drop]
    F2 --> J
    G2 --> K[Crack Propagation]
    
    H --> L[Property Degradation]
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    
    L --> M[Yellowing and Chalking]
    L --> N[Embrittlement]
    L --> O[Loss of Strength]
    L --> P[Permeability Increase]
    
    M --> Q[Service Failure]
    N --> Q
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Accelerated Weathering Test Setup (QUV / Xenon Arc)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LAB[Laboratory Accelerated Weathering Station]
        L1[UV Lamp Source] -->|emits| L2[UVA 340 nm or UVB 313 nm]
        L2 -->|irradiates| S[Polymer Test Specimen]
        S -->|heated| H[Black Panel Temperature Controller]
        S -->|sprayed| W[Water Spray / Condensation Unit]
        S -->|monitored| M[Property Measurement Module]
        M --> M1[Tensile Testing]
        M --> M2[FTIR Spectroscopy]
        M --> M3[Color and Gloss]
        M --> M4[Molecular Weight GPC]
    end
    
    O[Outdoor Service Exposure] -.compared with.-> M
    A[Arrhenius Extrapolation Model] -.predicts.-> SL[Service Life]
    M --> A
    A --> SL
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Stabilizer Action Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    UV[Incoming UV Photon] --> S1{First Line: UV Absorber}
    S1 -->|absorbed as heat| H1[Harmless Thermal Energy]
    S1 -->|transmitted| S2{Second Line: Quencher}
    S2 -->|energy transferred| H2[Deactivated Chromophore]
    S2 -->|not quenched| S3{Third Line: HALS}
    S3 -->|radical scavenging| H3[Stable Nitroxyl Radical]
    S3 -->|radical escapes| S4{Fourth Line: Antioxidant}
    S4 -->|hydroperoxide decomposition| H4[Stable Alcohol]
    S4 -->|unprotected| D[Polymer Damage Initiated]
    
    style S1 fill:#ffe0b2
    style S2 fill:#fff59d
    style S3 fill:#c8e6c9
    style S4 fill:#bbdefb
    style D fill:#ffcdd2
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define ageing in polymers. List any four factors that affect the ageing of construction polymers.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Ageing is the irreversible change in the chemical structure, physical properties, and mechanical performance of a polymer with time under the influence of environmental factors such as heat, light, oxygen, moisture, and mechanical stress.
- **Four factors (4 × 0.5 = 2 marks):**
  1. **UV radiation** (photo-oxidation)
  2. **Temperature** (thermal oxidation)
  3. **Humidity / water** (hydrolysis)
  4. **Chemical environment** (acids, alkalis, chlorides)
  5. *(Optional)* Mechanical stress / fatigue

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is the significance of accelerated weathering tests in polymer construction materials?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Time compression (1 mark):** Accelerated tests (QUV, Xenon arc) compress years of outdoor exposure into weeks/months, enabling timely product qualification.
2. **Quality control (1 mark):** Provides reproducible, standardized data for comparing polymer formulations and verifying compliance with codes (ASTM, ISO).
3. **Service-life prediction (1 mark):** Combined with Arrhenius extrapolation, the data enables prediction of 20–50 year service life from short-term test results.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **mechanism of photo-oxidative degradation** of construction polymers with suitable chemical equations. Discuss the role of **HALS (Hindered Amine Light Stabilizers)** in retarding this degradation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Arrhenius method of service life prediction** for construction polymers. An EPDM roofing membrane is tested at three elevated temperatures with the following failure times:
- At 50°C → $t_f = 8500$ hours
- At 70°C → $t_f = 1800$ hours
- At 90°C → $t_f = 420$ hours

Calculate the **activation energy** and predict the **service life at 30°C**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Mechanism (4 marks):**
Photo-oxidation proceeds via a **free-radical chain mechanism** consisting of four stages:

1. **Initiation:** Polymer absorbs UV photon → homolytic bond cleavage
$$\text{P–H} \xrightarrow{h\nu} \text{P}^{\bullet} + \text{H}^{\bullet}$$

2. **Propagation:** Alkyl radical reacts with atmospheric oxygen
$$\text{P}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{POO}^{\bullet}$$

3. **Chain branching:** Peroxy radical abstracts hydrogen
$$\text{POO}^{\bullet} + \text{P–H} \rightarrow \text{POOH} + \text{P}^{\bullet}$$
Hydroperoxides photolyze to give alkoxy and hydroxyl radicals:
$$\text{POOH} \xrightarrow{h\nu} \text{PO}^{\bullet} + {}^{\bullet}\text{OH}$$

4. **Termination:** Radical coupling or disproportionation
$$\text{P}^{\bullet} + \text{P}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{P–P}$$
$$\text{POO}^{\bullet} + \text{P}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{POO–P}$$

**Step 2 — HALS Mechanism (3 marks):**
HALS do **not absorb UV**. Instead, they function as **cyclic radical scavengers** through the **Denisov cycle**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{N–H (HALS)} + \text{POO}^{\bullet} &\rightarrow \text{N–O}^{\bullet} + \text{POOH} \\
\text{N–O}^{\bullet} + \text{P}^{\bullet} &\rightarrow \text{N–O–P} \\
\text{N–O–P} + \text{POO}^{\bullet} &\rightarrow \text{N–O}^{\bullet} + \text{POOP}
\end{aligned}$$

The **nitroxyl radical (N–O•) is regenerated**, making HALS a **catalytic stabilizer** — a single molecule can neutralize hundreds of radicals. This is why HALS concentrations as low as 0.1–0.5% provide years of protection.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Apply linearized Arrhenius (2 marks):**

$$\ln t_f = \text{const} + \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T}$$

| T (°C) | T (K) | 1/T (K⁻¹) | $t_f$ (hr) | $\ln t_f$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 50 | 323.15 | 3.0945 × 10⁻³ | 8500 | 9.048 |
| 70 | 343.15 | 2.9141 × 10⁻³ | 1800 | 7.496 |
| 90 | 363.15 | 2.7535 × 10⁻³ | 420 | 6.040 |

**Step 2 — Calculate slope using two extreme points (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{slope} &= \frac{\ln t_{f,90} - \ln t_{f,50}}{1/T_{90} - 1/T_{50}} \\
&= \frac{6.040 - 9.048}{(2.7535 - 3.0945) \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= \frac{-3.008}{-0.3410 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= 8821 \text{ K}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate activation energy (1 mark):**

$$E_a = \text{slope} \times R = 8821 \times 8.314 = 73{,}338 \text{ J/mol} \approx 73.3 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 4 — Predict service life at 30°C (1 mark):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\ln t_{f,30} &= \ln t_{f,50} + \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_{30}} - \frac{1}{T_{50}}\right) \\
&= 9.048 + 8821 \times \left(\frac{1}{303.15} - \frac{1}{323.15}\right) \\
&= 9.048 + 8821 \times (3.2987 - 3.0945) \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 9.048 + 8821 \times 0.2042 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 9.048 + 1.801 = 10.849
\end{aligned}$$

$$t_{f,30} = e^{10.849} \approx 51{,}170 \text{ hours} \approx 5.84 \text{ years}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** A common error is mixing up the sign convention in the slope calculation — remember that $1/T$ **decreases** as $T$ increases, but $\ln t_f$ **also decreases**, giving a **positive slope**. Also, students frequently forget to convert °C to **Kelvin** before substitution. Loss of 2 marks for unit errors is common.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Discuss the various **types of ageing** encountered in construction polymers. Explain in detail the **Environmental Stress Cracking (ESC)** phenomenon with reference to HDPE pipes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are the standard **accelerated weathering test methods** used for construction polymers? Compare **QUV and Xenon Arc** test chambers with respect to their spectral distribution, correlation with outdoor exposure, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Types of Ageing (4 marks):**

| Type | Description | Example Polymer |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical ageing** | Densification below $T_g$ | PVC, PMMA below $T_g$ |
| **Chemical ageing** | Reaction with environment | Polyester hydrolysis in concrete |
| **Photochemical ageing** | UV-driven radical oxidation | EPDM roofing, PVC window profiles |
| **Thermal ageing** | Heat-driven chain scission | PE pipes in hot climates |
| **Mechanical ageing** | Creep, fatigue, stress cracking | HDPE pressure pipes |
| **Biological ageing** | Microbial/biofouling attack | PU sealants in sewage works |

**Step 2 — Environmental Stress Cracking in HDPE (3 marks):**
ESC is the **premature brittle failure** of a polymer under the **simultaneous action** of tensile stress and a chemically active environment (often a surfactant like Igepal CO-630). 

Mechanism:
1. Stress concentrates at surface flaws
2. Active fluid **plasticizes** the amorphous regions, lowering $T_g$ locally
3. Microvoids coalesce into **crazes**
4. Crazes convert to **brittle cracks**
5. Crack propagates rapidly → sudden catastrophic failure

ESC is the **#1 cause of HDPE pipe failures** in service. The **Bell Telephone Test (ASTM D1693)** and **Notch Pipe Test (ISO 13479)** are standardized ESC tests.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

| Parameter | **QUV (ASTM G154)** | **Xenon Arc (ASTM G155)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Lamp type** | Fluorescent UVA-340 / UVB-313 | Xenon arc + filters |
| **Spectral match to sunlight** | Good for UV only; no visible match | Excellent full-spectrum match |
| **Acceleration level** | Very high (UV only) | Moderate (full spectrum) |
| **Correlation with outdoor** | Good for UV-driven damage | Best overall correlation |
| **Temperature control** | Black panel up to 70°C | Black panel up to 110°C |
| **Humidity control** | Condensation only | Spray + controlled RH |
| **Typical application** | Coatings, sealants, thin films | Automotive, roofing, structural polymers |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** For **construction polymers**, **Xenon arc** is preferred for full-service-life correlation, while **QUV** is used for rapid screening of UV-sensitive formulations.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently confuse ASTM standards — **G154 = QUV**, **G155 = Xenon Arc**, **G152 = Carbon Arc**, **G153 = Water/Immersion**. Wrong standard number = **2 marks lost** immediately. Also, do NOT claim QUV gives full-spectrum correlation — it lacks visible and infrared components.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Ageing = irreversible degradation** under environmental stressors — chemical, physical, biological.
- [ ] **Photo-oxidation** is the dominant outdoor ageing mechanism; it follows the **free-radical chain mechanism** (initiation → propagation → branching → termination).
- [ ] **Arrhenius equation** is the master equation for accelerated ageing: $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$.
- [ ] **Linearized Arrhenius:** Plot $\ln t_f$ vs $1/T$ → straight line with slope $E_a/R$.
- [ ] **HALS** are **catalytic** stabilizers — they regenerate via the Denisov cycle.
- [ ] **Carbonyl Index** (FTIR 1710 cm⁻¹) is the gold-standard metric for oxidation.
- [ ] **Yellowing Index** indicates early-stage photodegradation.
- [ ] **Environmental Stress Cracking (ESC)** is the leading cause of HDPE pipe failure.
- [ ] **QUV vs Xenon Arc:** QUV = UV-only (rapid, screening); Xenon = full-spectrum (realistic correlation).
- [ ] **Always convert °C to Kelvin** in Arrhenius calculations.
- [ ] Standard test references to memorize:
  - ASTM G154 (QUV), G155 (Xenon), D1693 (ESC), D638 (tensile), D3895 (OIT)
  - ISO 4892, ISO 13479 (pipe ESC)
- [ ] For **PVC pipes**: $E_a$ typically ranges **80–100 kJ/mol**.
- [ ] Rule of thumb: **Q₁₀ ≈ 2** (rate doubles every 10°C rise).
- [ ] Service life prediction requires combining **multiple stressors** (UV + heat + humidity) — not just temperature alone.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Injection Grouting
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT758 - POLYMERS IN CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Injection Grouting

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Injection Grouting** is a specialized ground-improvement and structural-rehabilitation technique in which a fluidized grouting material — typically a polymer-modified cementitious slurry, a chemical grout, or a reactive polymer resin — is injected under controlled pressure into cracks, voids, fissures, joints, or porous soil/rock masses. The injected medium subsequently sets, cures, or polymerizes **in-situ**, thereby restoring structural integrity, arresting water ingress, and/or improving the load-bearing characteristics of the host substrate.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (PEPOT758 – Polymers in Construction), injection grouting is treated as a **polymer-based rehabilitation methodology** where polymeric binders (epoxy, polyurethane, acrylates, methacrylates, vinyl esters) and polymer-modified cementitious grouts are the primary engineering materials of interest.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive (Module 4 Highlight):**
> The course specifically evaluates students on the **role of polymers as injectable media** — meaning, traditional OPC/cement grout alone is *insufficient* for a complete answer. The examiner expects emphasis on **polymer resins, polymer-modified cement grouts, and their rheology/curing behavior**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Medical Injection for Concrete"

Imagine a patient (the concrete structure or soil mass) who has developed internal fractures (cracks, voids) and is leaking vital fluid (water ingress). A doctor (the grouting engineer) injects a fast-setting medical-grade sealant (the polymer grout) through a needle (the injection nozzle/packer) directly into the affected zone. The sealant travels through the cracks, fills them, and **cures in place** to restore the patient's integrity.

- **Patient** = Concrete/Soil Mass
- **Fracture** = Crack/Void/Fissure
- **Needle** = Injection Packer / Port
- **Sealant** = Polymer Grout (Epoxy/PU/Acrylate)
- **Cure Time** = Polymerization / Gel Time

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metrics (Industry Standards):**
> - Typical injection pressure range: **0.2 MPa to 7.0 MPa** (depending on substrate and grout type)
> - Polymer grout viscosity (uncured): **1 cP to 500 cP** (vs. cement grout ~50–5000 cP)
> - Target gel/setting time: **30 seconds to several hours** (tunable via catalyst dosing)

## 1.3 Classification of Injection Grouting (Polymer Perspective)

| Category | Base Material | Typical Application | Polymer Role |
|----------|---------------|---------------------|--------------|
| **Cementitious Grouting** | OPC + Polymer Latex (SBR, acrylic) | Large voids, rock fissures | Polymer improves bond, flexibility, water-resistance |
| **Epoxy Resin Grouting** | Bisphenol-A epoxy + hardener | Structural crack repair (dry/damp) | Polymer is the **sole binder**; high strength |
| **Polyurethane (PU) Grouting** | MDI/polyol prepolymers | Active water-leak sealing | Polymer expands (foams) to block water |
| **Acrylate / Methacrylate** | Acrylic monomers + crosslinker | Hairline cracks, soil stabilization | Low-viscosity polymer penetrates micro-fissures |
| **Silicate-Modified Polymer** | Sodium silicate + acrylate | Soil permeation, curtain walls | Hybrid organic-inopolymer system |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanism — The Five Pillars of Injection Grouting

The success of any polymer injection grouting operation rests on five interconnected technical pillars. KTU examiners frequently test a student's understanding of *why* a particular polymer is selected, not just *what* it is.

### Pillar I: Substrate Diagnosis
- Identify crack width, depth, orientation, and moisture condition.
- **Dry crack** → Epoxy resin (high bond strength, structural).
- **Wet / active leak** → Polyurethane (hydrophobic, expansive foam).
- **Damp crack (no flow)** → Epoxy with water-tolerant hardener OR polyurethane-acrylate hybrid.
- **Hairline crack (< 0.1 mm)** → Low-viscosity methacrylate or acrylate (viscosity ~1–5 cP).

### Pillar II: Grout Selection (Polymer Chemistry Basis)
The polymer grout must satisfy the **Inverse Viscosity Rule**:
> *Lower the crack width, lower the required grout viscosity.*

$$
\nu_{\text{grout}} \leq k \cdot w_{\text{crack}}^{n}
$$
Where:
- $\nu_{\text{grout}}$ = kinematic viscosity of grout (cP)
- $w_{\text{crack}}$ = crack width (mm)
- $k, n$ = empirically determined constants (typically $n \approx 1.0$ to $1.5$)

### Pillar III: Injection Pressure Control
Pressure must be sufficient to **overcome hydrostatic head + capillary resistance** but **below the fracturing threshold** of the host concrete.

$$
P_{\text{injection}} = P_{\text{hydrostatic}} + P_{\text{capillary}} + P_{\text{friction}} + P_{\text{safety margin}}
$$

Typical engineered values:
- Low-pressure (gravity/curtain grouting): $P \leq 0.5$ MPa
- Medium-pressure (structural crack repair): $0.5 < P \leq 2.0$ MPa
- High-pressure (compensation grouting, soil stabilization): $P > 2.0$ MPa (up to 7.0 MPa)

### Pillar IV: Packer/Port Placement Geometry
- **Pattern**: Staggered or alternating sides of the crack, spaced at intervals of **1.5 × wall thickness** to **2.0 × wall thickness**.
- **Depth**: Packer must reach **2/3 of the member depth** for full penetration.

### Pillar V: Curing and Quality Verification
- Polymer grout must achieve **gel time** → **tack-free time** → **full-cure** sequentially.
- Verification: **core-drilling + compressive strength test** (typically at 7 and 28 days), **ultrasonic pulse velocity (UPV)**, or **resistivity mapping**.

## 2.2 Why Polymers? — The Engineering Justification

| Property | Cement Grout | Polymer-Modified Grout | Pure Polymer Resin |
|----------|--------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Tensile bond strength (MPa) | 1.0 – 2.5 | 3.0 – 6.0 | 8.0 – 25.0 (epoxy) |
| Water-tightness | Moderate | High | Excellent |
| Chemical resistance | Low | Moderate | High |
| Shrinkage on cure | ~0.5% | ~0.1% | < 0.05% |
| Penetration into hairline cracks | Poor | Moderate | Excellent |
| Flexibility | Brittle | Semi-flexible | Tunable (rigid to elastomeric) |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application Context |
|---|---------------------|------------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | Bingham fluid flow in fracture | $Q = \dfrac{w^{3} \cdot \Delta P}{12 \mu L}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | Grout penetration rate in planar crack |
| 2 | Hagen-Poiseuille (cylindrical pore) | $Q = \dfrac{\pi r^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | Grout flow in soil pores |
| 3 | Permeability reduction factor | $F_{R} = k_{\text{before}} / k_{\text{after}}$ | dimensionless | Effectiveness measure |
| 4 | Injection pressure (fracture limit) | $P_{\text{max}} = \sigma_{t} + \sigma_{0}$ | MPa | Max allowable without hydraulic fracture |
| 5 | Gel time of PU/acrylate | $t_{\text{gel}} = f([\text{cat.}], T)$ | seconds | Catalyst and temperature dependence |
| 6 | Foam expansion ratio (PU) | $E_{R} = V_{\text{foamed}} / V_{\text{liquid}}$ | ratio (typically 5–35×) | Leak-seal volume calculation |
| 7 | Polymer-cement ratio (modulus) | $P/C = m_{p} / m_{c}$ | dimensionless | Mix design parameter |
| 8 | Bond strength (pull-off test) | $\sigma_{b} = F / A$ | MPa | Quality verification |
| 9 | Viscosity-temperature (Arrhenius) | $\mu = \mu_{0} \exp(E_{a}/RT)$ | cP | Field temperature correction |
| 10 | Grout take (volume injected) | $V_{\text{take}} = V_{\text{voids}} \cdot n$ | liters | Site material planning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Reminder for Table Cells:** In all KTU answer scripts and notes, the absolute-value or "such that" symbol must be written as `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $P \leq \vert P_{\text{max}} \vert$) — never as a raw vertical pipe `|` — to avoid corrupting LaTeX/markdown rendering.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Civil Engineering

- **Dams & Hydraulic Structures**: Polyurethane injection to seal contraction joints and lift joints.
- **Tunnels & Underground Metro**: Acrylate gel curtains to arrest groundwater inflow.
- **Heritage Restoration**: Lime + acrylic emulsion injection to consolidate historic masonry without altering vapor permeability.
- **Bridge Piers & Pile Caps**: Epoxy injection for restoring load transfer in reinforced concrete.
- **Soil Stabilization (Jet Grouting)**: Polymer-cement hybrid columns for foundation underpinning.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Grout Flow Rate in a Planar Crack (Bingham/Newtonian Approximation)

The governing equation for grout flow in a single planar crack of width $w$ and length $L$, with a pressure differential $\Delta P$ across the crack, is derived from the **Reynolds lubrication approximation** (assuming laminar flow, no slip at walls).

### Step 1 — Set Up the Navier-Stokes Simplification
For an incompressible fluid in a thin slot, the Navier-Stokes equation reduces to:

$$
\frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial y^{2}} = \frac{1}{\mu} \cdot \frac{\partial P}{\partial x}
$$

Where $u$ is the velocity in the flow direction, $y$ is the transverse coordinate ($-w/2 \leq y \leq w/2$), and $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity.

### Step 2 — Integrate Twice with Respect to y

$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{1}{\mu} \cdot \frac{\partial P}{\partial x} \cdot y + C_{1}
$$

$$
u(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu} \cdot \frac{\partial P}{\partial x} \cdot y^{2} + C_{1} y + C_{2}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply No-Slip Boundary Conditions
At the walls, $u(\pm w/2) = 0$:

$$
0 = \frac{1}{2\mu} \cdot \frac{\partial P}{\partial x} \cdot \left(\frac{w}{2}\right)^{2} + C_{1} \cdot \frac{w}{2} + C_{2}
$$
$$
0 = \frac{1}{2\mu} \cdot \frac{\partial P}{\partial x} \cdot \left(\frac{w}{2}\right)^{2} - C_{1} \cdot \frac{w}{2} + C_{2}
$$

By symmetry, $C_{1} = 0$. Solving for $C_{2}$:

$$
C_{2} = -\frac{w^{2}}{8\mu} \cdot \frac{\partial P}{\partial x}
$$

### Step 4 — Velocity Profile

$$
u(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu} \cdot \frac{\partial P}{\partial x} \cdot \left(y^{2} - \frac{w^{2}}{4}\right)
$$

This is the classic **parabolic (Poiseuille) profile** in a planar slot.

### Step 5 — Volumetric Flow Rate per Unit Depth

$$
q = \int_{-w/2}^{+w/2} u(y) \, dy = \int_{-w/2}^{+w/2} \frac{1}{2\mu} \cdot \frac{\partial P}{\partial x} \cdot \left(y^{2} - \frac{w^{2}}{4}\right) dy
$$

$$
q = \frac{1}{2\mu} \cdot \frac{\partial P}{\partial x} \cdot \left[\frac{y^{3}}{3} - \frac{w^{2} y}{4}\right]_{-w/2}^{+w/2}
$$

$$
q = \frac{1}{2\mu} \cdot \frac{\partial P}{\partial x} \cdot \left[\left(\frac{w^{3}}{24} - \frac{w^{3}}{8}\right) - \left(-\frac{w^{3}}{24} + \frac{w^{3}}{8}\right)\right]
$$

$$
q = \frac{1}{2\mu} \cdot \frac{\partial P}{\partial x} \cdot \left[-\frac{w^{3}}{12} - \frac{w^{3}}{12}\right] = -\frac{w^{3}}{12\mu} \cdot \frac{\partial P}{\partial x}
$$

### Step 6 — Convert to Total Volumetric Flow Rate Q
For a crack of unit depth (or multiplying by crack depth $B$ for 3D flow):

$$
\boxed{Q = \frac{w^{3} \cdot B \cdot \Delta P}{12 \mu L}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** The flow rate scales with the **cube of the crack width**. This is the "cubic law" of fracture flow. A 2× wider crack admits **8× more grout flow** — explaining why narrow cracks are far harder to inject and why ultra-low-viscosity acrylates are essential for sub-millimeter fissures.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Polymer Grout Selection Decision Tree

| Substrate Condition | Crack Width (mm) | Moisture State | Recommended Polymer Grout | Injection Pressure (MPa) | Gel Time |
|---------------------|------------------|----------------|--------------------------|--------------------------|----------|
| Structural concrete, dry | 0.3 – 5.0 | Dry | **Epoxy resin (low-viscosity)** | 0.5 – 1.5 | 30 min – 4 hr |
| Structural concrete, damp | 0.3 – 5.0 | Damp (no flow) | **Epoxy (water-tolerant)** or **PU non-foam** | 0.5 – 2.0 | 15 min – 1 hr |
| Active water leak | > 0.5 | Wet (flowing) | **Hydrophobic PU foam** | 1.0 – 4.0 | 10 – 60 sec |
| Hairline cracks | < 0.3 | Any | **Acrylate / methacrylate** | 0.2 – 0.8 | 1 – 30 min |
| Soil / porous media | N/A (pore flow) | Saturated | **Acrylate gel** or **silicate-acrylate** | 0.3 – 2.0 | 10 sec – 10 min |
| Heritage masonry | 0.1 – 2.0 | Variable | **Lime + acrylic emulsion** | 0.1 – 0.5 | 1 – 24 hr |
| Post-tensioned ducts | Annular gap | Dry / filled | **Polymer cement grout** (SBR-modified) | 0.5 – 1.0 | 4 – 8 hr |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A 3.0 m long structural concrete beam has a crack of width $w = 0.5$ mm and depth $B = 200$ mm. An epoxy grout of viscosity $\mu = 200$ cP is injected at a pressure differential $\Delta P = 1.0$ MPa. Calculate the volumetric injection rate $Q$.

**Step 1 — Unit Conversions**

$$
w = 0.5 \, \text{mm} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}
$$
$$
\mu = 200 \, \text{cP} = 200 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s} = 0.2 \, \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}
$$
$$
\Delta P = 1.0 \, \text{MPa} = 10^{6} \, \text{Pa}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Cubic-Law Formula**

$$
Q = \frac{w^{3} \cdot B \cdot \Delta P}{12 \mu L}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-4})^{3} \cdot (0.2) \cdot (10^{6})}{12 \cdot (0.2) \cdot (3.0)}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{(1.25 \times 10^{-10}) \cdot (0.2) \cdot (10^{6})}{7.2}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-5}}{7.2} = 3.47 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Practical Units**

$$
Q = 3.47 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^{3}/\text{s} \times 10^{6} \, \text{cm}^{3}/\text{m}^{3} \times 60 \, \text{s}/\text{min}
$$
$$
\boxed{Q \approx 208 \, \text{cm}^{3}/\text{min} \approx 208 \, \text{mL}/\text{min}}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** This is a manageable field-injection rate. If the crack were narrower — say $w = 0.2$ mm — the flow would drop by a factor of $(0.5/0.2)^{3} = 15.6\times$, making injection impractical without switching to an ultra-low-viscosity acrylate ($\mu \sim 1$ cP).

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Polymer Injection Grouting — End-to-End Process Flow

flowchart TD

    A1b --> A2
    A1c --> A2
    A2 --> B1{Epoxy Resin}

    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Pressure Calibration 0.2 to 7.0 MPa]
    D1 --> D2[Sequential Injection from Lowest Port Upward]
    D2 --> D3[Monitor Flow Until Refusal Criteria Met]
    D3 --> E1{Quality Verification}

    E1 --> E1b[Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity UPV]

    E1c --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Documentation and Handover Report]
```

flowchart LR
    S1[Identify Crack Condition] --> S2{Active Water Leak}
    S2 -- Yes --> S3[Hydrophobic Polyurethane Foam]
    S2 -- No --> S4{Microcrack under 0.3 mm}
    S4 -- Yes --> S5[Low Viscosity Acrylate]
    S4 -- No --> S6{Structural Repair Required}
    S6 -- Yes --> S7[Low Viscosity Epoxy Resin]

```
